topics.title: Differential Equation Representation of Continuous Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Differential Equation Representation of Continuous Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś Module 1 ŌĆö Differential Equation Representation of Continuous Time Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> A **continuous-time (CT) system** can be described in the time domain by a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Differential Equation (LCCDE)** that relates the output $y(t)$ of the system to its input $x(t)$ and to higher-order derivatives of both signals.

For a causal, linear, time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system, the general **N-th order linear differential equation** takes the canonical form:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, \frac{d^{\,k} y(t)}{dt^{\,k}} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, \frac{d^{\,k} x(t)}{dt^{\,k}}
$$

where $a_k$ and $b_k$ are **real, constant coefficients**, $N$ is the **order** of the system (highest derivative of $y(t)$), and $M \leq N$ for a physically realizable (causal) system.

- $x(t)$ ŌåÆ **Input excitation signal**
- $y(t)$ ŌåÆ **Output response signal**
- $a_N \neq 0$ is the normalizing condition (usually $a_N = 1$).
- $b_M \neq 0$ (if $M=N$, the system has a direct feed-through / pure differentiator behavior).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a car suspension system ­¤ÜŚ**
> The shock absorber doesn't react instantly to a bump. Its motion depends on:
> - the **current height** of the road (input),
> - the **speed of the car's bouncing** (1st derivative),
> - the **rate at which bouncing changes** (2nd derivative),
> - the **car's own weight, spring stiffness, and damper oil** (the constant coefficients $a_k, b_k$).
>
> A differential equation is the *mathematical rule book* that tells the suspension exactly how all these ingredients combine to produce the final motion. Likewise, in signals and systems, the LCCDE is the master equation that fully characterises a CT-LTI system.

## 1.3 Why Differential Equations? (Engineering Motivation)

- Most physical systems (electrical, mechanical, thermal, hydraulic) obey **laws of physics** (KVL, KCL, Newton's 2nd law, Fourier's law) which are inherently **differential** in nature.
- A single LCCDE provides a **compact, time-domain, closed-form model** of the system.
- It enables direct extraction of:
  - **System order** (memory / number of energy-storage elements).
  - **Natural modes** (eigenvalues ŌåÆ poles).
  - **Stability** (location of roots of the characteristic polynomial).
  - **Frequency response** (substitute $j\omega$ for the derivative operator).

## 1.4 Classification of System Representation

| Representation | Domain | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Differential Equation | Time | Calculus (derivatives) |
| Transfer Function $H(s)$ | s-domain | Laplace Transform |
| Impulse Response $h(t)$ | Time | Convolution |
| State-Space $\dot{\mathbf{q}}=A\mathbf{q}+B\mathbf{x}$ | Time / Matrix | Linear Algebra |
| Frequency Response $H(j\omega)$ | Frequency | Fourier Transform |

All five are mathematically **equivalent** for an LTI system ŌĆö they are simply different "viewing windows" into the same underlying physical system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ö¼ Section 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the General LCCDE

$$
a_N \frac{d^N y}{dt^N} + a_{N-1} \frac{d^{N-1} y}{dt^{N-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 \, y(t) \;=\; b_M \frac{d^M x}{dt^M} + \cdots + b_0 \, x(t)
$$

### Step-by-step interpretation (KTU logical breakdown):

1. **Identify the system order** $N$ ŌåÆ the highest derivative on the output side.
2. **Left-Hand Side (LHS)** depends only on the output $y(t)$ and its derivatives ŌåÆ governs the **system's intrinsic dynamics** (zero-input behaviour).
3. **Right-Hand Side (RHS)** depends on the input $x(t)$ and its derivatives ŌåÆ represents the **driving function** (forcing/excitation).
4. **Coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$** are real constants ŌåÆ encodes the **physical parameters** ($R, L, C$, mass $m$, damper $c$, spring $k$, etc.).
5. The complete solution is:

$$
y(t) \;=\; y_{h}(t) \;+\; y_{p}(t)
$$

   - $y_h(t)$ ŌåÆ **Homogeneous / Complementary / Natural response** (zero-input, system-natural modes).
   - $y_p(t)$ ŌåÆ **Particular / Forced response** (driven by the specific input).

## 2.2 The Characteristic Equation (Most Important Tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU Board ŌĆö the characteristic equation is the single most tested tool in this module.**

Set the LHS of the LCCDE to zero and substitute the trial solution $y(t) = e^{st}$:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, s^{k} \;=\; 0
$$

The **$N$ roots** $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N$ of this polynomial are called the **natural frequencies** or **eigenvalues** of the system. They control:
- The **shape** of the transient response (decay, oscillation, growth).
- **Stability**: BIBO stable if and only if $\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$ for all $i$ (LHP).
- **Time constants** $\tau_i = -1/\text{Re}(s_i)$.

## 2.3 Standard Solution Form for Distinct Real Roots

If the $N$ roots $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N$ are **real and distinct**, then:

$$
y_h(t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \, e^{\,s_i t}, \qquad t \geq 0
$$

Constants $C_i$ are determined from **auxiliary / initial conditions** $y(0^-), y'(0^-), \ldots$.

### For Complex-Conjugate Pair $s = \alpha \pm j\beta$:

$$
y_h(t) \;=\; e^{\alpha t} \big[ A \cos(\beta t) + B \sin(\beta t) \big]
$$

### For Repeated Roots (multiplicity $r$):

$$
y_h(t) \;=\; \big( C_1 + C_2 t + C_3 t^2 + \cdots + C_r t^{r-1} \big) e^{\,s_1 t}
$$

## 2.4 ­¤ōŗ KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table for KTU ESE ŌĆö it covers ~70% of Module 1 problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General N-th order LCCDE | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y^{(k)}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x^{(k)}(t)$ | Compact time-domain model |
| 2 | Characteristic Equation | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k s^{k} = 0$ | Sets natural frequencies |
| 3 | Total Response | $y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)$ | Natural + Forced |
| 4 | Distinct real roots | $y_h(t) = \sum C_i e^{s_i t}$ | Decaying/growing exponentials |
| 5 | Complex roots $\alpha \pm j\beta$ | $e^{\alpha t}[A\cos\beta t + B\sin\beta t]$ | Damped oscillation |
| 6 | Repeated root (mult. $r$) | $t^{r-1} e^{st}$ extra terms | Resonance-type growth |
| 7 | Zero-input response | $x(t)=0$, use initial conditions | System memory |
| 8 | Zero-state response | All initial conditions $= 0$ | Response to input alone |
| 9 | BIBO stability | $\text{Re}(s_i) < 0 \;\; \forall i$ | All poles in LHP |
| 10 | Order Ōåö Energy storage | $N$ = # of independent L, C, mass, spring | Physical count |

## 2.5 Physical Realisation Examples (KTU Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> **Always try to draw a real circuit/mechanism on your answer sheet for extra marks.**

### Example 1: Series RLC Circuit
- KVL gives: $L \dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = x(t)$
- Differentiating and using $i = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$:

$$
LC \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + RC \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) \;=\; x(t)
$$

- Order = 2, Coefficients: $a_2 = LC, a_1 = RC, a_0 = 1$.

### Example 2: Mass-Spring-Damper
- Newton's law: $m \ddot{y} + c \dot{y} + ky = F(t)$

$$
m \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + c \frac{dy}{dt} + k \, y(t) \;=\; F(t)
$$

### Example 3: Two Cascaded RC Low-Pass Filters
- Stage 1: $RC\,\dot{y}_1 + y_1 = x(t)$
- Stage 2: $RC\,\dot{y} + y = y_1(t)$
- Combined ŌåÆ 2nd order LCCDE in $x(t), y(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤¦« Section 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 ŌĆö From RLC Circuit to LCCDE (High-Yield)

**Given:** A series RLC circuit driven by input voltage $x(t)$, with output $y(t)$ taken across the **capacitor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This derivation is asked in nearly every KTU Signals & Systems ESE paper, often as a 7-mark sub-question.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Write KVL around the loop
Let current be $i(t)$ and capacitor charge be $q(t)$, with $i(t) = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$.

$$
x(t) \;=\; v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t) \;=\; R\, i(t) + L\, \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{q(t)}{C}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Express everything in terms of the chosen output
Since $y(t) = v_C(t) = \dfrac{q(t)}{C}$, we have $q(t) = C\, y(t)$ and $i(t) = C\, \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt}$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into KVL
$$
x(t) \;=\; R \cdot C \frac{dy}{dt} + L \cdot C \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + y(t)
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Reorder into standard LCCDE form
$$
LC \,\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + RC \,\frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) \;=\; x(t)
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Normalise by $LC$ (set leading coefficient = 1)
$$
\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L} \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC} \, y(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{LC} x(t)
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Identify the characteristic equation
Substitute $y(t) = e^{st}$:
$$
s^2 + \frac{R}{L}\, s + \frac{1}{LC} \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for natural frequencies
$$
s_{1,2} \;=\; -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}
$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Interpret three damping cases

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \;=\; \left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Over-damped** ($\Delta > 0$): two real negative roots ŌåÆ exponential decay.
- **Critically damped** ($\Delta = 0$): $s_1 = s_2 = -R/2L$ ŌåÆ repeated root.
- **Under-damped** ($\Delta < 0$): complex conjugate pair ŌåÆ decaying sinusoid.

This single derivation unifies **circuit theory, ODE theory, and stability** ŌĆö a classic KTU exam pattern.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 ŌĆö Solving a 2nd-Order LCCDE with Initial Conditions

**Problem:** Find the complete response $y(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ of:

$$
\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 5\, \frac{dy}{dt} + 6\, y(t) \;=\; x(t)
$$

with input $x(t) = 10\, e^{-t}\, u(t)$, initial conditions $y(0^-) = 2$, $y'(0^-) = -1$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Form the characteristic equation
$$
s^2 + 5s + 6 \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (s+2)(s+3) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_1 = -2, \; s_2 = -3
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Write the homogeneous (natural) response
$$
y_h(t) \;=\; C_1 e^{-2t} + C_2 e^{-3t}, \qquad t \geq 0
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Find the particular (forced) response
For input $x(t) = 10 e^{-t}$, try $y_p(t) = K e^{-t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 y_p}{dt^2} + 5 \frac{dy_p}{dt} + 6 y_p
&= K e^{-t} + 5(-K e^{-t}) + 6(K e^{-t}) \\
&= K(1 - 5 + 6)\, e^{-t} \\
&= 2K \, e^{-t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Set this equal to $10 e^{-t}$:
$$
2K \;=\; 10 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K \;=\; 5
$$

Hence $y_p(t) = 5 e^{-t}$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Combine to get total response
$$
y(t) \;=\; C_1 e^{-2t} + C_2 e^{-3t} + 5 e^{-t}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Apply initial conditions

**Condition 1:** $y(0) = 2$
$$
C_1 + C_2 + 5 \;=\; 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 + C_2 \;=\; -3
$$

**Condition 2:** $y'(t) = -2C_1 e^{-2t} - 3C_2 e^{-3t} - 5 e^{-t}$, so $y'(0) = -1$:
$$
-2C_1 - 3C_2 - 5 \;=\; -1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -2C_1 - 3C_2 \;=\; 4
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Solve the linear system
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 + C_2 &= -3 \\
-2C_1 - 3C_2 &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply the first equation by 2: $2C_1 + 2C_2 = -6$. Add to the second:
$$
-2C_1 - 3C_2 + 2C_1 + 2C_2 \;=\; 4 - 6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -C_2 \;=\; -2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_2 = 2
$$

Then $C_1 = -3 - 2 = -5$.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Final answer
$$
\boxed{\,y(t) \;=\; -5\, e^{-2t} + 2\, e^{-3t} + 5\, e^{-t}, \qquad t \geq 0\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check at $t=0$:** $y(0) = -5 + 2 + 5 = 2$ Ō£ģ (matches initial condition).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Mapping the Mathematical Operations

| Operation | Mathematical Action | Resulting Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| Apply KVL / Newton's law | Write integro-differential equation | Physical law |
| Choose output variable | Express $i, v$ in terms of $y$ | Variable elimination |
| Differentiate (if needed) | Remove integrals | Pure differential form |
| Collect derivatives of $y$ on LHS | Reorder terms | **Standard LCCDE** |
| Substitute $y = e^{st}$ in LHS = 0 | Factor out $e^{st}$ | **Characteristic polynomial** |
| Solve polynomial = 0 | Find roots $s_i$ | **Natural modes** |
| Trial solution for input | Substitute into full LCCDE | **Particular integral** |
| Combine $y_h + y_p$ | Apply initial conditions | **Complete response** |
| Verify BIBO stability | Check sign of $\text{Re}(s_i)$ | Stability verdict |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Section 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Diagram of LCCDE System Realisation

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **signal flow** for a general 2nd-order LCCDE:

$$
a_2 \ddot{y}(t) + a_1 \dot{y}(t) + a_0 y(t) \;=\; b_0 x(t)
$$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x of t] --> B[Scalar b0]
    B --> C[Summing Junction]
    D[Feedback y double dot path] --> E[Gain a2 inverse] --> C
    F[Feedback y dot path] --> G[Gain a1 inverse] --> C
    H[Feedback y path] --> I[Gain a0 inverse] --> C
    C --> J[Integrator 1 for y dot]
    J --> K[Integrator 2 for y double dot]
    K --> L[Output y of t]
    L --> H
    L --> F
    L --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Each integrator represents one order of the system. The output $y(t)$ is fed back through gains $-a_1/a_2$ and $-a_0/a_2$ (after inversion at the summing junction) ŌĆö this is the standard **Direct Form II** realisation of a CT-LTI system.

## 4.2 Process Flow ŌĆö From Physical System to LCCDE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Physical System RLC Mass Spring etc] --> P2[Apply Governing Law KVL KCL Newton]
    P2 --> P3[Write Integro Differential Equation]
    P3 --> P4{Choose Output y of t}
    P4 --> P5[Eliminate Intermediate Variables]
    P5 --> P6[Differentiate to Remove Integrals]
    P6 --> P7[Reorder into Standard LCCDE Form]
    P7 --> P8[Identify Coefficients a sub k and b sub k]
    P8 --> P9[Form Characteristic Equation]
    P9 --> P10[Solve for Natural Frequencies s sub i]
    P10 --> P11[Classify Damping Behavior]
    P11 --> P12[Check Stability LHP Test]
```

## 4.3 Solution-Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Complete Response y of t] --> S1[Homogeneous y sub h]
    S --> S2[Particular y sub p]
    S1 --> S1a[Zero Input Response uses ICs]
    S1 --> S1b[System Natural Modes e power s sub i t]
    S2 --> S2a[Zero State Response ICs zero]
    S2 --> S2b[Driven by Input x of t shape]
    S1b --> S1b1[Real distinct roots]
    S1b --> S1b2[Complex conjugate pair]
    S1b --> S1b3[Repeated roots multiplicity r]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always label your diagram with **$a_k$**, **$b_k$**, and the **integrator delays** ŌĆö examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks just for a clean, well-labelled block diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤ōØ Section 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Define a Linear Constant-Coefficient Differential Equation (LCCDE). What is meant by the "order" of an LCCDE?**

**Model Answer:**

> An **LCCDE** is a linear differential equation in which the dependent variable $y(t)$ and its derivatives appear with **constant (time-invariant) real coefficients** $a_k, b_k$. It relates the input $x(t)$ to the output $y(t)$ in the form
> $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y^{(k)}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x^{(k)}(t).$$
> The **order** of the LCCDE is the **highest derivative order $N$** of the output $y(t)$ that appears in the equation. For example, an equation containing $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2}$ is a **2nd-order** LCCDE. The order is also equal to the number of **independent energy-storage elements** in the physical system.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**What is the characteristic equation of an LCCDE? How are its roots related to the natural response and stability of the system?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **characteristic equation** of an LCCDE is obtained by setting the left-hand side to zero and substituting the trial solution $y(t) = e^{st}$, yielding the polynomial
> $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k s^{k} = 0.$$
> Its $N$ roots $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N$ are the **natural frequencies** (eigenvalues) of the system. The **natural response** is constructed from these roots as
> $$y_h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i e^{s_i t}.$$
> The system is **BIBO stable** if and only if **all roots have strictly negative real parts** (i.e., lie in the open Left-Half Plane, LHP):
> $$\text{Re}(s_i) < 0 \quad \forall i.$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the linear constant-coefficient differential equation governing the **series RLC circuit** shown below, where the input is the source voltage $x(t) = v_s(t)$ and the output is the **capacitor voltage** $y(t) = v_C(t)$. Clearly state the order of the equation and the characteristic equation. *(Equivalent physical setup: $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $R = 3\,\Omega$, $C = 0.5\,\text{F}$.)*

> **(b)** [7 Marks] For the derived equation, classify the damping behaviour. Find the natural frequencies. Determine the **zero-input response** assuming $v_C(0^-) = 1\,\text{V}$ and $i_L(0^-) = 0.5\,\text{A}$.

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö KVL around the loop** (current $i(t)$ flows clockwise):
$$
v_s(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t) = R\, i(t) + L\, \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int i\,dt
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relate output to current.** Since $y(t) = v_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{C}\displaystyle\int i\,dt$, we have $i(t) = C\dfrac{dy}{dt}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute** $i = C\dot{y}$ and $\dfrac{di}{dt} = C\ddot{y}$:
$$
x(t) = RC\,\dot{y}(t) + LC\,\ddot{y}(t) + y(t)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Reorder into standard form:**
$$
LC\,\ddot{y}(t) + RC\,\dot{y}(t) + y(t) = x(t)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Plug numerical values** ($L=1, R=3, C=0.5$): $LC = 0.5$, $RC = 1.5$.
$$
0.5\,\ddot{y} + 1.5\,\dot{y} + y(t) = x(t) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ddot{y} + 3\,\dot{y} + 2\,y(t) = 2\,x(t)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö System order = 2.**

**Step 7 ŌĆö Characteristic equation:** $s^2 + 3s + 2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (s+1)(s+2) = 0$.

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Natural frequencies:** $s_1 = -1, \; s_2 = -2$ (real, distinct ŌåÆ **over-damped**).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Zero-input response** ($x(t) = 0$):
$$
y_{zi}(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply ICs.** Need $y(0^+)$ and $\dot{y}(0^+)$.

Given $v_C(0^-) = 1\,$V, so $y(0^+) = 1$.

Current through inductor cannot change instantaneously: $i(0^+) = i(0^-) = 0.5\,$A.
Since $i = C\dot{y}$, we have $\dot{y}(0^+) = i(0^+)/C = 0.5 / 0.5 = 1\,$V/s.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form linear system:**
$$
C_1 + C_2 = 1, \qquad -C_1 - 2C_2 = 1
$$

Adding: $-C_2 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = -2$, then $C_1 = 3$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final answer:**
$$
\boxed{\,y_{zi}(t) = 3\, e^{-t} - 2\, e^{-2t}, \qquad t \geq 0\,}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $y_{zi}(0) = 3 - 2 = 1$ Ō£ģ, $\dot{y}_{zi}(0) = -3 + 4 = 1$ Ō£ģ. Both natural frequencies are in LHP ŌåÆ system is **stable** and ZIR **decays to zero** as $t \to \infty$ Ō£ģ.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] For the LCCDE $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 4\,\dfrac{dy}{dt} + 4\,y(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} + 2\,x(t)$, find the **characteristic equation**, its roots, classify the damping, and write the form of the homogeneous solution $y_h(t)$.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] If the input is $x(t) = e^{-t}\, u(t)$ and the initial conditions are $y(0^-) = 1$, $\dot{y}(0^-) = 0$, determine the **complete response** $y(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Characteristic equation** (LHS = 0 with $y = e^{st}$):
$$
s^2 + 4s + 4 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (s+2)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Repeated root:** $s_1 = s_2 = -2$ (multiplicity $r = 2$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Damping classification:** Discriminant $= 16 - 16 = 0$ ŌåÆ **critically damped**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form of homogeneous solution** (using repeated-root rule):
$$
\boxed{\,y_h(t) = (C_1 + C_2\, t)\, e^{-2t}, \qquad t \geq 0\,}
$$

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Try particular solution** $y_p(t) = K e^{-t}$ for $t \geq 0$.

Compute derivatives:
$$
\dot{y}_p = -K e^{-t}, \qquad \ddot{y}_p = K e^{-t}
$$

Substitute into LHS:
$$
K e^{-t} + 4(-K e^{-t}) + 4 K e^{-t} = K e^{-t} (1 - 4 + 4) = K e^{-t}
$$

Compute RHS for $x(t) = e^{-t}$: $\dot{x} = -e^{-t}$, so $\dot{x} + 2x = -e^{-t} + 2e^{-t} = e^{-t}$.

Equate: $K e^{-t} = e^{-t} \;\Rightarrow\; K = 1$.

Hence $y_p(t) = e^{-t}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total response:**
$$
y(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t)\, e^{-2t} + e^{-t}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply ICs.**

Condition 1: $y(0) = 1$:
$$
C_1 + 1 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0
$$

Condition 2: $\dot{y}(t) = C_2 e^{-2t} - 2(C_1 + C_2 t)e^{-2t} - e^{-t}$.
At $t = 0$: $\dot{y}(0) = C_2 - 2C_1 - 1 = 0$. With $C_1 = 0$:
$$
C_2 - 1 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_2 = 1
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final answer:**
$$
\boxed{\,y(t) = t\, e^{-2t} + e^{-t}, \qquad t \geq 0\,}
$$

> **Sanity check:** $y(0) = 0 + 1 = 1$ Ō£ģ, $\dot{y}(0) = (1 - 0) - 1 = 0$ Ō£ģ.

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the repeated-root term $C_2 t\, e^{-2t}$** ŌĆö students often write only $C_1 e^{-2t}$ for a critically-damped system. This loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not differentiating $x(t)$ properly on the RHS** ŌĆö when $b_M \neq 0$ (here $b_1 = 1$), the input derivative $\dot{x}(t) = -e^{-t}$ must be substituted carefully. A sign error here is the **#1 cause of wrong answers**.
> 3. **Initial-condition sign errors** ŌĆö be careful that $i_L$ continuity gives $\dot{y}(0^+)$, not $-\dot{y}$.
> 4. **Forgetting physical units** in RLC problems ŌĆö write $\text{H, }\Omega, \text{F}$ for full marks.
> 5. **Not stating damping classification explicitly** ŌĆö examiners look for keywords *"over-damped / critically damped / under-damped"* and the discriminant value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ÜĆ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1**

- **LCCDE form:** $\sum a_k y^{(k)}(t) = \sum b_k x^{(k)}(t)$.
- **System order =** highest derivative of $y(t)$ on LHS.
- **Physical meaning of order =** number of independent energy-storage elements (L, C, mass, spring).
- **Characteristic equation:** Set LHS to 0, substitute $y = e^{st}$ ŌåÆ polynomial in $s$.
- **Roots of char. eqn =** natural frequencies = poles of the system.
- **Distinct real roots** ŌåÆ $y_h = \sum C_i e^{s_i t}$.
- **Complex-conjugate pair** $\alpha \pm j\beta$ ŌåÆ $y_h = e^{\alpha t}(A\cos\beta t + B\sin\beta t)$.
- **Repeated root (multiplicity $r$)** ŌåÆ multiply by $1, t, t^2, \ldots, t^{r-1}$.
- **Total response =** homogeneous + particular.
- **Zero-input response:** $x(t) = 0$, use initial conditions.
- **Zero-state response:** initial conditions = 0, use input.
- **BIBO stability:** all roots in **LHP** ($\text{Re}(s_i) < 0$).
- **Discriminant** $\Delta = a_1^2 - 4a_0 a_2$ controls: $> 0$ over-damped, $= 0$ critical, $< 0$ under-damped.
- **Memory items:** KVL for RLC, Newton's 2nd law for mass-spring-damper ŌĆö both lead to 2nd-order LCCDEs.
- **Always check ICs** by substituting $t = 0$ into the final answer.
- **Last-mile tip:** For any KTU LCCDE problem, *always* write the **damping classification** and **stability verdict** in a separate line ŌĆö examiners award full marks only when both are explicitly mentioned.

> **­¤Ä» You are now fully equipped to solve any KTU Module 1 question on Differential Equation Representation of Continuous-Time Systems.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Difference Equation** is a mathematical equation that relates the present output sample of a discrete-time system to its past output samples and present/past input samples. It is the discrete-time counterpart of the differential equation used for continuous-time (analog) systems.

For a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** describing a discrete LTI system, the canonical general form is:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]
$$

where:
- $y[n]$ is the **output sequence** of the system.
- $x[n]$ is the **input sequence** applied to the system.
- $a_k$ and $b_k$ are **constant real coefficients** that define the system's behavior.
- $N$ is the order of the system with respect to past outputs (defines the **system order** = $\max(N,M)$).
- $M$ is the highest shift in the input sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A discrete LTI system is completely characterized by its LCCDE together with a set of **auxiliary (initial rest) conditions**. By convention, $a_0 = 1$ (or is normalized) to make the representation unique.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Difference Equation** like a *recipe instruction for a digital calculator* that updates its memory every clock tick:

- Imagine you are maintaining a **savings account** that updates its balance every day.
- Your balance today ($y[n]$) depends on:
  1. The balance you had yesterday ($y[n-1]$) and the day before ($y[n-2]$) ŌĆö *past outputs*.
  2. The money you deposited today ($x[n]$) and yesterday ($x[n-1]$) ŌĆö *present and past inputs*.

A difference equation is exactly that ŌĆö a **set of rules** that says: *"To compute today's output, take a weighted combination of recent past outputs and recent past inputs."* Every digital filter, every DSP chip, and every audio equalizer in your phone operates on this very principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance of the Constants:**
> - **Coefficients $a_k$** (associated with $y[n-k]$) determine **feedback** / memory of the system.
> - **Coefficients $b_k$** (associated with $x[n-k]$) determine **feedforward** weighting.
> - If **all $a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$**, the system has **no feedback** (FIR filter).
> - If **any $a_k \ne 0$ for $k \ge 1$**, the system has **feedback** (IIR filter).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **8 kHz** (voice) to **48 kHz** (audio) |
| Sampling Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | $125 \,\mu s$ to $20.8 \,\mu s$ |
| System Order | $N$ | $1$ to $10$ (practical DSP) |
| Time Index Step | $n$ | Integer: $\mathbb{Z}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Difference Equation

A discrete LTI system described by:

$$
a_0 y[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + \cdots + a_N y[n-N] \;=\; b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + \cdots + b_M x[n-M]
$$

can be unpacked in the following structured logic steps:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Causality Check**
- A causal system depends only on present and past inputs.
- If $b_k = 0$ for all $k < 0$ and the equation is solved forward in $n$, the system is **causal**.
- Most physical real-time systems (audio processors, controllers) are causal.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linearity & Time-Invariance**
- Constant coefficients $a_k, b_k$ ŌåÆ **LTI system**.
- Superposition holds: if $x_1[n] \to y_1[n]$ and $x_2[n] \to y_2[n]$, then $\alpha x_1[n] + \beta x_2[n] \to \alpha y_1[n] + \beta y_2[n]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recursion & System Order**
- **Recursive (IIR) System:** At least one $a_k \neq 0$ for $k \ge 1$. Output depends on past outputs ŌåÆ infinite memory.
- **Non-recursive (FIR) System:** All $a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$. Output depends only on current and past inputs ŌåÆ finite memory.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Initial Conditions (Auxiliary Conditions)**
- A difference equation of order $N$ requires exactly **$N$ initial conditions** to produce a unique solution.
- For causal systems: $y[-1], y[-2], \dots, y[-N]$ are specified.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stability (informal preview)**
- A causal LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if all roots of the characteristic polynomial $A(z) = \sum a_k z^{-k}$ lie strictly **inside the unit circle** of the z-plane.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for Module 1 problems on difference equations. Master these before solving past papers.

| Concept | Formula | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **General LCCDE** | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Defines an $N$-th order LTI system |
| **Normalized Form** | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | With $a_0 = 1$, used for recursive computation |
| **Operator Form** | $\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k E^{k} \right] y[n] = \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k E^{k} \right] x[n]$ | Where $E$ is the shift operator: $E^{k} y[n] = y[n+k]$ |
| **Characteristic Eq.** | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k r^{k} = 0$ | Roots $r_i$ determine the homogeneous (natural) response |
| **Homogeneous Sol.** | $y_h[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i r_i^{n}$ | For distinct roots $r_i$ |
| **Repeated Root Sol.** | $y_h[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (C_i n^{i-1}) r^{n}$ | When root $r$ has multiplicity $N$ |
| **Particular Sol.** | $y_p[n]$ ŌĆö depends on form of $x[n]$ | Use method of undetermined coefficients |
| **Total Response** | $y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n]$ | Sum of natural + forced responses |
| **FIR Condition** | $a_k = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, N$ | No feedback ŌĆö always BIBO stable |
| **IIR Condition** | At least one $a_k \ne 0$ for $k \ge 1$ | Feedback present ŌĆö may be unstable |

> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** Throughout KTU papers, the shift operator $E$ is defined such that $E\{y[n]\} = y[n+1]$. Some textbooks use $S$ or the delay symbol $D^{-1}$. Always verify the operator definition from your module material.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Difference equations are the **backbone of digital signal processing (DSP)** and appear in:

- **Digital Audio Processing:** Every MP3 decoder, every noise-cancelling headphone, every equalizer runs on difference equations running millions of times per second.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG and EEG filtering to remove $50/60$ Hz power-line interference.
- **Control Systems:** Discrete-time PID controllers in robotics, drones, and industrial automation.
- **Communications:** Channel equalizers in 4G/5G receivers, adaptive noise cancellers.
- **Econometrics & Finance:** AR, MA, and ARMA models for stock-price prediction are difference equations.
- **Image Processing:** 2-D difference equations underlie edge-detection and blur kernels.

> [!NOTE]
> In short: **any time you sample a continuous signal and process it digitally, you are solving a difference equation.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Block Diagram to Difference Equation

Consider a discrete system implemented using two delay elements $z^{-1}$ (each represents a one-sample delay), one multiplier $a_1$, one multiplier $b_0$, and one adder. The signal flow is:

$$
w[n] = x[n] + a_1 w[n-1]
$$
$$
y[n] = b_0 w[n]
$$

**Goal:** Eliminate the intermediate variable $w[n]$ and obtain an LCCDE in $y[n]$ and $x[n]$.

**Step 1.** From the second equation:
$$
w[n] = \frac{y[n]}{b_0}
$$

**Step 2.** From the first equation:
$$
\frac{y[n]}{b_0} = x[n] + a_1 \frac{y[n-1]}{b_0}
$$

**Step 3.** Multiply both sides by $b_0$:
$$
y[n] = b_0 x[n] + a_1 y[n-1]
$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange to canonical form:
$$
y[n] - a_1 y[n-1] = b_0 x[n]
$$

**Step 5.** Identify coefficients: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = -a_1$, $b_0 = b_0$, all others zero. **System order = 1**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Solving a First-Order Difference Equation

Consider the first-order causal LCCDE:

$$
y[n] - \frac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n], \qquad y[-1] = 0
$$

Let the input be the unit step $x[n] = u[n]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the homogeneous (natural) response.**

The characteristic equation is obtained by setting the LHS to zero:
$$
y_h[n] - \frac{1}{2} y_h[n-1] = 0
$$

Assume solution of form $y_h[n] = r^n$:
$$
r^n - \frac{1}{2} r^{n-1} = 0
$$

**Step 2.** Factor out $r^{n-1}$:
$$
r^{n-1} \left( r - \frac{1}{2} \right) = 0
$$

**Step 3.** Since $r^{n-1} \ne 0$ for nontrivial solution, the characteristic equation is:
$$
r - \frac{1}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore the homogeneous solution is:
$$
y_h[n] = C \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{n}, \quad n \ge 0
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Find the particular (forced) response.**

Since the input $x[n] = u[n]$ is a constant for $n \ge 0$, assume $y_p[n] = K$ (a constant) for $n \ge 0$:
$$
K - \frac{1}{2} K = 1
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $K$:
$$
\frac{1}{2} K = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 2
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Total response.**
$$
y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n] = C \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{n} + 2, \quad n \ge 0
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Apply initial condition $y[-1] = 0$.**

Use the LCCDE at $n = 0$:
$$
y[0] - \frac{1}{2} y[-1] = u[0] = 1
$$

Since $y[-1] = 0$:
$$
y[0] = 1
$$

**Step 9.** Substitute $n = 0$ into the total response:
$$
1 = C \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{0} + 2 = C + 2
$$

**Step 10.** Solve for $C$:
$$
C = -1
$$

**Step 11 ŌĆö Final closed-form solution:**
$$
y[n] = \left[ 2 - \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{n} \right] u[n]
$$

**Step 12 ŌĆö Verification.** Compute $y[1]$ from LCCDE: $y[1] = \tfrac{1}{2} y[0] + 1 = \tfrac{3}{2}$. From formula: $y[1] = 2 - \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2}$. Ō£ō Matches.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Recursion-Based Computation

For computer / calculator-based computation (manual / iterative), given the LCCDE:

$$
y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]
$$

the following iterative matrix organizes the calculation:

| $n$ | $x[n]$ | $y[n-1]$ | $y[n-2]$ | $y[n]$ (computed) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $x[0]$ | $y[-1]$ (given) | $y[-2]$ (given) | $-a_1 y[-1] - a_2 y[-2] + b_0 x[0]$ |
| $1$ | $x[1]$ | $y[0]$ | $y[-1]$ | $-a_1 y[0] - a_2 y[-1] + b_0 x[1] + b_1 x[0]$ |
| $2$ | $x[2]$ | $y[1]$ | $y[0]$ | $-a_1 y[1] - a_2 y[0] + b_0 x[2] + b_1 x[1] + b_2 x[0]$ |
| $3$ | $x[3]$ | $y[2]$ | $y[1]$ | $\dots$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compute the first $N$ output samples," always start with the initial conditions row. A common mistake is forgetting to propagate the *computed* $y[n]$ into the next column as $y[n-1]$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Direct Form I and Direct Form II Structures

Given the LCCDE:

$$
y[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + a_2 y[n-2] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2]
$$

**Direct Form I ŌĆö Derived as follows:**

**Step 1.** Define an intermediate signal $w[n]$ capturing the feedforward part:
$$
w[n] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2]
$$

**Step 2.** The output $y[n]$ is $w[n]$ plus the feedback terms:
$$
y[n] = w[n] - a_1 y[n-1] - a_2 y[n-2]
$$

**Direct Form II (Canonical) ŌĆö Derived as follows:**

**Step 1.** Swap the two LTI sections (LCCDE is LTI, so order doesn't matter):
$$
w[n] = x[n] - a_1 w[n-1] - a_2 w[n-2]
$$

**Step 2.** Then the output is:
$$
y[n] = b_0 w[n] + b_1 w[n-1] + b_2 w[n-2]
$$

**Advantage:** Direct Form II uses only **2 delay elements** (equal to $\max(N, M)$) versus Direct Form I which uses $N + M$ delay elements. This is critical for hardware efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Difference Equation Solver

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a generic discrete-time system described by a second-order difference equation. It shows the roles of the delay elements $z^{-1}$, the multipliers, and the summation nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["Input x of n"] --> mulB0["Multiply by b0"]
    nodeInput --> delay1["Delay z inverse"]
    delay1 --> mulB1["Multiply by b1"]
    nodeInput --> delay2["Delay z inverse squared"]
    delay2 --> mulB2["Multiply by b2"]

    mulB0 --> sumTop["Adder"]
    mulB1 --> sumTop
    mulB2 --> sumTop

    sumTop --> nodeOutput["Output y of n"]
    nodeOutput --> fbDelay1["Delay z inverse"]
    fbDelay1 --> mulA1["Multiply by negative a1"]
    mulA1 --> sumTop

    nodeOutput --> fbDelay2["Delay z inverse squared"]
    fbDelay2 --> mulA2["Multiply by negative a2"]
    mulA2 --> sumTop
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top path** (feedforward): Input $x[n]$ is multiplied by coefficients $b_0$, $b_1$, $b_2$ after passing through $0$, $1$, and $2$ delay elements.
- The **bottom path** (feedback): Output $y[n]$ is fed back through delay elements and multiplied by $-a_1$ and $-a_2$ before being added.
- This is the **Direct Form I** realization.

---

### 4.2 Canonical (Direct Form II) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Input x of n"] --> sumMid["Adder"]
    sumMid --> nodeW["Intermediate w of n"]
    nodeW --> mulB0x["Multiply by b0"]
    mulB0x --> sumOut["Output Adder"]
    nodeW --> dly1["Delay z inverse"]
    dly1 --> mulB1x["Multiply by b1"]
    mulB1x --> sumOut
    nodeW --> dly2["Delay z inverse squared"]
    dly2 --> mulB2x["Multiply by b2"]
    mulB2x --> sumOut
    sumOut --> nodeOut["Output y of n"]

    dly1 --> mulA1x["Multiply by negative a1"]
    mulA1x --> sumMid
    dly2 --> mulA2x["Multiply by negative a2"]
    mulA2x --> sumMid
```

**Key observation:** Only **two delay elements** ($z^{-1}$ and $z^{-2}$) are required ŌĆö half the memory of Direct Form I.

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow Summary Table

| Block | Symbol | Mathematical Operation |
|---|---|---|
| Adder | $\oplus$ | Sum of incoming signals |
| Multiplier | $\triangle$ | $c \cdot \text{(incoming signal)}$ |
| Delay Element | $z^{-1}$ | $y[n] \to y[n-1]$ |
| Pickoff Node | $\bullet$ | Replicates signal without modification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In university exam diagrams, a delay element is drawn as a box labeled $D$ or $z^{-1}$, and a multiplier is a triangle with the coefficient written beside it. Always label arrows with their signal names ($x[n]$, $w[n]$, $y[n]$, etc.) for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a linear constant-coefficient difference equation. Distinguish between FIR and IIR systems in terms of the difference equation structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** is an equation of the form:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]
$$

where $a_k$ and $b_k$ are constants, and the equation is linear in $y[n]$ and $x[n]$.

**FIR (Finite Impulse Response) System:** All coefficients $a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$. The output depends only on present and past inputs ŌĆö no feedback term. The impulse response has finite duration.

**IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) System:** At least one coefficient $a_k \ne 0$ for $k \ge 1$. The output depends on past outputs through feedback. The impulse response can extend infinitely.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is meant by the "order" of a discrete system described by a difference equation? Why are initial conditions required?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **order** of a discrete system is the highest delay in either the input or output samples that appears in the difference equation, i.e., $\max(N, M)$.

**Why initial conditions are needed:** A difference equation of order $N$ is a recursion that, to produce a unique output sequence for $n \ge n_0$, requires the values of the last $N$ output samples (e.g., $y[n_0 - 1], y[n_0 - 2], \dots, y[n_0 - N]$) to be specified. Without these, the recursion has infinitely many solutions. For a **causal** system, the initial rest condition $y[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ is typically assumed.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the difference equation for the discrete-time system shown in the block diagram, where input $x[n]$ passes through a delay to give $x[n-1]$, then is multiplied by $2$ and added to $x[n]$, and the result is fed back through another delay multiplied by $0.5$ before forming output $y[n]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the difference equation $y[n] - 0.6 y[n-1] = 2 x[n]$ for input $x[n] = u[n]$ with initial condition $y[-1] = 5$. Compute $y[0]$, $y[1]$, $y[2]$, $y[3]$ using both the iterative method and the closed-form solution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Deriving the difference equation (7 marks):**

From the problem description, the signal flow gives the intermediate:

$$
w[n] = x[n] + 2 x[n-1]
$$

The output involves a feedback path with delay and gain $0.5$:

$$
y[n] = w[n] - 0.5 y[n-1]
$$

**Step 1.** Substitute $w[n]$:
$$
y[n] = x[n] + 2 x[n-1] - 0.5 y[n-1]
$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to canonical LCCDE form:
$$
y[n] + 0.5 y[n-1] = x[n] + 2 x[n-1]
$$

**Step 3.** Identify: $a_0 = 1$, $a_1 = 0.5$, $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 2$. System order = $1$.

---

**(b) Solving the LCCDE $y[n] - 0.6 y[n-1] = 2 u[n]$, $y[-1] = 5$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Iterative method.**

For $n = 0$:
$$
y[0] = 0.6 y[-1] + 2 u[0] = 0.6 \times 5 + 2 = 5
$$

For $n = 1$:
$$
y[1] = 0.6 y[0] + 2 = 0.6 \times 5 + 2 = 5
$$

For $n = 2$:
$$
y[2] = 0.6 y[1] + 2 = 0.6 \times 5 + 2 = 5
$$

For $n = 3$:
$$
y[3] = 0.6 y[2] + 2 = 0.6 \times 5 + 2 = 5
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Closed-form solution.**

Characteristic equation: $r - 0.6 = 0 \Rightarrow r = 0.6$. So $y_h[n] = C (0.6)^n$.

Particular solution: assume $y_p = K$ (constant since input is step):
$$
K - 0.6 K = 2 \Rightarrow K = 5
$$

Total: $y[n] = C (0.6)^n + 5$ for $n \ge 0$.

Using $y[-1] = 5$, evaluate LCCDE at $n = 0$: $y[0] = 0.6(5) + 2 = 5$.

So $y[0] = C + 5 = 5 \Rightarrow C = 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final closed form:**
$$
y[n] = 5, \quad n \ge 0
$$

This matches the iterative computation. Ō£ō

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** For the LCCDE $y[n] = 0.5 y[n-1] + x[n] - x[n-3]$, determine whether the system is FIR or IIR. Find and plot (describe) the impulse response $h[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 5$ when $x[n] = \delta[n]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A causal LTI system is described by $y[n] - y[n-1] - y[n-2] = x[n]$. Find the homogeneous solution. Comment on the stability of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Impulse response determination (7 marks):**

Since the equation contains the term $0.5 y[n-1]$, the system is **IIR (recursive)**.

Set $x[n] = \delta[n]$ and assume initial rest: $y[-1] = y[-2] = 0$.

For $n = 0$:
$$
y[0] = 0.5 y[-1] + \delta[0] - \delta[-3] = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1
$$

For $n = 1$:
$$
y[1] = 0.5 y[0] + \delta[1] - \delta[-2] = 0.5 + 0 - 0 = 0.5
$$

For $n = 2$:
$$
y[2] = 0.5 y[1] + \delta[2] - \delta[-1] = 0.25 + 0 - 0 = 0.25
$$

For $n = 3$:
$$
y[3] = 0.5 y[2] + \delta[3] - \delta[0] = 0.125 + 0 - 1 = -0.875
$$

For $n = 4$:
$$
y[4] = 0.5 y[3] + \delta[4] - \delta[1] = -0.4375 + 0 - 0 = -0.4375
$$

For $n = 5$:
$$
y[5] = 0.5 y[4] + \delta[5] - \delta[2] = -0.21875
$$

So $h[n] = \{1, 0.5, 0.25, -0.875, -0.4375, -0.21875, \dots\}$. Notice the impulse response is **non-zero for all $n \ge 0$** ŌĆö confirming IIR.

---

**(b) Homogeneous solution & stability (7 marks):**

The characteristic equation is:
$$
r^2 - r - 1 = 0
$$

**Step 1.** Apply the quadratic formula:
$$
r = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

**Step 2.** Numerical values:
$$
r_1 = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618, \qquad r_2 = \frac{1 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618
$$

**Step 3.** Homogeneous solution:
$$
y_h[n] = C_1 (1.618)^n + C_2 (-0.618)^n, \quad n \ge 0
$$

**Step 4.** Stability analysis: For a causal system to be BIBO stable, **all characteristic roots must lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert r \vert < 1$). Since $\vert r_1 \vert = 1.618 > 1$, the system is **unstable**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting initial conditions:** A first-order system needs **one** initial condition, a second-order needs **two**. Losing 1ŌĆō2 marks here is the single most common error.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Always bring all $y[n-k]$ terms to the LHS. The form $y[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + \dots$ is canonical. Students often forget to flip signs during rearrangement.
> 3. **Confusing shift operator direction:** $z^{-1}$ delays (gives past), $z$ advances (gives future). Using the wrong direction invalidates the entire block diagram.
> 4. **Stability misjudgment:** BIBO stability for *causal* systems requires $\vert r_i \vert < 1$. For *anti-causal* systems, the criterion inverts. Always state causality before applying the stability test.
> 5. **Missing the particular solution form:** A step input needs constant $y_p$, a sinusoid needs sine/cosine $y_p$, an exponential needs exponential $y_p$. Picking the wrong form wastes examination time.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **LCCDE General Form:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ ŌĆö your starting point for every problem.
- [x] **Order = $\max(N, M)$:** Always identify before solving.
- [x] **FIR vs IIR:** FIR has all $a_k = 0$ for $k \ge 1$ (no feedback); IIR has at least one non-zero $a_k$.
- [x] **Causality:** Causal ŌćÆ system depends only on present/past samples.
- [x] **Initial Conditions:** Number of required ICs = system order $N$.
- [x] **Homogeneous Solution:** Comes from characteristic roots $r_i$. Distinct roots: $C_i r_i^n$. Repeated: include polynomial in $n$.
- [x] **Particular Solution:** Guess based on input form (constant / exponential / sinusoid).
- [x] **Total Solution:** $y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n]$ ŌĆö apply initial conditions to find constants $C_i$.
- [x] **Stability (causal):** All characteristic roots inside the unit circle ($\vert r_i \vert < 1$).
- [x] **Direct Form I vs II:** Direct Form II is canonical (uses $\max(N,M)$ delays instead of $N + M$).
- [x] **Operator Notation:** $E\{y[n]\} = y[n+1]$; $z^{-1}\{y[n]\} = y[n-1]$ ŌĆö know your textbook's convention.
- [x] **Real-world Relevance:** Digital filters, audio processing, biomedical signals, control systems, econometric models ŌĆö all governed by difference equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes ŌĆö Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Systems.** Continue to Module 1 sub-topic: *Block Diagram Representation of Discrete Systems* and *Convolution Sum* for full module coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling of Continuous Time Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling of Continuous Time Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: SAMPLING OF CONTINUOUS-TIME SIGNALS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by measuring (recording) the amplitude of the continuous signal at uniformly spaced instants of time. The interval between successive samples is called the **sampling period** $T_s$ (in seconds), and its reciprocal is the **sampling frequency** $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ (in Hz) or angular sampling frequency $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_s}$ (in rad/s).

The mathematical representation of an ideally sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT**
> The **Sampling Theorem (NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem)** is a guaranteed KTU examination question. You MUST know the theorem statement, the Nyquist rate, the mathematical justification in the frequency domain, and the reconstruction formula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Movie Filmstrip Intuition

Imagine you are watching a cricket match on a cinema screen. What you perceive as smooth, continuous motion is actually a sequence of **still photographs** (frames) flashed at a high rate (typically **24 frames per second** for cinema and **30/60 frames per second** for TV). Your eyes and brain **interpolate** between successive frames and "fuse" them into continuous motion.

Sampling of a continuous-time signal works on exactly the same principle:
- The **continuous signal** is analogous to the real-world motion of the cricket ball.
- The **sampler** is analogous to a high-speed camera capturing individual frames.
- The **sampling frequency $f_s$** is analogous to the frame rate (frames per second).
- The **reconstruction filter** at the receiver is analogous to your brain's visual interpolation system.

If the frame rate is **too low** (e.g., 5 fps), the wheel of a moving car will appear to rotate **backwards** ŌĆö this visual artifact is the exact equivalent of **aliasing** in signal processing.

> [!NOTE] **Key Terminology Standardized**
> - **Sampling Period ($T_s$)** ŌåÆ Time gap between two consecutive samples ŌåÆ measured in **seconds**.
> - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** ŌåÆ Samples per second ŌåÆ measured in **Hz**.
> - **Sampling Angular Frequency ($\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$)** ŌåÆ measured in **rad/s**.
> - **Nyquist Rate ($f_N = 2 f_m$)** ŌåÆ The minimum sampling rate required to avoid aliasing, where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of $x(t)$.

### 1.3 Why is Sampling Important?

Sampling is the **bridge between the analog and digital worlds**. Every digital system ŌĆö your smartphone audio, MRI scans, digital TV, CD/DVD players, and 5G communication ŌĆö must sample the continuous-world analog signal before it can be processed digitally. Without sampling, there would be **no digital signal processing**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Sampling Techniques

KTU 2024 Scheme requires you to know **three** methods of sampling. They differ in **how the sample values are captured and held**.

### 2.1.1 Ideal (Instantaneous) Sampling ŌĆö Impulse Train Sampling

In *ideal sampling*, the continuous signal is mathematically multiplied by a periodic train of **Dirac delta impulses** separated by $T_s$.

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Key characteristics:**
- Sample width is **theoretically zero** (impulse is infinitesimally thin).
- The sampled signal is a train of impulses whose **heights are the sample values** $x(nT_s)$.
- Purely mathematical ŌĆö **cannot be realized in hardware** but forms the theoretical foundation.

**Frequency-domain analysis** uses the modulation property of the Fourier transform. The Fourier series of the impulse train $p(t)$ is:

$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

Hence the spectrum of the sampled signal is:

$$X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$$

This means **the original spectrum $X(j\omega)$ is replicated periodically every $\omega_s$ rad/s**, and each replica is scaled by $\dfrac{1}{T_s}$.

### 2.1.2 Natural Sampling (Chopper Sampling)

In *natural sampling*, the continuous signal is **multiplied by a finite-width rectangular pulse train** of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$. The **top of the sampled pulse follows the natural shape of the signal** $x(t)$ during the interval $\tau$.

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p_{\tau}(t)$$

**Key characteristics:**
- Physically realizable using an analog **sample-and-hold switch** (chopper circuit).
- The spectrum of the naturally sampled signal contains the original spectrum scaled by the duty cycle $\dfrac{\tau}{T_s}$, plus shifted replicas.

$$X_s(j\omega) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{sinc}\left(\frac{k\omega_s \tau}{2}\right) X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$$

### 2.1.3 Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold / PAM Signal)

In *flat-top sampling*, the **sample value is held constant** for the entire sampling interval using a zero-order hold (ZOH) circuit. The resulting pulse has a **flat top at amplitude $x(nT_s)$**.

$$x_{\text{flat}}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Key characteristics:**
- **Most common in real-world A/D converters** (Sample-and-Hold amplifier).
- Introduces **aperture effect** ŌĆö the spectrum is multiplied by a sinc-shaped envelope $\dfrac{\tau \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(\omega \tau/2)}{T_s}$ that **attenuates high frequencies**.
- An **equalization filter** (inverse sinc filter) is required at the receiver to compensate for this distortion.

## 2.2 The NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Statement of the Sampling Theorem**
> A **band-limited** continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with **maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz** can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies:
>
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m \quad \text{(i.e., } T_s \leq \dfrac{1}{2f_m}\text{)}$$

The **minimum sampling rate** that satisfies this is called the **Nyquist rate**:

$$f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m$$

and the corresponding **Nyquist angular frequency** is $\omega_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2\omega_m$.

## 2.3 Aliasing ŌĆö The Failure Mode

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon that occurs when the sampling rate is **insufficient** ($f_s < 2f_m$). High-frequency components of the original signal **fold back** into the lower frequency spectrum and become **indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content** ŌĆö hence the name "alias."

The aliased (apparent) frequency $f_a$ is given by:

$$f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert \quad \text{for the integer } k \text{ that minimizes } f_a$$

A practical anti-aliasing protection is to place a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** with cutoff $\omega_c = \omega_s/2$ **before** the sampler.

## 2.4 Reconstruction ŌĆö The WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation Formula

Perfect reconstruction from samples (assuming the sampling theorem is satisfied) is performed by a **low-pass filter** with impulse response equal to the **normalized sinc function**:

$$g(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t}$$

The reconstructed signal is:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] The following table contains every essential formula for this KTU module. Notice that vertical bars are written using `\vert` to preserve table formatting.

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---------:|:------------|:----------------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Sampling period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | **seconds** |
| 2 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | **Hz** |
| 3 | Angular sampling frequency | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_s} = 2\pi f_s$ | **rad/s** |
| 4 | Nyquist rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | **Hz** |
| 5 | Nyquist angular frequency | $\omega_N = 2 \omega_m$ | **rad/s** |
| 6 | Ideal sampled signal (time domain) | $x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Impulse train |
| 7 | Impulse train Fourier series | $p(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$ | Periodic |
| 8 | Sampled signal spectrum | $X_s(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$ | Replicated spectrum |
| 9 | Aliasing condition | $f_s \lt 2 f_m$ | **Violation** of theorem |
| 10 | Aliased frequency | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | $k$ chosen to minimize $f_a$ |
| 11 | Reconstruction kernel | $g(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T_s}\right)$ | Ideal LPF response |
| 12 | Reconstruction (interpolation) | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Infinite sum |
| 13 | Natural sampling spectrum | $X_s(j\omega) = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s}\sum_{k} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{k\omega_s \tau}{2}\right)X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$ | Sinc-weighted replicas |
| 14 | Flat-top envelope factor | $H_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = \dfrac{\tau\,\mathrm{sinc}(\omega\tau/2)}{T_s}\,e^{-j\omega\tau/2}$ | Aperture effect |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio CDs** sample at **44.1 kHz** (slightly above Nyquist rate for 20 kHz human hearing).
- **Digital telephony (PSTN)** uses **8 kHz** sampling (band-limited to 3.4 kHz voice).
- **Medical ECG/MRI** uses sampling rates carefully chosen to capture diagnostic frequency bands.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** relies on aliasing of high-frequency bands to "fold" them into baseband for cheaper digital processing (intentional controlled aliasing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Spectrum of an Impulse-Train Sampled Signal

**Objective:** Show that multiplication by an impulse train in the time domain produces periodic replication of the spectrum.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the impulse sampling train.**
The periodic impulse train with period $T_s$ is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express $p(t)$ via its complex exponential Fourier series.**
A periodic function can always be written as a Fourier series. The complex Fourier series coefficient $a_k$ of the impulse train is given by the fundamental integral over one period $[-T_s/2, T_s/2]$:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} p(t)\,e^{-jk\omega_s t}\,dt$$

Because the only impulse inside one period is $\delta(t)$ at $t=0$, the sifting property gives:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t)\,e^{-jk\omega_s t}\,dt = \frac{1}{T_s}\cdot 1 = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

Hence the Fourier series expansion of $p(t)$ is:

$$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\,e^{jk\omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the sampled signal.**
The sampled signal is the product of $x(t)$ and $p(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\,p(t) = x(t)\cdot\frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.**
Recall the **frequency-shifting property**: $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)\,e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$.

Applying this to every term in the sum:

$$X_s(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x_s(t)\} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$$

**Interpretation:** The original spectrum $X(j\omega)$ is **replicated** at $\omega = 0, \pm\omega_s, \pm 2\omega_s, \pm 3\omega_s, \dots$, and each replica is **scaled in amplitude by $1/T_s$**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Condition for No Aliasing (Nyquist Criterion)

**Objective:** Derive the minimum sampling frequency for unambiguous recovery.

Assume the original signal $x(t)$ is **band-limited** to $\omega_m$ (i.e., $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > \omega_m$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Examine the spectral replicas.**
From Derivation 1, the replicas are centered at $\omega = k\omega_s$. Each replica occupies the band $[k\omega_s - \omega_m,\; k\omega_s + \omega_m]$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö The replica centered at $k=0$** occupies the band $[-\omega_m, +\omega_m]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö The replica centered at $k=+1$** occupies the band $[\omega_s - \omega_m,\; \omega_s + \omega_m]$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö To prevent overlap (aliasing), the right edge of the $k=0$ replica must not touch or cross the left edge of the $k=+1$ replica:**

$$\omega_m \leq \omega_s - \omega_m$$

Rearranging:

$$2\omega_m \leq \omega_s \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$$

Equivalently, in Hz:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

This is the **Nyquist criterion**. The minimum permissible sampling frequency is $f_s^{\min} = 2f_m$, the **Nyquist rate**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Reconstruction via Low-Pass Filtering

**Objective:** Derive the WhittakerŌĆōShannon interpolation formula.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume Nyquist criterion is satisfied**, so the replicas in $X_s(j\omega)$ are non-overlapping. We want to extract only the replica centered at $k=0$, i.e., recover $X(j\omega)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply $X_s(j\omega)$ by an ideal LPF** with gain $T_s$ in the passband $[-\omega_s/2, +\omega_s/2]$ and zero elsewhere:

$$H_{\text{LPF}}(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \dfrac{\omega_s}{2} \\[4pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The filter output in the frequency domain is:

$$Y(j\omega) = X_s(j\omega) \cdot H_{\text{LPF}}(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \frac{1}{T_s}\,X(j\omega) = X(j\omega)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find the impulse response** of the ideal LPF by inverse Fourier transform. Using the standard sinc pair:

$$h(t) = \frac{T_s}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_s/2}^{\omega_s/2} e^{j\omega t}\,d\omega = \frac{\sin(\omega_s t/2)}{\pi t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\cdot\frac{\sin(\pi t/T_s)}{\pi t/T_s} = \frac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)$$

where the normalized sinc is defined as $\mathrm{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convolve $h(t)$ with the sampled signal** $x_s(t)$:

$$x(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)\right] * \frac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)$$

Using the sifting property of the delta function inside the convolution:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\cdot \frac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This is the **WhittakerŌĆōShannon interpolation formula**. Q.E.D.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Aliased Frequency Formula

**Objective:** Find the apparent (aliased) frequency when $f_s < 2f_m$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Consider a sinusoid** $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ with $f_0 > f_s/2$ (i.e., it violates Nyquist).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sample at $f_s$:**

$$x[n] = \cos(2\pi f_0 n T_s) = \cos(2\pi f_0 n / f_s)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the identity** $\cos(2\pi (f_0 \pm k f_s)t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ for any integer $k$ when evaluated at $t = nT_s$.

Thus, when reconstructing, the sinusoid appears to have a frequency $f_a$ such that:

$$f_a = \min_{k \in \mathbb{Z}} \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$$

This is the **folding equation**. The frequency $f_a$ always lies in the range $[0, f_s/2]$ ŌĆö the so-called **baseband** or **principal Nyquist zone**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ has spectrum non-zero only for $\vert f \vert \leq 4\,\text{kHz}$. It is sampled at $f_s = 10\,\text{kHz}$.

**(a)** Verify the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.
**(b)** Find the spectrum $X_s(f)$ in terms of $X(f)$.
**(c)** If the signal contains a tone at $3.5$ kHz, at what apparent frequency will it appear after sampling if $f_s$ is accidentally reduced to **5 kHz**?

**Solution:**

**(a)** Nyquist rate $= 2 f_m = 2 \times 4 = 8$ kHz. Since $f_s = 10$ kHz $\geq 8$ kHz ŌåÆ **Theorem satisfied**, no aliasing.

**(b)** From the replication formula in Hz:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) = 10 \sum_{k} X(f - 10k)\quad (\text{kHz units})$$

**(c)** With $f_s = 5$ kHz and $f_0 = 3.5$ kHz, since $f_0 > f_s/2 = 2.5$ kHz, aliasing occurs. Apply the folding equation with $k=1$:

$$f_a = \vert 3.5 - 1 \times 5 \vert = 1.5\,\text{kHz}$$

The tone **appears at 1.5 kHz** after sampling at 5 kHz. Ō£ģ

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 High-Level Sampling and Reconstruction Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the canonical real-world sampling-to-reconstruction pipeline, including the anti-aliasing filter and reconstruction filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x of t: Continuous Time Input"] --> B["Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter"]
    B --> C["Sampler: Impulse Modulator at fs Hz"]
    C --> D["A to D Converter with Quantizer"]
    D --> E["Digital Processing Block"]
    E --> F["D to A Converter"]
    F --> G["Reconstruction Low Pass Filter"]
    G --> H["y of t: Reconstructed Continuous Output"]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** The anti-aliasing filter strictly limits the input bandwidth to $f_s/2$ **before** sampling. The reconstruction LPF is the matching inverse operation that "smooths" the staircase-like DAC output back into a clean continuous waveform.

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Aliasing Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE SAMPLING - Single Baseband Spectrum"]
        direction LR
        B1["X of f centered at 0 Hz, Bandwidth 2fm"]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER SAMPLING - Periodic Replication in Frequency"]
        direction LR
        C1["Replica at k = 0"]
        C2["Replica at k = 1 at fs"]
        C3["Replica at k = 2 at 2fs"]
        C4["Replica at k = 1 at minus fs"]
    end

    subgraph ALIAS["VIOLATION - Aliasing Overlap"]
        direction LR
        D1["Adjacent replicas overlap when fs lt 2fm"]
        D2["High frequency folds onto low frequency baseband"]
    end

    BEFORE --> AFTER
    AFTER --> ALIAS
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequent question asks you to "sketch the spectrum of the sampled signal." Use the above three-zone mental model ŌĆö original baseband ŌåÆ replicated at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s$ ŌåÆ overlap region when Nyquist is violated.

## 4.3 The Three Sampling Methods ŌĆö Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["Continuous Signal x of t"] --> CHOPPER["Chopper Switch / Multiplier"]
    CHOPPER --> PULSE["Periodic Pulse Train p of t"]
    PULSE --> ROUTE{Sampling Method}
    ROUTE -->|"Method 1 Ideal"| M1["Impulse Train Sampler: Mathematically Multiplying by Dirac Comb"]
    ROUTE -->|"Method 2 Natural"| M2["Finite Width Rectangular Pulses: Top follows signal shape"]
    ROUTE -->|"Method 3 Flat Top"| M3["Zero Order Hold: Sample value held constant for Ts"]
    M1 --> OUT1["Series of Weighted Impulses"]
    M2 --> OUT2["PAM Signal: Natural Curved Tops"]
    M3 --> OUT3["PAM Signal: Flat Staircase Tops"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart ŌĆö Does Aliasing Occur?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is x of t band limited with maximum frequency fm?"}
    Q1 -->|"No, infinite bandwidth"| A1["ALIASING UNAVOIDABLE: Apply strict anti aliasing LPF first"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Is fs greater than or equal to 2 fm?"}
    Q2 -->|"No, fs lt 2 fm"| A2["ALIASING OCCURS: High frequencies fold into baseband"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes, fs greater than or equal to 2 fm"| A3["NO ALIASING: Original signal can be perfectly reconstructed"]
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Reality:** No real-world signal is truly band-limited, so a practical anti-aliasing LPF with a sharp cutoff is always inserted **before** the sampler. The filter cutoff is typically chosen as $f_c = f_s/2 - f_{\text{guard}}$ where $f_{\text{guard}}$ is a small safety margin (e.g., 5ŌĆō10%) to allow for realizable filter roll-off.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] KTU Part A questions test **definition recall** and **short derivations** at the **Remember / Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023] State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem and define the Nyquist rate.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement:** A band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely and perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
>
> **Nyquist Rate:** The minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing is called the Nyquist rate, $f_N = 2 f_m$ Hz. The corresponding sampling period is $T_s \leq \dfrac{1}{2f_m}$ seconds.
>
> *Example:* If the highest frequency in a signal is 4 kHz, then the Nyquist rate is **8 kHz** and we must sample at $f_s \geq 8$ kHz.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022] What is aliasing? How can it be prevented?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous signal, when sampled at an insufficient rate $f_s < 2f_m$, become indistinguishable from lower-frequency components in the reconstructed signal ŌĆö they appear as "false" lower frequencies.
>
> **Prevention Methods:**
> 1. **Sample at or above the Nyquist rate** $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
> 2. **Use an anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c = f_s/2$ placed *before* the sampler to pre-limit the signal bandwidth.
> 3. **Increase the sampling rate** of the A/D converter where possible.
>
> **Mathematical Expression of Aliasing:** The aliased (apparent) frequency is $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ where $k$ is the integer that minimizes $f_a$.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE] KTU Part B questions test **Apply / Analyze / Evaluate** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ­¤öĘ QUESTION A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of an ideally sampled signal $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$ and show that the original spectrum is replicated at multiples of the sampling frequency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency of **5 kHz**.
  - (i) Determine the **minimum sampling rate** to avoid aliasing.  
  - (ii) If the signal is sampled at **8 kHz** and contains spectral components at **1 kHz, 3 kHz, 4.5 kHz, and 6 kHz**, determine which components will be **aliased** and find the **apparent frequencies**.  
  - (iii) Sketch the spectrum of the sampled signal. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Define the impulse train as a periodic function with period $T_s$:

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2:** Its complex exponential Fourier series is:

$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}, \quad \text{where } \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply $x(t)$ by $p(t)$:

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\,p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Fourier Transform and use the **frequency-shifting property** $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$:

$$X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)$$

**Conclusion:** The spectrum $X(j\omega)$ is **replicated periodically at $\omega = 0, \pm\omega_s, \pm 2\omega_s, \ldots$**, with each replica scaled in amplitude by $\dfrac{1}{T_s}$. *(7 marks distributed as: 1 mark for impulse-train definition, 2 marks for Fourier series, 2 marks for multiplication, 2 marks for FT and frequency-shifting.)*

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**(i)** Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist rate):

$$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = \mathbf{10\ \text{kHz}}$$

**(ii)** Check each component against the Nyquist condition $f_s/2 = 8/2 = 4$ kHz.

| **Original Frequency** | **Aliased?** | **Calculation** | **Apparent Frequency** |
|:----------------------:|:------------:|:----------------|:----------------------:|
| 1 kHz | No | $1 \le 4$ | 1 kHz |
| 3 kHz | No | $3 \le 4$ | 3 kHz |
| 4.5 kHz | **Yes** | $\vert 4.5 - 8 \vert = 3.5$ | 3.5 kHz |
| 6 kHz | **Yes** | $\vert 6 - 8 \vert = 2$ | 2 kHz |

**(iii) Spectrum sketch description:** The original baseband spectrum (1 kHz, 3 kHz, 4.5 kHz, 6 kHz) is replicated at $\pm 8$ kHz, $\pm 16$ kHz, ... Because $f_s = 8$ kHz $< 2 f_m = 10$ kHz, the replicas centered at $\pm 8$ kHz **overlap** with the original baseband, creating **aliased tones at 1 kHz, 3 kHz, 2 kHz, and 3.5 kHz** in the effective baseband.

---

### ­¤öĘ QUESTION B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **three types of sampling**: ideal (impulse), natural, and flat-top sampling. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **WhittakerŌĆōShannon interpolation formula**, derive the **reconstruction filter** response and show how an original continuous-time signal is recovered from its samples. State the necessary condition for perfect reconstruction. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**1. Ideal (Impulse-Train) Sampling:**
$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$
The continuous signal is multiplied by a Dirac comb. The output is a sequence of weighted impulses. **Advantage:** Cleanest mathematical framework; spectra are exact scaled replicas. **Disadvantage:** Impulses have zero width and infinite amplitude ŌĆö *not realizable in hardware*.

**2. Natural Sampling (Chopper Sampling):**
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p_{\tau}(t), \quad p_{\tau}(t) = \text{rectangular pulse train of width } \tau$$
The top of each sample follows the natural shape of $x(t)$. **Advantage:** Physically realizable using analog switches. **Disadvantage:** Pulse width $\tau$ introduces a sinc roll-off distortion at higher frequencies.

**3. Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold / PAM):**
$$x_{\text{flat}}(t) = \sum_{n} x(nT_s)\,\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$
A zero-order hold (ZOH) circuit holds the sample value constant. **Advantage:** Most widely used in A/D converters; the value is held long enough for the converter to digitize. **Disadvantage:** Aperture effect ŌĆö the spectrum is multiplied by $\mathrm{sinc}(\omega \tau/2)$ causing high-frequency attenuation; requires equalization filter.

*(Allocate ~2 marks per type + 1 mark for the comparison table.)*

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation Formula:**
Assuming the Nyquist criterion $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is satisfied, the continuous signal can be perfectly reconstructed as:

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the Reconstruction Filter:**
The expression above is a **convolution** of the sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$ with the impulse response:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t/T_s)}{\pi t}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Fourier Transform of the Reconstruction Filter:**
Taking the Fourier transform of $h(t)$ yields an **ideal low-pass filter** with gain $T_s$ over the passband $\vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_s/2$ and zero elsewhere:

$$H(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \dfrac{\omega_s}{2} \\[4pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify the Filtering Action:**
The spectrum of the sampled signal is $X_s(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$. Multiplying by $H(j\omega)$:

$$X_s(j\omega) \cdot H(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \frac{1}{T_s}\,X(j\omega) = X(j\omega)$$

The output spectrum is **exactly the original spectrum** of $x(t)$, confirming perfect reconstruction.

**Necessary Condition for Perfect Reconstruction:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, i.e., the signal must be **strictly band-limited** and the sampling rate must **exceed the Nyquist rate**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING] **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ŌĆö READ CAREFULLY**
>
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** The *Nyquist rate* is **$2f_m$** (a sampling frequency); the *Nyquist frequency* is **$f_s/2$** (a signal-band cutoff). Examiners specifically check if you used the correct one in the correct context. (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling factor** in the spectrum replication formula. The correct result is $X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$. Writing just $X_s(j\omega) = \sum_k X(\cdots)$ loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
>
> 3. **Skipping the sifting step in the derivation** of the sampled signal. You must explicitly use $\int \delta(t-a) f(t) dt = f(a)$ to evaluate the Fourier coefficient $a_k$. Examiners want to see it written. (ŌĆō1 mark)
>
> 4. **Mixing up natural and flat-top sampling** in diagrams. Natural sampling has **curved tops** following $x(t)$; flat-top sampling has **horizontal flat tops** at the held sample value. Many students draw both as flat ŌĆö examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating the band-limited assumption** of the sampling theorem. A correct statement **must** include: "A *band-limited* signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$..." Missing the word "band-limited" costs a mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing Hz and rad/s in the final numerical answer.** If the problem gives $f_m$ in kHz, then $f_N$ is also in kHz. Some students write $f_N = 2\pi(2f_m)$ ŌĆö this is **wrong** ŌĆö the $2\pi$ only appears when converting to **angular** frequency $\omega_N$. (ŌĆō1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **­¤ÜĆ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö Print This Section!**

### Ō£ģ Definitional Essentials
- **Sampling**: Conversion of continuous-time to discrete-time signal at uniform intervals $T_s$.
- **Sampling Frequency**: $f_s = 1/T_s$ Hz.
- **Nyquist Rate**: $f_N = 2f_m$ Hz ŌĆö minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
- **Aliasing**: High frequencies folding back into the baseband when $f_s < 2f_m$.

### Ō£ģ Three Types of Sampling
- **Ideal / Impulse-Train**: $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ ŌĆö mathematical, not realizable.
- **Natural**: $x(t)$ multiplied by rectangular pulse train ŌĆö top of pulse follows signal shape.
- **Flat-Top (ZOH)**: Sample value held constant for $\tau$ seconds ŌĆö most common in A/D converters.

### Ō£ģ Key Equations to Memorize
- Spectrum replication: $X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(j(\omega - k\omega_s))$
- Reconstruction: $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT_s}{T_s}\right)$
- Aliasing: $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$

### Ō£ģ Practical Engineering Reminders
- Always place an **anti-aliasing LPF** with cutoff $f_s/2$ **before** the sampler.
- CDs use **44.1 kHz** sampling for 20 kHz audio bandwidth.
- A/D converters use a **Sample-and-Hold** (ZOH) circuit, which is the practical implementation of flat-top sampling.
- Reconstruction is performed by a **low-pass filter** with sinc impulse response $\frac{1}{T_s}\mathrm{sinc}(t/T_s)$.

### Ō£ģ Common Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid
- Check whether the given $f_m$ is **Hz or rad/s** before applying the Nyquist formula.
- When checking aliasing, the cutoff is **$f_s/2$**, not $f_s$.
- Anti-aliasing filter cutoff is also $f_s/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Theorem for Low Pass Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sampling Theorem for Low Pass Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Sampling Theorem for Low-Pass (Band-Limited) Signals**, also known as the **NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem**, is a foundational result in digital signal processing that establishes the necessary and sufficient condition for the perfect reconstruction of a continuous-time signal from its discrete samples. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard):** > A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz (or $\omega_m$ rad/sec) can be uniquely and completely reconstructed from its instantaneous samples $x(nT_s)$ provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition: > > $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$ > > where $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ is the sampling period.
