The minimum theoretical sampling rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Frequency**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Stroboscopic Wheel" Effect** > > Imagine a spinning bicycle wheel with a single white mark. If you take photos of it at very slow intervals (low sampling rate), the wheel may appear stationary or rotating backwards ŌĆö this is the visual equivalent of **aliasing**. However, if you capture frames fast enough (more than twice per revolution), the mark's position in every frame is unique and you can correctly determine the actual rotational speed and direction. > > **The Rule of Thumb:** A "smooth" signal (one that does not wiggle too fast) needs only a few snapshots per second to be uniquely identified. The fastest wiggle it contains is $f_m$, and you need at least $2 f_m$ snapshots per second to catch every wiggle accurately. ### 1.3 Key Terminology and Metrics | Term | Definition | Mathematical Symbol | |------|------------|---------------------| | Maximum Signal Frequency | Highest frequency present in the bandlimited signal | $f_m$ (Hz) or $\omega_m$ (rad/s) | | Sampling Frequency | Number of samples taken per second | $f_s$ (Hz) or $\omega_s$ (rad/s) | | Sampling Period | Time interval between two consecutive samples | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | | Nyquist Rate | Minimum sampling rate that allows perfect reconstruction | $f_N = 2f_m$ | | Oversampling | Sampling rate strictly greater than Nyquist rate | $f_s > 2f_m$ | | Undersampling | Sampling rate less than Nyquist rate (causes aliasing) | $f_s < 2f_m$ | ### 1.4 Why This Theorem Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Significance:** This theorem is the mathematical bridge that allows the real-world continuous (analog) signals ŌĆö voice, music, images, video, biomedical signals ŌĆö to be processed by digital systems (computers, DSP chips, mobile phones). Without it, modern digital communication, CD/DVD audio, digital photography, and MRI/CT scans would be mathematically impossible. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Theorem The sampling theorem rests on three critical pillars. Let us examine the "Why" and "How" of each: **Pillar 1 ŌĆö The Bandlimited Assumption** - *Why:* A signal that contains frequencies from $0$ to a finite maximum $f_m$ Hz is called **bandlimited**. Such a signal has a Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ that is non-zero only over a finite band $[-\omega_m, \omega_m]$. - *How:* The sampling theorem ONLY applies to such bandlimited signals. Real-world signals (like speech which extends to $\infty$) must be pre-filtered using an **Anti-Aliasing Filter** before sampling. **Pillar 2 ŌĆö The Sampling Operation** - *Why:* To convert a continuous signal into discrete samples, we multiply it by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$. - *How:* The resulting sampled signal $x_s(t)$ contains the original information scaled and replicated in the frequency domain at integer multiples of $\omega_s$. **Pillar 3 ŌĆö The Reconstruction Condition (Nyquist Criterion)** - *Why:* If the spectral replicas overlap, the original signal's spectrum is corrupted and **cannot be recovered** ŌĆö this phenomenon is called **aliasing**. - *How:* To prevent overlap, the gap between adjacent replicas must remain non-negative. This forces the condition $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$, equivalently $f_s \geq 2f_m$. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Reference Table** (Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity): | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Condition | |---|--------------|------------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Samples per second | | 2 | Angular Sampling Frequency | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_s}$ | Radians per second | | 3 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | | 4 | Nyquist Condition | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Reconstruction is possible only if this holds | | 5 | Impulse Train (Sampling Function) | $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$ | Mathematical model of ideal sampler | | 6 | Fourier Series of Impulse Train | $p(t) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$ | Used in frequency domain analysis | | 7 | Sampled Signal in Time Domain | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$ | Output of ideal sampler | | 8 | Sampled Signal in Frequency Domain | $X_s(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ | Periodic replication of spectrum | | 9 | Reconstruction Filter (Ideal LPF) | $H(\omega) = T_s$ for $\vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_m$, and $0$ otherwise | Extracts baseband replica | | 10 | Reconstruction Formula (Time Domain) | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{\omega_s(t - nT_s)}{2}\right)$ | WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Digital Audio (CD Standard):** Audio signals are bandlimited at $f_m = 20$ kHz, sampled at $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, comfortably exceeding the Nyquist rate of $40$ kHz. - **Telephony:** Voice is bandlimited to $f_m = 3.4$ kHz and sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz (Nyquist rate would be $6.8$ kHz). - **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** k-space data is sampled and reconstructed using the sampling theorem principles. - **Oscilloscopes and Data Acquisition (DAQ):** Bandwidth determines the minimum required sample rate. - **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Wideband signals are sampled directly (with careful anti-aliasing). - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG and EEG signals are sampled per the theorem to enable digital diagnosis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Practical Note:** In every real-world system, an **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** ŌĆö a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c \leq f_m$ ŌĆö is placed *before* the sampler. This ensures that out-of-band noise and high-frequency content above $f_m$ do not corrupt the sampled data. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mathematical Statement of the Sampling Theorem **Statement:** Let $x(t)$ be a bandlimited signal with $X(\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > \omega_m$. Then $x(t)$ is uniquely determined by its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided that $\omega_s = 2\pi/T_s \geq 2\omega_m$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Spectrum of the Sampled Signal (Core Proof) **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Ideal Sampling Signal** Consider an ideal impulse train (a periodic train of Dirac delta functions) with period $T_s$: $$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Express the Impulse Train via Fourier Series** Because $p(t)$ is periodic with period $T_s$, it admits a complex exponential Fourier series expansion: $$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} c_k \, e^{jk\omega_s t}$$ The Fourier series coefficients $c_k$ are computed as: $$c_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} p(t) \, e^{-jk\omega_s t} \, dt$$ Substituting the impulse train definition and using the sifting property of the delta function $\int \delta(t) f(t) \, dt = f(0)$: $$c_k = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t) \, e^{-jk\omega_s t} \, dt = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot e^{0} = \frac{1}{T_s}$$ Therefore, the impulse train can be rewritten as: $$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Form the Sampled Signal in the Time Domain** Multiplication of $x(t)$ by $p(t)$ gives the ideal sampled signal: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = x(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$ Rearranging the order of summation and multiplication: $$x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{jk\omega_s t}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Take the Fourier Transform of Both Sides** Using the **frequency shifting property** of the Fourier Transform: $\mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X(\omega - k\omega_s)$, we obtain: $$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Interpret the Result** This is the **central equation** of the sampling theorem. It states that the spectrum of the sampled signal $X_s(\omega)$ consists of **infinitely many copies** of the original spectrum $X(\omega)$, each shifted to center frequencies $k\omega_s$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$, and each scaled by $1/T_s$. When $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$, these replicas are **non-overlapping** and the original spectrum $X(\omega)$ can be extracted by passing $x_s(t)$ through an ideal low-pass filter. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Reconstruction Formula (WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation) Assume that the Nyquist condition $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ holds, so the replicas do not overlap. We pass $x_s(t)$ through an ideal low-pass filter with gain $T_s$ in the passband: $$H(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_c \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \omega_c \end{cases}$$ where the cutoff frequency $\omega_c$ is chosen such that $\omega_m \leq \omega_c \leq \omega_s - \omega_m$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Frequency Domain Output of the Filter** The output spectrum is: $$Y(\omega) = X_s(\omega) \cdot H(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \cdot X(\omega) \cdot T_s = X(\omega)$$ This confirms that the LPF perfectly recovers the original spectrum when there is no aliasing. **Step 2 ŌĆö Time Domain Reconstruction Formula** The impulse response of the ideal LPF is the inverse Fourier transform of $H(\omega)$: $$h(t) = \frac{T_s \omega_c}{\pi} \, \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\omega_c t}{\pi}\right) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c t)}{\pi t / T_s}$$ If we choose $\omega_c = \omega_s / 2 = \pi / T_s$, then: $$h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convolve with the Sampled Signal** The reconstructed signal is $y(t) = x_s(t) * h(t)$: $$x(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s) \right] * h(t)$$ Using the sifting property of the delta function under convolution: $$\boxed{x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi (t - nT_s)}{T_s}\right)}$$ This is the famous **WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation Formula**. It expresses the original continuous signal as a weighted sum of shifted sinc functions, with weights equal to the sample values $x(nT_s)$. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Proof that $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ is Necessary Assume two adjacent spectral replicas are centered at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \omega_s$. To prevent their overlap, the right edge of the baseband replica ($\omega = +\omega_m$) must be strictly less than the left edge of the first shifted replica ($\omega = \omega_s - \omega_m$): $$\omega_m \leq \omega_s - \omega_m$$ Rearranging: $$2\omega_m \leq \omega_s$$ Equivalently, in Hz: $$2f_m \leq f_s$$ This is the **Nyquist Criterion**. If $f_s < 2f_m$, the replicas overlap and information is irreversibly lost ŌĆö this is the **aliasing** condition. --- ### 3.5 Aliasing ŌĆö Mathematical Insight When $f_s < 2f_m$, the high-frequency components fold back into the baseband. A frequency component at $f_a$ is observed (aliased) at a lower frequency $f_{\text{alias}}$ given by: $$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_a - k f_s \vert$$ where $k$ is the integer chosen so that the result lies in $[0, f_s/2]$. The aliased frequency is the only one retrievable, since the original high-frequency content is now indistinguishable from the low-frequency content. **Example:** If $f_s = 30$ Hz and a signal contains a frequency $f_a = 40$ Hz (with $f_m = 40$ Hz, so $f_s < 2f_m = 80$ Hz), then $k = 1$ gives $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 40 - 30 \vert = 10$ Hz. The 40 Hz component will *appear* in the reconstruction as a 10 Hz component. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Sampling and Reconstruction System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CONTINUOUS
INPUT SIGNAL
x(t)"] --> B["ANTI-ALIASING
LOW-PASS FILTER
Cutoff = fm"] B --> C["IDEAL SAMPLER
Impulse Train
fs >= 2fm"] C --> D["SAMPLED SIGNAL
xs(t)
Discrete Samples"] D --> E["DIGITAL
PROCESSING
DSP System"] E --> F["RECONSTRUCTION
LOW-PASS FILTER
Cutoff = fm"] F --> G["RECONSTRUCTED
OUTPUT SIGNAL
y(t) = x(t)"] A -. BANDLIMITED
Up to fm .-> A ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Frequency Domain Spectral Replication ```mermaid flowchart TD input["ORIGINAL SPECTRUM
X of omega
BANDLIMITED to minus omega m to plus omega m"] --> sampler["SAMPLING OPERATION
Multiplication by Impulse Train"] sampler --> replica1["REPLICA 1
Centered at zero
Scaled by one over Ts"] sampler --> replica2["REPLICA 2
Centered at plus omega s
Scaled by one over Ts"] sampler --> replica3["REPLICA 3
Centered at minus omega s
Scaled by one over Ts"] sampler --> replicaN["REPLICA K
Centered at k times omega s"] replica1 --> overlap{IS omega s
AT LEAST 2 omega m} overlap -->|YES| lpf["LOW PASS FILTER
Gain Ts, Cutoff omega m"] overlap -->|NO| alias["ALIASING OCCURS
Spectrum Corrupted"] lpf --> output["RECONSTRUCTED
X of omega
Original Signal"] style input fill:#e1f5ff style lpf fill:#d4f4dd style alias fill:#ffd4d4 style output fill:#d4f4dd ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: Nyquist Condition Graphical Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["BANDWIDTH
2 times omega m
Total width of one replica"] --> B["GUARD BAND
omega s minus 2 omega m
Gap between adjacent replicas"] B --> C{IS GUARD BAND
POSITIVE OR ZERO} C -->|YES| D["fs greater or equal 2 fm
NO ALIASING
Reconstruction Possible"] C -->|NO| E["fs less than 2 fm
ALIASING OCCURS
Reconstruction Impossible"] D --> F["Safe Operating Region"] E --> G["Danger Region"] style F fill:#d4f4dd style G fill:#ffd4d4 ``` ### 4.4 Process Flow: Three Sampling Rate Regimes ```mermaid flowchart TD start["CHOOSE SAMPLING FREQUENCY fs"] --> q1["WHAT IS MAXIMUM
SIGNAL FREQUENCY fm"] q1 --> q2{"IS fs GREATER
THAN 2 fm"} q2 -->|YES OVERSAMPLING| p1["Easy reconstruction
Large guard band
Low aliasing risk"] q2 -->|NO| q3{"IS fs EQUAL
TO 2 fm"} q3 -->|YES NYQUIST RATE| p2["Theoretical minimum
No guard band
Ideal LPF required"] q3 -->|NO UNDER SAMPLING| p3["Aliasing occurs
Information lost
Reconstruction FAILS"] style p1 fill:#d4f4dd style p2 fill:#fff4cc style p3 fill:#ffd4d4 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question A1]** > **Q1.** State the sampling theorem for low-pass signals. Define Nyquist rate. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Statement:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$. **Nyquist Rate Definition:** The minimum sampling rate at which a bandlimited signal can be sampled without causing aliasing, given by: $$f_N = 2f_m$$ --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question A2]** > **Q2.** What is aliasing? How can it be prevented? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal appear as lower frequencies in the reconstructed signal due to insufficient sampling rate, causing irreversible distortion. **Prevention Methods:** 1. Sample the signal at a rate $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (satisfy Nyquist criterion). 2. Use an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before sampling to bandlimit the signal to $f_m$. 3. Choose a sampling rate comfortably above the Nyquist rate (oversampling) to provide a practical guard band. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question B1]** > ### Question A (14 Marks) > **(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a signal $x(t)$ when sampled by an impulse train of period $T_s$. Hence obtain the Nyquist criterion for sampling. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the process of reconstruction of the signal from its samples using an ideal low-pass filter. Derive the WhittakerŌĆōShannon interpolation formula. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Consider a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $\omega_m$, and an ideal impulse train sampler: $$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 2:** The Fourier series representation of $p(t)$ is: $$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$$ **Step 3:** The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$: $$x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{jk\omega_s t}$$ **Step 4:** Taking the Fourier Transform on both sides and using the frequency shifting property: $$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$ This shows that the spectrum of the sampled signal consists of infinitely many shifted replicas of $X(\omega)$ centered at $k\omega_s$. **Step 5:** To prevent overlap between adjacent replicas, we require: $$\omega_m + \omega_m \leq \omega_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$$ Equivalently, $f_s \geq 2f_m$. This is the **Nyquist Criterion**. **[1 Mark for conclusion]** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks] **Step 1:** Assuming the Nyquist condition is satisfied, the spectrum $X(\omega)$ can be extracted by passing $x_s(t)$ through an ideal low-pass filter: $$H(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_c \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \omega_c \end{cases}$$ where $\omega_m \leq \omega_c \leq \omega_s - \omega_m$. **Step 2:** The impulse response of this LPF is: $$h(t) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c t)}{\pi t} \cdot T_s$$ **Step 3:** With $\omega_c = \omega_s/2 = \pi/T_s$: $$h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi t}{T_s}\right)$$ **Step 4:** The reconstructed signal is the convolution $y(t) = x_s(t) * h(t)$: $$y(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s) \right] * \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi t}{T_s}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Using the sifting property of convolution with delta functions: $$\boxed{x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi (t - nT_s)}{T_s}\right)}$$ This is the **WhittakerŌĆōShannon Interpolation Formula**. **[1 Mark for final formula]** --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative > **(a)** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ has maximum frequency $f_m = 5$ kHz. Find the Nyquist rate. If the signal is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz, determine whether aliasing will occur. Justify. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of aliasing in detail with a suitable diagram. Explain the role of the anti-aliasing filter in practical sampling systems. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks] **Given:** $f_m = 5$ kHz, $f_s = 8$ kHz **Step 1 ŌĆö Nyquist Rate:** $$f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compare $f_s$ with $f_N$:** $$f_s = 8 \text{ kHz} < f_N = 10 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion:** Since $f_s < 2f_m$, the **Nyquist condition is violated**, and **aliasing will occur**. **[1 Mark for conclusion]** **Step 4 ŌĆö Quantitative Impact (Bonus Insight):** The spectral replicas will overlap. The aliased frequency of a component at $f_a$ can be computed as: $$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_a - k f_s \vert$$ For example, a $7$ kHz component appears at $\vert 7 - 1 \cdot 8 \vert = 1$ kHz in the reconstructed signal. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks] **Aliasing Explanation:** Aliasing is the irreversible distortion that occurs when a continuous-time signal is sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$). High-frequency components above $f_s/2$ fold back into the baseband, masquerading as low-frequency components. **Diagram Description (to be drawn in exam):** Plot the original spectrum $X(\omega)$ from $-\omega_m$ to $+\omega_m$. Then show the shifted replicas centered at $\pm \omega_s, \pm 2\omega_s, \ldots$ If $\omega_s < 2\omega_m$, the replicas overlap, and a portion of the high-frequency energy falls back into the baseband region. **[2 Marks for explanation]** **Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):** 1. The AAF is a low-pass filter placed **before** the sampler. 2. Its cutoff frequency $f_c$ is chosen such that $f_c \leq f_s/2$ (less than the Nyquist frequency). 3. It removes all frequency content above $f_c$ that could cause aliasing upon sampling. 4. In practice, ideal filters are unrealizable, so practical AAFs use Butterworth or Chebyshev approximations with sufficient stopband attenuation. 5. **Trade-off:** A very low $f_c$ discards useful high-frequency information; a very high $f_c$ allows aliasing. The choice balances signal fidelity and noise rejection. **[2 Marks for AAF role]** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** The Nyquist **Rate** is $2f_m$ (a sampling frequency). The Nyquist **Frequency** is $f_s/2$ (the highest recoverable frequency in Hz). Many students interchange them ŌĆö the examiner will deduct marks. > > 2. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling factor:** In the equation $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum X(\omega - k\omega_s)$, the $1/T_s$ factor MUST appear. Omitting it is a frequent error. > > 3. **Missing the "bandlimited" assumption:** The theorem ONLY holds for bandlimited signals. Writing the theorem without stating this assumption loses at least **1 mark** in 7-mark sub-questions. > > 4. **Wrong sinc argument:** In the reconstruction formula, the argument must be $\dfrac{\pi(t - nT_s)}{T_s}$ (or equivalently $\dfrac{\omega_s(t - nT_s)}{2}$). Using $\dfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}$ without the $\pi$ factor is incorrect. > > 5. **Sign error in aliasing formula:** The aliasing fold-over formula $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_a - k f_s \vert$ requires the **absolute value**. Writing $f_a - k f_s$ without the modulus is a common error. > > 6. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter discussion:** Whenever aliasing is mentioned in a KTU question, the expected answer should include a brief note on anti-aliasing filtering ŌĆö even if not explicitly asked. > > 7. **Units mismatch:** Mixing up Hz and rad/s without conversion (e.g., $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$) costs marks. Always state units explicitly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **The Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal with $f_m$ Hz maximum frequency can be reconstructed from its samples if and only if $f_s \geq 2f_m$. **[Must state the "bandlimited" condition]** - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$ Hz ŌĆö the minimum sampling rate. - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_s / 2$ Hz ŌĆö the highest baseband frequency recoverable. - **Impulse Train Sampler:** $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$ - **Sampled Spectrum Formula:** $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ ŌĆö periodic replication of the original spectrum. - **Reconstruction Formula (WhittakerŌĆōShannon):** $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi(t - nT_s)}{T_s}\right)$ - **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$; causes irreversible loss of high-frequency information. - **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A pre-sampling LPF with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ to bandlimit the signal and prevent aliasing. - **Oversampling:** $f_s > 2f_m$ ŌĆö provides a guard band, making filter design easier and more tolerant to practical imperfections. - **Nyquist Sampling:** $f_s = 2f_m$ ŌĆö theoretical minimum; requires an ideal LPF (impractical in real systems). - **Undersampling:** $f_s < 2f_m$ ŌĆö aliasing occurs; signal is corrupted beyond recovery. - **Practical Example:** Voice signal ($f_m = 4$ kHz) is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz in standard telephony systems.

topics.title: Aliasing, Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aliasing, Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aliasing

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Aliasing is a phenomenon in signal processing where a continuous-time signal becomes indistinguishable from another signal after sampling, due to insufficient sampling rate. Mathematically, when a signal $x(t)$ containing frequency components higher than half the sampling frequency ($f_s/2$) is sampled, these high-frequency components "fold back" into the lower frequency range, creating false (aliased) frequency components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_m$ is referred to as the **Nyquist Frequency**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):**
Imagine a spinning wheel with spokes in a video. As the wheel speeds up, beyond a certain rotation rate, the wheel appears to spin **backwards** or **stand still**. This is aliasing in real life ŌĆö your eyes (the sampler) cannot capture the wheel's true fast motion, so it "pretends" to move differently. Similarly, when we sample a signal too slowly, the computer gets fooled into thinking a fast sine wave is actually a slow one.

**Key Physical Constants & Metrics (in bold):**
- **Nyquist Rate: $f_s = 2 f_m$** (minimum safe sampling frequency)
- **Nyquist Frequency: $f_N = f_s / 2$** (highest recoverable frequency)
- **Sampling Period: $T = 1 / f_s$** seconds

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass analog filter placed *before* the sampler, designed to limit the input signal's bandwidth to $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing. This is a **mandatory component** in any practical Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) system.

---

## 1.2 The Z-Transform

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The Z-Transform of a discrete-time signal $x(n)$ is a mathematical transform that converts a discrete sequence in the time domain into a complex frequency-domain representation. It is the discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace Transform and serves as the primary tool for analyzing Linear Shift-Invariant (LSI) discrete-time systems and Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equations (LCCDE).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):**
Think of the Z-Transform as a "spectroscope for digital signals." Just as a prism splits white light into rainbow colors (frequencies), the Z-Transform takes a sequence of numbers $x(n)$ and tells you *which growth/decay patterns* and *which oscillation rates* are hiding inside it. If a signal is *growing* rapidly, the Z-Transform sees that as a pole near the outside. If it's *oscillating* rapidly, the Z-Transform shows that as a pole near the unit circle at an angle.

**The Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform Definition:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable, $z = re^{j\omega}$.

**The Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform Definition:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x(n) \, z^{-n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Fourier Transform:**
> When we evaluate the Z-Transform *on the unit circle* ($z = e^{j\omega}$), we get the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**:
> $$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$
> This means the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) is the **boundary of convergence** for the DTFT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Aliasing Mechanism ŌĆö Deep Analysis

### The Underlying Mathematics of Aliasing
When a continuous sinusoid $\cos(2\pi f t)$ is sampled with period $T$, the sampled sequence is:

$$x(nT) = \cos(2\pi f n T) = \cos(2\pi n f / f_s)$$

Frequencies $f$ and $f \pm k f_s$ (for any integer $k$) produce **identical samples**. This is the mathematical root of aliasing. The apparent (aliased) frequency is:

$$f_{alias} = \left| f - k f_s \right| \quad \text{where } k = \left\lfloor \frac{f + f_s/2}{f_s} \right\rfloor$$

### Why Does Aliasing Happen? (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. **Sampled signal becomes periodic in frequency:** Sampling replicates the spectrum $X(f)$ at multiples of $f_s$.
2. **Spectrum overlap:** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas **overlap** in the baseband.
3. **Information loss:** Once overlap occurs, the original high-frequency content cannot be separated from the low-frequency content, regardless of any post-processing filter.
4. **Irreversibility:** The damage is **permanent** ŌĆö no digital filter can fix aliased data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** In audio CD systems, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, capturing human hearing up to ~$20$ kHz. In digital telephony, $f_s = 8$ kHz is sufficient for voice (limited to ~$3.4$ kHz). Always budget: $f_s \geq 2.2 \times f_m$ (slight over-sampling margin) in real designs.

---

## 2.2 Z-Transform ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Complex Plane View
The variable $z$ is complex, $z = re^{j\omega}$, so:

$$z^{-n} = r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

This splits $z^{-n}$ into:
- $r^{-n}$: **damping/growth** factor (radial behavior)
- $e^{-j\omega n}$: **oscillation** factor (angular behavior)

### 2.2.2 Region of Convergence (ROC)
The set of all $z$ values in the complex plane for which the Z-Transform sum **converges** (i.e., $\sum \vert x(n) z^{-n} \vert < \infty$) is called the ROC.

**Properties of ROC (KTU High-Yield):**
1. The ROC is a **ring** in the z-plane: $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$
2. The ROC **does not include** any poles of $X(z)$
3. If $x(n)$ is **right-sided** (causal), ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_1$ (exterior of a circle)
4. If $x(n)$ is **left-sided** (anti-causal), ROC is $\vert z \vert < r_2$ (interior of a circle)
5. If $x(n)$ is **two-sided**, ROC is an **annulus** $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$
6. If $x(n)$ is **finite-duration** and causal, ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0$ (entire z-plane except $z=0$)
7. Stability requires the ROC to **include the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$)

### 2.2.3 Poles and Zeros ŌĆö Intuition
- **Poles** ($X(z) = \infty$): Values of $z$ that **blow up** $X(z)$. They represent "natural" growth/decay/oscillation patterns of the signal.
- **Zeros** ($X(z) = 0$): Values of $z$ that **cancel out** $X(z)$. They represent frequencies the signal explicitly does not contain.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table 2.1: Standard Z-Transform Pairs (KTU Essential)

| $x(n)$ | $X(z)$ | ROC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\delta(n)$ (unit impulse) | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u(n)$ (unit step) | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^{n} u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $-a^{n} u(-n-1)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| $n a^{n} u(n)$ | $\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^{2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1 - \cos(\omega_0) z^{-1}}{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{\sin(\omega_0) z^{-1}}{1 - 2\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1 - a\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1}}{1 - 2a\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + a^{2} z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $a^{n} \sin(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{a\sin(\omega_0) z^{-1}}{1 - 2a\cos(\omega_0) z^{-1} + a^{2} z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |

### Table 2.2: Essential Z-Transform Properties (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Time Domain $x(n)$ | Z-Domain $X(z)$ | ROC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linearity** | $a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | At least $\text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x(n - n_0)$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | ROC unchanged (except possible $z=0, \infty$) |
| **Time Reversal** | $x(-n)$ | $X(1/z)$ | Inverted: $1/r_1 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_2$ |
| **Multiplication by $a^n$** | $a^{n} x(n)$ | $X(z/a)$ | Scaled: $\vert a \vert r_1 < \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert r_2$ |
| **Multiplication by $n$** | $n x(n)$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | ROC unchanged (except $z=0$) |
| **Convolution** | $x_1(n) * x_2(n)$ | $X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | At least $\text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ |
| **Correlation** | $x_1(n) \star x_2(n)$ | $X_1(z) X_2(1/z)$ | $1/r_{2,1} < \vert z \vert < 1/r_{1,2}$ |
| **Initial Value** | $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | ŌĆö | For causal $x(n)$ |
| **Final Value** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ | ŌĆö | ROC includes $z=1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Z-Transform:** The Z-Transform is the workhorse of **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**. It is used in designing **digital filters (FIR/IIR)**, solving **difference equations**, analyzing **discrete-time control systems**, and implementing **speech/audio codecs** like MP3 and AAC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Standard Z-Transforms

### Derivation 1: Z-Transform of $x(n) = a^{n} u(n)$ (Right-Sided Exponential)

Starting from the definition (causal, $n \geq 0$):

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} u(n) \, z^{-n}$$

Since $u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise, the sum becomes:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^{n}$$

This is an **infinite geometric series** with first term $1$ and common ratio $r = a z^{-1}$. The series converges only if $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

Applying the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z$:

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Pole-Zero Pattern:** One pole at $z = a$, one zero at $z = 0$ (when expressed in $z^{-1}$ form, no zero).

---

### Derivation 2: Z-Transform of $x(n) = -a^{n} u(-n-1)$ (Left-Sided Exponential)

This is a left-sided sequence (non-zero for $n \leq -1$). Using the bilateral definition:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} -a^{n} z^{-n}$$

Substitute $m = -n$ (so $n = -m$, and as $n$ goes from $-\infty$ to $-1$, $m$ goes from $\infty$ to $1$):

$$X(z) = -\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} a^{-m} z^{m} = -\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (a^{-1} z)^{m}$$

This is a geometric series with first term $a^{-1} z$ and ratio $a^{-1} z$. Converges when $\vert a^{-1} z \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$:

$$X(z) = -\frac{a^{-1} z}{1 - a^{-1} z}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $a z^{-1}$:

$$X(z) = -\frac{1}{a z^{-1} - 1} = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert}$$

**Critical Insight:** The Z-domain expression is **identical** to Derivation 1, but the ROC is the **opposite** region. This shows why **ROC is essential** to uniquely determine the time-domain signal!

---

### Derivation 3: Z-Transform of $x(n) = n a^{n} u(n)$

Starting from the known result $a^{n} u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, apply the **multiplication by $n$ property**:

$$n x(n) \leftrightarrow -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

Computing the derivative:

$$-z \frac{d}{dz}\left[ \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \right] = -z \cdot \frac{a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

This result reveals the squared denominator characteristic of $n$-multiplied exponentials.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivations: Aliasing Math

### Derivation 4: The Aliasing Frequency Formula

Consider sampling $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f t)$ with sampling frequency $f_s$. The discrete-time signal is:

$$x(n) = \cos(2\pi f n T_s) = \cos\left(2\pi n \frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$

Now sample another sinusoid at frequency $f' = f + k f_s$ (for integer $k$):

$$x'(n) = \cos\left(2\pi n \frac{f + k f_s}{f_s}\right) = \cos\left(2\pi n \frac{f}{f_s} + 2\pi n k\right)$$

Since $\cos(\theta + 2\pi n k) = \cos(\theta)$ for integer $k$ and integer $n$:

$$x'(n) = \cos\left(2\pi n \frac{f}{f_s}\right) = x(n)$$

**Conclusion:** Frequencies separated by integer multiples of $f_s$ are **indistinguishable** in the sampled signal ŌĆö this is the mathematical definition of aliasing.

**Aliasing Frequency (folded into baseband):**

$$f_{alias} = \left| f - f_s \cdot \text{round}\left( \frac{f}{f_s} \right) \right|$$

For $0 < f < f_s/2$: $f_{alias} = f$ (no aliasing ŌĆö proper sampling)
For $f_s/2 < f < f_s$: $f_{alias} = f_s - f$ (folded)
For $f_s < f < 3 f_s/2$: $f_{alias} = f - f_s$ (folded back)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Verifying the Sampling Theorem

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(200\pi t) + 3\sin(400\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 300$ Hz. Determine if aliasing occurs. If yes, find the aliased frequencies.

**Step 1: Identify signal frequencies.**
- $f_1 = 200\pi / (2\pi) = 100$ Hz
- $f_2 = 400\pi / (2\pi) = 200$ Hz
- Maximum frequency $f_m = 200$ Hz

**Step 2: Compute Nyquist rate.**
$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Compare.**
$$f_s = 300 \text{ Hz} < 400 \text{ Hz} = f_{Nyquist}$$

**Aliasing occurs!**

**Step 4: Find the aliased frequencies.**

For $f_1 = 100$ Hz: $f_1 < f_s/2 = 150$ Hz, so $f_{1,alias} = 100$ Hz (no aliasing)

For $f_2 = 200$ Hz: $f_2 > 150$ Hz, so use $f_{2,alias} = f_s - f_2 = 300 - 200 = 100$ Hz

**Aliased signal:**

$$x_{alias}(n) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot n / 300) + 3\sin(2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot n / 300)$$

$$x_{alias}(n) = 5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{3}\right) + 3\sin\left(\frac{2\pi n}{3}\right)$$

Both original distinct frequencies (100 Hz and 200 Hz) become indistinguishable at the same aliased frequency 100 Hz ŌĆö **catastrophic information loss**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Z-Transform via Long Division

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Find the Z-transform of $x(n) = \{1, 2, 5, 0, 1\}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 0$.

**Step 1:** Write the bilateral sum directly:

$$X(z) = 1 \cdot z^{0} + 2 z^{-1} + 5 z^{-2} + 0 \cdot z^{-3} + 1 \cdot z^{-4}$$

$$X(z) = 1 + 2 z^{-1} + 5 z^{-2} + z^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Identify poles and zeros. As a finite sum of $z^{-1}$ powers, this is a **polynomial in $z^{-1}$** with no poles (except possibly at $z=0$). The zeros are the roots of $X(z) = 0$ in the $z$-plane.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete signal chain** for a sampled-data system, showing *where* aliasing occurs and *how* the anti-aliasing filter prevents it.

```mermaid
    F --> G[Analog Output ya of t]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define aliasing. State the Nyquist criterion for sampling and explain why it must be satisfied.**

**Model Answer:**
Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate lower than twice its highest frequency component, becomes indistinguishable from another signal of lower frequency. The high-frequency content "folds back" into the lower frequency band, causing irreversible distortion.

The **Nyquist criterion** states that to avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
where $f_m$ is the highest frequency present in the signal. The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_m$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

It must be satisfied because if $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of the sampled signal overlap in the frequency domain, and no reconstruction filter can recover the original signal.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the Z-transform of a discrete-time signal. What is the Region of Convergence (ROC)?**

**Model Answer:**
The **bilateral Z-transform** of a discrete-time signal $x(n)$ is defined as:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$$
where $z$ is a complex variable.

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the set of all values of $z$ in the complex plane for which the Z-transform sum converges to a finite value, i.e., $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) z^{-n} \vert < \infty$.

The ROC is essential because two different sequences can have the same $X(z)$ expression but different ROCs. The ROC uniquely determines which time-domain signal corresponds to a given $X(z)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A signal $x(t) = 3\cos(100\pi t) + 2\sin(300\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 250$ Hz.
(i) Determine whether aliasing occurs. (ii) If yes, find the discrete-time signal obtained after sampling. (iii) Find the Nyquist rate for this signal.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the Z-transform and ROC of the following signals:
(i) $x(n) = (0.5)^{n} u(n)$
(ii) $x(n) = -(0.5)^{n} u(-n-1)$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify component frequencies.**
- $f_1 = 100\pi / (2\pi) = 50$ Hz
- $f_2 = 300\pi / (2\pi) = 150$ Hz
- Maximum frequency: $f_m = 150$ Hz

**Step 2: Nyquist rate.**
$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Aliasing check.**
$f_s = 250$ Hz $< 300$ Hz $= f_{Nyquist}$. **Aliasing occurs.**

**Step 4: Aliased frequencies.**
- $f_1 = 50$ Hz $< f_s/2 = 125$ Hz ŌåÆ **no aliasing** for this component: $f_{1,alias} = 50$ Hz
- $f_2 = 150$ Hz $> 125$ Hz ŌåÆ **aliased**: $f_{2,alias} = f_s - f_2 = 250 - 150 = 100$ Hz

**Step 5: Sampled (discrete-time) signal.**
$$x(n) = 3\cos\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot n}{250}\right) + 2\sin\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot 100 \cdot n}{250}\right)$$

$$x(n) = 3\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{5}\right) + 2\sin\left(\frac{4\pi n}{5}\right)$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) $x(n) = (0.5)^{n} u(n)$** (right-sided, causal)

By the standard pair (Table 2.1) with $a = 0.5$:

$$X_1(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 0.5}$$

ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ (exterior of pole at $z = 0.5$)

Pole: $z = 0.5$ (inside unit circle ŌåÆ stable), Zero: $z = 0$

**(ii) $x(n) = -(0.5)^{n} u(-n-1)$** (left-sided, anti-causal)

By Derivation 2 from Section 3.1, with $a = 0.5$:

$$X_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 0.5}$$

ROC: $\vert z \vert < 0.5$ (interior of pole at $z = 0.5$)

**Critical observation:** The algebraic form of $X_1(z)$ and $X_2(z)$ is **identical**, but the ROC differs. Without specifying ROC, the time-domain signal is **ambiguous**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of aliasing with a suitable diagram. A bandlimited signal has maximum frequency $5$ kHz. Calculate the minimum sampling rate. If the signal is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz, determine the range of input frequencies that will alias to the baseband range $0$ to $f_s/2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Z-transform properties, find the Z-transform of:
$$x(n) = n (0.8)^{n} u(n)$$

Show all intermediate steps clearly.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Aliasing Explanation:**
Aliasing is the distortion that occurs when a continuous-time signal is sampled at a rate insufficient to capture its high-frequency content. After sampling, frequencies higher than $f_s/2$ "fold back" into the lower frequency band, masquerading as legitimate low-frequency components.

*(Refer to the spectrum replication diagram in Section 4.2 for visual aid.)*

**Minimum Sampling Rate:**
$$f_{min} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

This is the **Nyquist rate** for a $5$ kHz bandlimited signal.

**Range of Aliasing when $f_s = 8$ kHz:**

Nyquist frequency $= f_s/2 = 4$ kHz.

Any frequency $f$ in the range $4 \text{ kHz} < f < 8$ kHz will alias to $f' = f_s - f$, which lies in $0$ to $4$ kHz.

More generally, frequencies in $[4, 12)$ kHz, $[12, 20)$ kHz, etc., will all fold into $[0, 4]$ kHz.

**Specifically:** An input tone at $5$ kHz aliases to $f' = 8 - 5 = 3$ kHz. An input tone at $6$ kHz aliases to $f' = 8 - 6 = 2$ kHz.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** $x(n) = n (0.8)^{n} u(n)$

**Step 1: Identify known transform pair.**
From Table 2.1: $(0.8)^{n} u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.8$

**Step 2: Apply the multiplication-by-$n$ property.**
$$n x(n) \leftrightarrow -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the derivative.**
$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[ \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \right] = \frac{0.8 z^{-2}}{(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 4: Apply the $-z \frac{d}{dz}$ operator.**
$$-z \cdot \frac{0.8 z^{-2}}{(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-0.8 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 5: Final answer.**
$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{0.8 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})^2} = \frac{0.8 z}{(z - 0.8)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8}$$

**Pole-Zero Analysis:**
- Double pole at $z = 0.8$ (inside unit circle, multiplicity 2)
- Zero at $z = 0$ (multiplicity 1)
- ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.8$, which **includes the unit circle** ŌåÆ the system is **stable**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC:** Many students write $X(z)$ but omit the ROC. In KTU valuation, **ROC carries 1ŌĆō2 marks** out of the 7 in part (b). Always specify ROC explicitly.
> 2. **Sign errors in aliasing:** When computing the aliased frequency, students often write $f_{alias} = f_s + f$ (wrong) instead of $f_s - f$ (correct for $f_s/2 < f < f_s$). **Memorize:** positive frequencies fold back via *subtraction* from $f_s$.
> 3. **Conflating DTFT and Z-transform:** Some students evaluate $X(z)$ on the unit circle without realizing this *restricts* the ROC. Always check whether the unit circle lies within the ROC before claiming DTFT convergence.
> 4. **Pole multiplicities:** When applying the $n \cdot$ property, the pole becomes a *double pole*. Don't simplify $(z - 0.8)^2$ ŌĆö keep the squared factor to show the multiplicity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Aliasing = high frequencies masquerading as low frequencies** after insufficient sampling.
- [ ] **Nyquist Rate = $2 f_m$** is the *minimum* safe sampling frequency; Nyquist Frequency = $f_s/2$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** is a *low-pass analog* filter placed *before* the sampler, not after.
- [ ] Aliasing is **irreversible** ŌĆö once it occurs, no digital filter can recover the original signal.
- [ ] **Z-transform definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$ (bilateral).
- [ ] **ROC = ring** in z-plane: $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$; ROC **never includes poles**.
- [ ] **Causal signal** ŌåÆ ROC is *outside* the outermost pole.
- [ ] **Anti-causal signal** ŌåÆ ROC is *inside* the innermost pole.
- [ ] **Stability** requires the ROC to *include the unit circle* $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- [ ] **Time shifting:** $x(n - n_0) \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$ ŌĆö introduces a factor of $z^{-n_0}$.
- [ ] **Convolution in time** = **multiplication in Z-domain** ŌĆö the foundation of LSI system analysis.
- [ ] **Initial value theorem:** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ (for causal $x(n)$).
- [ ] **Final value theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ (valid only if ROC includes $z = 1$).
- [ ] **Same $X(z)$, different ROCs** = **different time-domain signals** ŌĆö ROC is mandatory for uniqueness.
- [ ] **Pole inside unit circle** Ōåö **decaying exponential** in time domain; pole outside Ōåö **growing** exponential.
- [ ] **Pole on the unit circle** Ōåö **constant-amplitude oscillation** (sustained sinusoid).
- [ ] **Memorize Table 2.1** (standard pairs) and **Table 2.2** (properties) ŌĆö these are KTU exam gold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 ŌĆö INVERSE Z TRANSFORM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Inverse Z Transform** is the mathematical procedure used to recover a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation $X(z)$. It is the reverse operation of the Z-transform, and is formally defined by the **Bromwich Contour Integral** (also called the Complex Inversion Integral):

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{\,n-1}\, dz$$

where the contour $C$ is a closed path encircling the origin and lying entirely within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$, taken in the counter-clockwise (positive) direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The inverse Z-transform is *not* unique. A given $X(z)$ can correspond to many possible $x[n]$ sequences ŌĆö the **ROC uniquely identifies the correct sequence** for a causal, anti-causal, or two-sided signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Z-transform as a **smoothie blender**.
- The **forward Z-transform** takes whole fruits (time-domain samples $x[n]$) and blends them into a single smoothie $X(z)$ ŌĆö you can no longer see the individual fruit pieces.
- The **inverse Z-transform** is the chef's job ŌĆö using a sieve, a recipe book (the **ROC**), and a filter, the chef must reconstruct which fruits were originally used, and in what order.

The chef (mathematician) has four popular recipes (methods) to choose from: **Long Division, Partial Fractions, Residue Theorem, and the Power-Series method** ŌĆö each suitable for different "fruit types" (rational, non-rational, or difference-equation forms of $X(z)$).

### 1.3 Why is the ROC So Important?

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point ŌĆö Examiner's Favourite:**
> Different ROC's for the *same* algebraic $X(z)$ produce *different* $x[n]$:
>
> | $X(z)$ | ROC | Resulting $x[n]$ |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | $\frac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | $a^n u[n]$ (right-sided, causal) |
> | $\frac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | $-a^n u[-n-1]$ (left-sided, anti-causal) |
>
> So the ROC is the **identity card** of the sequence.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Sampling Rate:** $F_s$ in **Hz** or $T = 1/F_s$ in **seconds**.
- **Unit Circle:** $\vert z \vert = 1$ ŌĆö corresponds to $s = j\omega$ in the s-plane.
- **Pole radius:** $\vert p_k \vert$ ŌĆö defines stability boundary.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard Methods of Inversion

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly prescribes the following four methods (Module 2). Each is best suited to a particular form of $X(z)$:

1. **Long Division (Power Series Expansion Method)**
2. **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method**
3. **Residue Method (Contour Integration / Cauchy's Residue Theorem)**
4. **Difference Equation Method (Realization / Iterative)**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below. Every KTU Part-B question uses at least one of these identities.**

| # | Forward Z-Transform Pair $x[n]$ | $X(z)$ | ROC |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | All $z$ |
| 2 | $u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 3 | $-u[-n-1]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ |
| 4 | $a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 5 | $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| 6 | $n\,a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{az^{-1}}{(1-az^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 7 | $\cos(\omega_0 n)\,u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1-z^{-1}\cos\omega_0}{1-2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 8 | $\sin(\omega_0 n)\,u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^{-1}\sin\omega_0}{1-2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 9 | $r^n \cos(\omega_0 n)\,u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1-rz^{-1}\cos\omega_0}{1-2rz^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > r$ |
| 10 | $r^n \sin(\omega_0 n)\,u[n]$ | $\dfrac{rz^{-1}\sin\omega_0}{1-2rz^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + r^2 z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > r$ |

### 2.3 Method 1 ŌĆö Long Division (Power Series)

**When to use:** $X(z)$ is *non-rational* (e.g., $\log$, $\sinh^{-1}$, transcendental) or when only the first few samples are required.

**Procedure:**
1. Re-express $X(z)$ as a rational function in $z^{-1}$ (negative powers).
2. Perform long division of numerator by denominator.
3. The coefficients of $z^0, z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots$ give $x[0], x[1], x[2], \ldots$ respectively.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If the ROC is $\vert z \vert > a$ (outside), divide so that the series begins with $z^0$ (causal sequence). If ROC is $\vert z \vert < a$ (inside), invert numerator & denominator first to get a series in $z^{+1}, z^{+2}, \ldots$ for the *anti-causal* sequence.

### 2.4 Method 2 ŌĆö Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)

**When to use:** $X(z)$ is a *proper rational function* with distinct or repeated real poles.

**Procedure (for $X(z)$ in $z^{-1}$):**
1. Convert to $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$ form.
2. Do PFE on $\dfrac{X(z)}{z}$.
3. Multiply each term by $z$ back.
4. Match each term to the standard table.

For distinct poles:
$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{z - p_k}$$

where $R_k = \left. (z - p_k)\dfrac{X(z)}{z} \right|_{z=p_k}$.

For repeated poles of order $m$:
$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{A_k}{(z - p_i)^k} + \cdots$$

### 2.5 Method 3 ŌĆö Residue Method (Contour Integration)

**When to use:** $X(z)$ has *complex poles* (e.g., $p_k = re^{\pm j\omega_0}$) ŌĆö preferred for sinusoidal terms.

**Residue Theorem:**
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{\,n-1}\, dz = \sum \text{Residues of } X(z)z^{n-1} \text{ at poles inside } C$$

**For simple poles $p_k$:**
$$\text{Res}_{z=p_k} \left[ X(z) z^{n-1} \right] = \left. (z - p_k)\, X(z)\, z^{n-1} \right|_{z=p_k}$$

**For repeated pole of order $m$ at $p_k$:**
$$\text{Res}_{z=p_k} = \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z \to p_k} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} \left[ (z-p_k)^m X(z) z^{n-1} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ROC Drives Pole Selection:** Sum residues only at poles *inside* the contour $C$. The contour is closed depending on whether $n \geq 0$ or $n < 0$. This automatically gives a causal/anti-causal answer.

### 2.6 Method 4 ŌĆö Difference Equation Method (Iterative)

**When to use:** $X(z)$ is given in a recursive/difference-equation form:
$$X(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$

**Procedure:** Cross-multiply and take the inverse Z-transform term-by-term using the time-shift property:
$$z^{-k} X(z) \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\;}\; x[n-k]$$

This yields a linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE) that can be solved iteratively (assuming $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ in causal case).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** Given a transfer function $H(z)$, computing $h[n]$ lets you implement the filter as a tapped-delay line.
- **System Stability Analysis:** If ROC of $H(z)$ includes the unit circle, the LTI system is **BIBO stable** ŌĆö critical in DSP chips, audio codecs, and control systems.
- **Spectral Estimation:** Inverse Z-transform is the discrete analogue of the inverse Laplace transform used in radar, biomedical signal processing (ECG, EEG), and OFDM receivers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A ŌĆö Long Division Method

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Solution:**

Since ROC is *outside* $0.5$, the sequence is **right-sided (causal)**. We expand $X(z)$ as a power series in $z^{-1}$ by long division.

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \\
1 \div (1 - 0.5z^{-1}) &= 1 + 0.5z^{-1} + 0.25z^{-2} + 0.125 z^{-3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step long division:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\phantom{(1-0.5z^{-1})\;}\;\; 1 + 0.5z^{-1} + 0.25z^{-2} + \cdots \\
&\quad(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;\overline{\smash{)}\,1\,} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\underline{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\quad\;\;0.5 z^{-1} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\underline{0.5z^{-1} - 0.25z^{-2}} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\quad\;\;\;0.25 z^{-2} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\underline{0.25z^{-2} - 0.125z^{-3}} \\
&\phantom{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})\;}\quad\;\;\;\;\;0.125z^{-3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

**Identifying coefficients:**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | $\cdots$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $x[n]$ | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0.25$ | $0.125$ | $0.0625$ | $\cdots$ |

**General closed-form result (matching the table):**
$$x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B ŌĆö Partial Fraction Expansion (Distinct Real Poles)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express $X(z)/z$ in standard form.**

$$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 2)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)(z-2)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Partial Fraction Expansion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{X(z)}{z} &= \frac{A}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B}{z - 2}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for residues:
$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \left. \frac{z}{z - 2} \right|_{z=0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.5 - 2} = \frac{0.5}{-1.5} = -\frac{1}{3} \\[4pt]
B &= \left. \frac{z}{z - 0.5} \right|_{z=2} = \frac{2}{2 - 0.5} = \frac{2}{1.5} = \frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply back by $z$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{-\tfrac{1}{3}\,z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{\tfrac{4}{3}\,z}{z - 2} \\[4pt]
X(z) &= -\frac{1}{3}\cdot \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{4}{3}\cdot \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply inverse Z-transform (with ROC $\vert z \vert > 2$ ŌćÆ both terms causal):**

$$x[n] = -\frac{1}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] + \frac{4}{3}(2)^n u[n]$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C ŌĆö Residue Method (Complex Poles)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the poles.**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2} &= 0 \\
\text{Multiply by } z^2: \quad z^2 - 0.8z + 0.64 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Discriminant $= (-0.8)^2 - 4(0.64) = 0.64 - 2.56 < 0$, so the poles are complex conjugates.

$$\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{0.8 \pm j\sqrt{2.56 - 0.64}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \pm j\sqrt{1.92}}{2} \\
z &= 0.4 \pm j\,0.6928 \\
r &= \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.6928^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.48} = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8 \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.6928}{0.4}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.732) = \frac{\pi}{3} = 60^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

So poles are $p_{1,2} = 0.8 e^{\pm j\pi/3}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express in standard form using the sin/cos table.**

Comparing with $\dfrac{1 - rz^{-1}\cos\theta}{1 - 2rz^{-1}\cos\theta + r^2 z^{-2}}$, we have $r = 0.8$, $\cos\theta = 0.5$. Then:

$$2r\cos\theta = 1.6 \cdot 0.5 = 0.8 \;\checkmark \quad r^2 = 0.64 \;\checkmark$$

The numerator is $1$, but the standard form requires $1 - rz^{-1}\cos\theta = 1 - 0.4 z^{-1}$. So we need to **add and subtract**:

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{1 - 0.4z^{-1} + 0.4z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - 0.4z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}} + \frac{0.4z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Match each term to the standard table.**

Using $r = 0.8$, $\sin\theta = \sin 60^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1 - rz^{-1}\cos\theta}{1 - 2rz^{-1}\cos\theta + r^2 z^{-2}} &\;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\;}\; r^n \cos(\omega_0 n)\, u[n] \\[4pt]
\frac{rz^{-1}\sin\theta}{1 - 2rz^{-1}\cos\theta + r^2 z^{-2}} &\;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\;}\; r^n \sin(\omega_0 n)\, u[n]
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging our $X(z)$ to match these:

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \underbrace{\frac{1 - 0.4z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}}}_{\text{cosine term}} + \frac{0.4}{0.8 \cdot 0.866}\cdot\underbrace{\frac{0.8 \cdot 0.866\,z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}}}_{\text{sine term}} \\[4pt]
X(z) &= (0.8)^n \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u[n] + \frac{0.4}{0.6928}\cdot (0.8)^n \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u[n] \\[4pt]
X(z) &= (0.8)^n \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u[n] + 0.5774 \cdot (0.8)^n \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u[n]
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$x[n] = (0.8)^n \left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) + 0.577\,\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right)\right] u[n]$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Complex Inversion Integral

Starting from the forward Z-transform:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

Multiply both sides by $z^{k-1}$ and integrate around a closed contour $C$ inside the ROC:

$$\begin{aligned}
\oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{k-1}\, dz &= \oint_{C} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{k-1-n}\, dz \\
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \oint_{C} z^{k-1-n}\, dz
\end{aligned}$$

By **Cauchy's Integral Theorem**:
$$\oint_{C} z^{m-1}\, dz = \begin{cases} 2\pi j, & m = 0 \\ 0, & m \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

This integral equals $2\pi j$ only when $k - 1 - n = 0$, i.e., $n = k$. So:

$$\oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{k-1}\, dz = 2\pi j\, x[k]$$

Rearranging:
$$x[k] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{k-1}\, dz$$

Replacing dummy index $k$ with $n$:
$$\boxed{\,x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z)\, z^{n-1}\, dz\,}$$

This is the formal definition of the inverse Z-transform used at the start of Section 1. The contour $C$ is **counter-clockwise** and must lie entirely within the ROC.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Method

| Form of $X(z)$ | Best Method | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Non-rational ($\log$, $\cosh^{-1}$, $\sin^{-1}$) | **Long Division** | Closed-form PFE impossible |
| Rational, distinct real poles | **PFE** | Fastest for causal/anti-causal |
| Rational, complex conjugate poles | **Residue** | Gives $\sin$/$\cos$ terms directly |
| Rational, repeated poles | **Residue + Derivatives** | PFE requires $A_k$ for each order |
| Given as difference equation | **Iterative / Difference Eq.** | Avoids algebra entirely |
| Only need first few samples | **Long Division** | Truncates after $N$ terms |

---


## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Inverse Z-Transform Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["X z rational form"]:::input
    PRE["Pre-processing: Convert to z or z inv form, identify ROC, list poles"]:::process
    CHK["Validity Check: Does ROC include unit circle? If yes system is BIBO stable"]:::decision
    PFE["Partial Fraction Engine: Decompose X z over z into sum of first-order terms"]:::process
    RES["Residue Engine: Apply Cauchy theorem, sum residues at enclosed poles"]:::process
    MAP["Lookup Table Mapping: Match each term to standard pair with ROC check"]:::process
    SUM["Summation Block: Combine all inverse-transformed sub-sequences"]:::process
    OUT["x n final time-domain sequence"]:::output

    IN --> PRE --> CHK
    CHK -- Causal ROC outside --> PFE
    CHK -- Anti-causal ROC inside --> RES
    PFE --> MAP
    RES --> MAP
    MAP --> SUM --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f3864,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Pole p1 inside unit circle"]:::stable
    P2["Pole p2 outside unit circle"]:::unstable
    P3["Pole p3 ON unit circle"]:::marginally
    ROCA["ROC: outside outermost pole: includes unit circle if no pole outside"]:::roclab
    ROCB["ROC: bounded between two poles: excludes unit circle"]:::roclab
    ROCC["ROC: bounded up to unit circle: marginally stable, not BIBO"]:::roclab
    SA["x n causal, system is STABLE"]:::safelab
    SB["x n two-sided, system UNSTABLE"]:::unsafelab
    SC["x n right-sided, system MARGINALLY UNSTABLE"]:::unsafelab

    P1 --> ROCA --> SA
    P2 --> ROCB --> SB
    P3 --> ROCC --> SC
```
