# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 ├Ś 3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` ŌĆö 3 Marks
**State the conditions under which the inverse Z-transform of $X(z)$ is unique. Why is the Region of Convergence important in determining $x[n]$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Uniqueness (1 mark):** The inverse Z-transform is unique *only when the ROC is specified* along with $X(z)$.
- **ROC determines causality (1 mark):** For a given rational $X(z)$, different ROC's yield different sequences:
  - $\vert z \vert > r$ ŌćÆ right-sided (causal) sequence.
  - $\vert z \vert < r$ ŌćÆ left-sided (anti-causal) sequence.
  - $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ ŌćÆ two-sided sequence.
- **ROC determines stability (1 mark):** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable **iff** the ROC of $H(z)$ includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ŌĆö 3 Marks
**List any four methods used to find the inverse Z-transform of a given $X(z)$. For what type of $X(z)$ is the long-division method preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Four methods (2 marks):**
  1. Long Division (Power Series Expansion)
  2. Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)
  3. Residue Method (Contour Integration)
  4. Difference Equation Method (Iterative)
- **Long division preferred for (1 mark):** Non-rational functions of $z$ (e.g., $\log(1+az^{-1})$, $\sin^{-1}(z^{-1})$, $\cosh^{-1}$) or when only the first few samples are required.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions ŌĆö 14-Mark ESE Module Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always specify the ROC** in your final answer ŌĆö losing 1ŌĆō2 marks is common if you omit it.
> - For PFE, the **standard form is $X(z)/z$**, not $X(z)$ ŌĆö students often skip the $z$ and end up with shifted answers.
> - In residue method, you must **close the contour correctly for $n \geq 0$ and $n < 0$** ŌĆö this determines causal vs anti-causal.
> - Always show the **substitution of $z = p_k$** in the residue formula ŌĆö partial credit requires visible steps.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Determine the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$
using the **Partial Fraction Expansion Method**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Find the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.6$$
using the **Residue Method** for a repeated pole. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Step 1:** Convert to $X(z)/z$ form.

$$X(z) = \frac{z^2 + 2z}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z + 2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)}$$

**Step 2:** Partial fraction:

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B}{z - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Find residues.

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \left.\frac{z+2}{z - 1}\right|_{z=0.5} = \frac{2.5}{-0.5} = -5 \\[4pt]
B &= \left.\frac{z+2}{z - 0.5}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{3}{0.5} = 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $z$ and match.

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{-5z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{6z}{z - 1} \\[4pt]
X(z) &= -5\cdot\frac{1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + 6\cdot\frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$ (causal), both terms are right-sided.

$$\boxed{\,x[n] = -5(0.5)^n u[n] + 6\,u[n]\,}$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1:** Express in $z$-form.

$$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.6)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inverse via the residue method. The pole $p = 0.6$ is of order $m = 2$.

For $n \geq 0$, close the contour to *include* the pole (since $|0.6|^n < \infty$ for the inner term):

$$x[n] = \text{Res}_{z=0.6} \left[ \frac{z^2}{(z-0.6)^2}\cdot z^{n-1} \right] = \text{Res}_{z=0.6} \left[ \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z-0.6)^2} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the residue formula for a pole of order 2.

$$\text{Res}_{z=p} = \frac{1}{(2-1)!}\lim_{z \to p}\frac{d}{dz}\left[(z-p)^2 f(z)\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \lim_{z \to 0.6} \frac{d}{dz}\left[ z^{n+1} \right] \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{z \to 0.6} (n+1) z^{n} \\[4pt]
&= (n+1)(0.6)^n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Since the ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.6$ implies a right-sided sequence, multiply by $u[n]$:

$$\boxed{\,x[n] = (n+1)(0.6)^n u[n]\,}$$

This matches the standard pair #6 from the formula sheet (with $a = 0.6$, plus a shift of $n \to n+1$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Use the **Long Division Method** to find the first four samples of $x[n]$ for
$$X(z) = \frac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.1 z^{-2}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Determine the inverse Z-transform of
$$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4})(z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$
using **PFE** and express $x[n]$ in real sinusoidal form. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Step 1:** Long division of $1 + 0.4z^{-1}$ by $1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}$.

**Step 2:** Set up the division:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\phantom{)}\; 1 + 1.1 z^{-1} + 0.67 z^{-2} + 0.379 z^{-3} + \cdots \\
&\overline{(1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2})\;)\;1 + 0.4 z^{-1}} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\underline{1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\quad\;\;1.1 z^{-1} - 0.1 z^{-2} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\underline{1.1 z^{-1} - 0.77 z^{-2} + 0.11 z^{-3}} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\quad\;\;\;0.67 z^{-2} - 0.11 z^{-3} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\underline{0.67 z^{-2} - 0.469 z^{-3} + 0.067 z^{-4}} \\
&\phantom{)1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}\;}\quad\;\;\;\;\;0.379 z^{-3} - 0.067 z^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Read off samples.

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $x[n]$ | $1$ | $1.1$ | $0.67$ | $0.379$ |

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1:** $X(z)/z$ in standard form.

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4})(z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4})}$$

**Step 2:** PFE.

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4}} + \frac{A^*}{z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $A$.

$$A = \left. \frac{z}{z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}} \right|_{z=0.8 e^{j\pi/4}} = \frac{0.8 e^{j\pi/4}}{0.8 e^{j\pi/4} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}} = \frac{e^{j\pi/4}}{e^{j\pi/4} - e^{-j\pi/4}}$$

Using $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:

$$A = \frac{e^{j\pi/4}}{2j\sin(\pi/4)} = \frac{e^{j\pi/4}}{j\sqrt{2}} = \frac{(\cos 45^\circ + j\sin 45^\circ)}{j\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 + j)}{j\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1 + j}{2j}$$

Rationalize:

$$A = \frac{(1+j)(-j)}{2j(-j)} = \frac{-j - j^2}{2} = \frac{1 - j}{2}$$

So $|A| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\angle A = -\pi/4$.

**Step 4:** Multiply by $z$ back and use the causal pair.

$$X(z) = A\cdot\frac{z}{z - 0.8 e^{j\pi/4}} + A^*\cdot\frac{z}{z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/4}}$$

**Step 5:** Combine into a real form.

For complex-conjugate pole pair $r e^{\pm j\theta}$ with residue magnitude $|A|$ and phase $\phi$:
$$x[n] = 2|A|\, r^n \cos(n\theta + \phi)\, u[n]$$

Here $r = 0.8$, $\theta = \pi/4$, $|A| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, $\phi = -\pi/4$:

$$\boxed{\,x[n] = \sqrt{2}\cdot (0.8)^n \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4}n - \tfrac{\pi}{4}\right) u[n]\,}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Inverse Z Transform:**

- [ ] **Always state the ROC** alongside $X(z)$ ŌĆö it uniquely determines $x[n]$ and signal stability.
- [ ] **Causal sequence** ŌćÆ ROC is *outside* the outermost pole.
- [ ] **Anti-causal sequence** ŌćÆ ROC is *inside* the innermost pole.
- [ ] **Two-sided sequence** ŌćÆ ROC is an annulus (ring) between two poles.
- [ ] **Stability of an LTI system** Ōćö ROC of $H(z)$ *includes* the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- [ ] For **PFE**, always work with $X(z)/z$ first, then multiply by $z$ before using the lookup table.
- [ ] **Long Division** is your *only* option for non-rational $X(z)$ (e.g., $\log$, $\sin^{-1}$, $\cosh^{-1}$).
- [ ] **Residue method** is the cleanest approach for *complex conjugate* poles ŌĆö yields $\sin$/$\cos$ forms directly.
- [ ] For a **repeated pole of order $m$** at $z = p$:
$$\text{Res} = \frac{1}{(m-1)!}\lim_{z\to p}\frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}}\bigl[(z-p)^m X(z) z^{n-1}\bigr]$$
- [ ] For $n < 0$ in residue method, close the contour *outward* (toward $\infty$) and the integral equals $-(2\pi j)\sum$ (residues outside).
- [ ] The difference-equation (iterative) method gives you $x[n]$ one sample at a time, but the **closed form** must still be backed up by PFE/residue for analytical insight.
- [ ] The standard pair $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ (ROC $|z|>|a|$) is the **workhorse identity** ŌĆö memorize it.
- [ ] The pair $n a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{az^{-1}}{(1-az^{-1})^2}$ appears in *every* KTU module test ŌĆö memorize it.
- [ ] Always **verify pole-zero cancellation** ŌĆö if a zero cancels a pole, that pole does *not* contribute to the residue sum.
- [ ] **Time-shift property:** $z^{-k} X(z) \leftrightarrow x[n-k]$ ŌĆö used heavily in the difference-equation method.
- [ ] **Final theorem:** $\displaystyle x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j}\oint_C X(z)\, z^{n-1}\, dz$ ŌĆö define, derive once, and never forget.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a System

A **system** is a mathematical operator that transforms an input signal into an output signal according to a defined rule. In Signals and Systems, the system is the central processing entity that defines the inputŌĆōoutput relationship.

**Continuous-Time (CT) System:**

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$$

**Discrete-Time (DT) System:**

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$

Where:
- $T\{\cdot\}$ is the **system operator** (transformation rule).
- $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ is the **input** (excitation).
- $y(t)$ or $y[n]$ is the **output** (response).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, the "Properties" topic refers to the **fundamental properties of systems** (not signals). These properties classify a system's behavior into distinct, testable categories: **Memory, Invertibility, Causality, Stability, Time-Invariance, and Linearity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Black Box Machine")

Imagine a **vending machine** (the system). You insert a coin (the input signal), press a button, and it dispenses a snack (the output signal). The properties of the system tell us:

- **Memory** ŌĆö Does the machine remember the coins you inserted 5 minutes ago? (If yes ŌåÆ "has memory"; if no ŌåÆ "memoryless".)
- **Causality** ŌĆö Does the machine ever give you a snack BEFORE you insert a coin? (If yes ŌåÆ non-causal; physically impossible in real-time.)
- **Stability** ŌĆö If you keep inserting valid coins forever, will the machine break or behave normally?
- **Time-Invariance** ŌĆö If you use the machine today or tomorrow, does it work identically?
- **Linearity** ŌĆö If you press two buttons simultaneously, is the result the sum of pressing each separately?
- **Invertibility** ŌĆö Given the snack that comes out, can you uniquely determine which coin and button you used?

> [!NOTE]
> A single system **may or may not** possess each property independently. The art of Signals and Systems lies in **testing** whether a given system satisfies each property by formal mathematical proof.

## 1.3 The Six Core System Properties

A summary of the six primary properties tested in KTU examinations:

| **#** | **Property** | **Core Question** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory / Memoryless | Does output at time $t$ depend only on input at time $t$? |
| 2 | Invertibility | Do distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs? |
| 3 | Causality | Does output depend only on present/past inputs? |
| 4 | BIBO Stability | Does every bounded input give a bounded output? |
| 5 | Time-Invariance | Does a time shift in input cause the same shift in output? |
| 6 | Linearity | Does the system obey additivity + homogeneity? |

> [!TIP]
> Always write the system in standard form first: $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ for CT or $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ for DT. Then apply the formal test conditions for each property.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Property 1 ŌĆö Memory and Memoryless Systems

**Definition (CT):** A system is **memoryless** if the output $y(t)$ at any time $t$ depends **only** on the input $x(t)$ at the **same** time $t$.

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{where the rule uses only } x(t), \text{ not } x(t-k) \text{ for any } k \neq 0$$

**Definition (DT):**

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{where the rule uses only } x[n], \text{ not } x[n-k] \text{ for any } k \neq 0$$

**Test Logic:**
- If the input argument contains any shift $x(t-t_0)$ or $x[n-n_0]$ with $t_0 \neq 0$ (or $n_0 \neq 0$), the system **has memory**.
- If the input argument is purely $x(t)$ or $x[n]$, the system is **memoryless**.

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = 5x(t)$ ŌåÆ **memoryless** Ō£ō
- $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-3)$ ŌåÆ **has memory** Ō£Ś
- $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ ŌåÆ **memoryless** Ō£ō
- $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-2]$ ŌåÆ **has memory** Ō£Ś

## 2.2 Property 2 ŌĆö Invertibility

**Definition:** A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs $x_1 \neq x_2$ always produce distinct outputs $y_1 \neq y_2$. The system must be **one-to-one (injective)**.

The **inverse system** is denoted $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ and satisfies:

$$x(t) = T^{-1}\{y(t)\} \quad \text{or} \quad x[n] = T^{-1}\{y[n]\}$$

**Test Logic:**
- If you can solve for $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) uniquely in terms of $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$), the system is **invertible**.
- If the operation loses information (e.g., squaring, absolute value, cosine), the system is **non-invertible**.

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = 3x(t)$ ŌåÆ **invertible**, inverse: $x(t) = y(t)/3$ Ō£ō
- $y(t) = x^2(t)$ ŌåÆ **non-invertible** (since both $x(t)$ and $-x(t)$ give same $y(t)$) Ō£Ś
- $y[n] = x[n-1]$ ŌåÆ **invertible**, inverse: $x[n] = y[n+1]$ Ō£ō
- $y(t) = \cos(x(t))$ ŌåÆ **non-invertible** (cosine is not one-to-one globally) Ō£Ś

## 2.3 Property 3 ŌĆö Causality

**Definition (CT):** A system is **causal** if the output $y(t_0)$ at any time $t_0$ depends only on input values $x(t)$ for $t \leq t_0$ (present and past only, **never future**).

$$y(t_0) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{requires } x(t) \text{ for } t \leq t_0 \text{ only}$$

**Definition (DT):**

$$y[n_0] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{requires } x[n] \text{ for } n \leq n_0 \text{ only}$$

**Test Logic:**
- Inspect the input argument in the system equation.
- If the input has a **positive shift** like $x(t+1)$ or $x[n+2]$, the system requires **future** values ŌåÆ **non-causal**.
- If the input has a **zero or negative shift** like $x(t)$ or $x(t-1)$ or $x[n-2]$, the system is **causal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality is a must for real-time physical systems.** A non-causal system cannot be implemented in real-time because it would need to "know the future."

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-2)$ ŌåÆ **causal** Ō£ō
- $y(t) = x(t+5)$ ŌåÆ **non-causal** Ō£Ś (requires future input)
- $y[n] = n \cdot x[n-1]$ ŌåÆ **causal** Ō£ō
- $y[n] = x[-n]$ ŌåÆ **non-causal** for $n < 0$ Ō£Ś

## 2.4 Property 4 ŌĆö BIBO Stability (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output)

**Definition (CT):** A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input $x(t)$ produces a bounded output $y(t)$.

$$\text{If } \vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty \text{ for all } t, \text{ then } \vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty \text{ for all } t$$

**Definition (DT):**

$$\text{If } \vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x < \infty \text{ for all } n, \text{ then } \vert y[n] \vert \leq M_y < \infty \text{ for all } n$$

**Test Logic:**
- Assume a bounded input: $\vert x(t) \vert < M$ or $\vert x[n] \vert < M$.
- Compute $\vert y(t) \vert$ or $\vert y[n] \vert$ in terms of $\vert x \vert$.
- If the bound $M_y$ exists (a finite number independent of $t$ or $n$), the system is **stable**. Otherwise, **unstable**.

> [!NOTE]
> For LTI systems, the impulse response test: an LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** the impulse response is absolutely summable (DT) or absolutely integrable (CT).

**Examples:**
- $y[n] = 2x[n]$ ŌåÆ **stable** ($\vert y[n] \vert \leq 2M$) Ō£ō
- $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ ŌåÆ **unstable** (output grows without bound as $n \to \infty$) Ō£Ś
- $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ ŌåÆ **unstable** (integrator accumulates) Ō£Ś
- $y(t) = 5 \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$ ŌåÆ **stable** ($\vert y(t) \vert \leq 5M$) Ō£ō

## 2.5 Property 5 ŌĆö Time-Invariance

**Definition (CT):** A system is **time-invariant (TI)** if a time shift of $t_0$ in the input causes an **identical** time shift of $t_0$ in the output.

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$$

**Definition (DT):**

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$$

**Test Logic (4-step procedure):**

> **Step 1:** Let $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ be the original response.
> **Step 2:** Form the shifted input $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$.
> **Step 3:** Compute $y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$.
> **Step 4:** Compute the shifted output $y(t - t_0)$ and compare with $y_1(t)$.
> **Result:** If $y_1(t) = y(t - t_0)$ for all $t$ and all $t_0$, the system is **time-invariant**. Otherwise, **time-varying**.

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = x(t-2)$ ŌåÆ **TI** Ō£ō
- $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ ŌåÆ **time-varying** Ō£Ś
- $y(t) = x(2t)$ ŌåÆ **time-varying** Ō£Ś
- $y[n] = x[n-1] + 2x[n-3]$ ŌåÆ **TI** Ō£ō

## 2.6 Property 6 ŌĆö Linearity

A system is **linear** if and only if it satisfies **both** additivity and homogeneity (scaling), combined into the **Superposition Principle**.

**Additivity:**

$$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\} = y_1(t) + y_2(t)$$

**Homogeneity (Scaling):**

$$T\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x(t)\} = a \cdot y(t)$$

**Combined Superposition Condition:**

$$T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary constants.

**Test Logic:**
- Compute LHS = $T\{a \cdot x_1 + b \cdot x_2\}$.
- Compute RHS = $a \cdot T\{x_1\} + b \cdot T\{x_2\}$.
- If LHS = RHS, the system is **linear**. Otherwise, **non-linear**.

> [!WARNING]
> A constant offset breaks linearity! E.g., $y(t) = x(t) + 5$ is **non-linear** even though it may look simple, because the superposition condition fails.

**Examples:**
- $y(t) = 3x(t)$ ŌåÆ **linear** Ō£ō
- $y(t) = x^2(t)$ ŌåÆ **non-linear** Ō£Ś
- $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ ŌåÆ **linear** (the multiplier $t$ is a function, not depending on $x$) Ō£ō
- $y(t) = x(t) + 7$ ŌåÆ **non-linear** Ō£Ś (constant offset)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 ŌĆö Properties. Master these expressions and the test conditions to score full marks.

| **Property** | **CT Test Condition** | **DT Test Condition** | **Failing Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memoryless | $y(t)$ uses only $x(t)$ | $y[n]$ uses only $x[n]$ | $y(t) = x(t-1)$ |
| Invertible | Distinct inputs $\Rightarrow$ distinct outputs | Same as CT | $y(t) = x^2(t)$ |
| Causal | Output uses $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \leq t$ only | Output uses $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$ only | $y(t) = x(t+2)$ |
| BIBO Stable | $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M \Rightarrow \vert y(t) \vert \leq N$ | $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M \Rightarrow \vert y[n] \vert \leq N$ | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ |
| Time-Invariant | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ |
| Linear | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$ | Same as CT | $y(t) = x(t) + c$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Domain** | **Critical Property Required** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Audio amplifiers | BIBO Stability | Avoid speaker damage and distortion |
| Digital filters (FIR/IIR) | Linearity + Time-Invariance | Enables LTI analysis (convolution, DTFT) |
| Real-time control systems (e.g., ABS) | Causality | Must respond using only present/past sensor data |
| Communication channels | Invertibility | Enable channel equalization and signal recovery |
| DSP processors | Memoryless amplifiers (op-amps) | Predictable, instantaneous response |
| Radar/Sonar systems | Time-Invariance | Performance independent of when signal arrives |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of property tests for representative systems. Each step is fully expanded to match KTU valuation key expectations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Linearity of $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$

**Given:** $y(t) = T\{x(t)\} = t \cdot x(t)$

**Test Inputs:** Let $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ be two arbitrary inputs, and $a$, $b$ be arbitrary constants.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the superposition test:**

$$T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} \stackrel{?}{=} a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the LHS (left-hand side) by substituting the input:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} &= t \cdot \big[ a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t) \big] \\
&= t \cdot a \cdot x_1(t) + t \cdot b \cdot x_2(t) \\
&= a \cdot \big[ t \cdot x_1(t) \big] + b \cdot \big[ t \cdot x_2(t) \big] \\
&= a \cdot y_1(t) + b \cdot y_2(t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification:** The LHS equals the RHS identically. Hence, the system **satisfies linearity** (it is a **linear system**).

> [!NOTE]
> The multiplier $t$ is an **explicit function of time, not a function of the input signal $x(t)$**. This is the reason $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is linear.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time-Invariance of $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$

**Given:** $y(t) = T\{x(t)\} = t \cdot x(t)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the original output:**

$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply a time shift $t_0$ to the input and compute the response:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

$$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the same shift to the original output:**

$$y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compare $y_1(t)$ and $y(t - t_0)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1(t) &= t \cdot x(t - t_0) \\
y(t - t_0) &= (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0) = t \cdot x(t - t_0) - t_0 \cdot x(t - t_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion:** $y_1(t) \neq y(t - t_0)$ for $t_0 \neq 0$. Therefore, the system is **time-varying** (not time-invariant).

> [!TIP]
> The presence of an explicit time-dependent coefficient (like $t$ or $\cos(t)$ inside the system operator) almost always makes a system **time-varying**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: BIBO Stability of $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$

**Given:** $y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = n \cdot x[n]$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume a bounded input:**

$$\text{Let } \vert x[n] \vert \leq M < \infty \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the bound on the output:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert y[n] \vert &= \vert n \cdot x[n] \vert \\
&= \vert n \vert \cdot \vert x[n] \vert \\
&\leq \vert n \vert \cdot M
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Check if the bound is finite for all $n$:**

As $n \to \infty$, we have $\vert n \vert \to \infty$, so:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \to \infty$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:** No finite bound $M_y$ exists. The system is **BIBO unstable**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Invertibility of $y[n] = x[n-1]$

**Given:** $y[n] = x[n-1]$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Check if the mapping $x[n] \to y[n]$ is one-to-one:**

For any two distinct inputs $x_1[n] \neq x_2[n]$, we have:

$$y_1[n] = x_1[n-1] \quad \text{and} \quad y_2[n] = x_2[n-1]$$

Since the shift operation is a bijection on $\mathbb{Z}$, distinct $x_1, x_2$ give distinct $y_1, y_2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the inverse system:**

Let $w[n] = y[n] = x[n-1]$. Replace $n$ with $n+1$:

$$w[n+1] = x[n]$$

$$x[n] = w[n+1] = y[n+1]$$

Hence, the inverse system is:

$$T^{-1}\{y[n]\} = y[n+1]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification:**

$$T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = T^{-1}\{x[n-1]\} = x[n-1+1] = x[n] \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:** The system is **invertible**, with inverse $T^{-1}\{y[n]\} = y[n+1]$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Causality of $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t+1)$

**Given:** $y(t) = T\{x(t)\} = x(t) \cdot \cos(t+1)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inspect the input argument at any time $t_0$:**

$$y(t_0) = x(t_0) \cdot \cos(t_0 + 1)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the time arguments of $x$ in the expression:**

The only $x$ term is $x(t_0)$, evaluated at the **same** time $t_0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Check for any future dependency:**

There is no $x(t_0 + k)$ for $k > 0$. The factor $\cos(t_0+1)$ depends only on the time $t_0$, not on the input $x$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion:** The output at $t_0$ depends only on the input at $t_0$ (the present). The system is **causal**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Memoryless Test for $y(t) = \sin(t) \cdot x(t) + x^2(t-5)$

**Given:** $y(t) = \sin(t) \cdot x(t) + x^2(t-5)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inspect the input arguments in the equation:**

The expression contains:
- $x(t)$ ŌåÆ argument $t$ (present, no shift)
- $x(t-5)$ ŌåÆ argument $t-5$ (past shift by 5)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the memoryless test:**

For the system to be memoryless, **every** input must be $x(t)$ with no shift. Since $x(t-5)$ is present, the system **requires past input values**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion:** The system is **not memoryless**; it **has memory**.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Master Test Table

The following table consolidates the **6 test procedures** with one canonical example each.

| **Property** | **Canonical Test Equation** | **Example System** | **Conclusion** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linearity | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | Linear Ō£ō |
| Time-Invariance | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | Time-Varying Ō£Ś |
| BIBO Stability | $\vert x \vert \leq M \Rightarrow \vert y \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ | $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ | Unstable Ō£Ś |
| Causality | $y(t_0)$ uses $x(t)$ for $t \leq t_0$ only | $y(t) = x(t+2)$ | Non-Causal Ō£Ś |
| Memoryless | $y(t)$ uses only $x(t)$ (no shifts) | $y(t) = \sin(t) \cdot x(t)$ | Memoryless Ō£ō |
| Invertibility | One-to-one mapping; $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ exists | $y(t) = x^3(t)$ | Invertible Ō£ō |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö System Property Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[System T Mapping x to y] --> B[Memory Property]
    A --> C[Invertibility]
    A --> D[Causality]
    A --> E[BIBO Stability]
    A --> F[Time Invariance]
    A --> G[Linearity]

    B --> B1[Memoryless: only x of t]
    B --> B2[Has Memory: x of t minus t0]

    C --> C1[Invertible: unique inverse exists]
    C --> C2[Non Invertible: info loss]

    D --> D1[Causal: present and past inputs]
    D --> D2[Non Causal: future inputs]

    E --> E1[Stable: bounded in bounded out]
    E --> E2[Unstable: bounded in unbounded out]

    F --> F1[Time Invariant: shift commutes]
    F --> F2[Time Varying: shift changes behavior]

    G --> G1[Linear: additivity and homogeneity]
    G --> G2[Non Linear: superposition fails]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture for Property Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input x1 of t] --> SUM[Weighted Sum a x1 + b x2]
    IN2[Input x2 of t] --> SUM
    SUM --> T1[Apply System T]
    T1 --> OUT1[Output y = T of a x1 + b x2]
    T1 -.LHS.-> COMP[Comparison]
    PATH1[Apply T to x1] --> MUL1[Multiply by a]
    MUL1 --> ADD[a y1]
    PATH2[Apply T to x2] --> MUL2[Multiply by b]
    MUL2 --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT2[Output a y1 + b y2]
    ADD -.RHS.-> COMP
    COMP --> DEC{Equals}
    DEC -- Yes --> L[System is LINEAR]
    DEC -- No --> NL[System is NON LINEAR]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 ŌĆö Time-Invariance Test Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given System y of t = T of x of t] --> STEP1[Step 1: Apply time shift t0 to input]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Form x1 of t = x of t minus t0]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Step 2: Compute y1 of t = T of x1 of t]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Step 3: Compute shifted output y of t minus t0]
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Step 4: Compare y1 of t and y of t minus t0]
    STEP5 --> DEC{Equal for all t and t0}
    DEC -- Yes --> TI[System is TIME INVARIANT]
    DEC -- No --> TV[System is TIME VARYING]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 ŌĆö BIBO Stability Test Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Given System y of t = T of x of t] --> S1[Assume bounded input: absolute value of x of t less than M]
    S1 --> S2[Compute absolute value of y of t in terms of absolute value of x of t]
    S2 --> S3[Check if bound M y exists and is finite]
    S3 --> DEC{Bounded for all t}
    DEC -- Yes --> STABLE[System is BIBO STABLE]
    DEC -- No --> UNSTABLE[System is BIBO UNSTABLE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Linearity vs Time-Invariance confusion:** A system like $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is **linear** (multiplier depends on $t$, not $x$) but **time-varying** (the operator changes with time). Always test each property separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant offset rule:** $y(t) = x(t) + 5$ is **non-linear** because of the constant. Many students lose 2ŌĆō3 marks here.
> 3. **Causality check must be at $t_0$:** Write $y(t_0)$ explicitly and check that no $x(t_0 + k)$ with $k > 0$ appears.
> 4. **BIBO stability requires the bound to be finite for all $n$ or $t$:** An output that grows as $n \cdot M$ is unbounded.
> 5. **Squaring and absolute value break invertibility:** Students often claim $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is invertible. It is **not** (since $x$ and $-x$ give the same $y$).
> 6. **Memoryless ŌēĀ Causal:** A memoryless system is always causal, but a causal system need not be memoryless.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a memoryless system. Check whether $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ is memoryless.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A system is **memoryless** if its output at any time depends only on the input at the **same** time, and not on past or future values.

For the system $y[n] = (x[n])^2$:
- The output at index $n$ depends on $x[n]$ alone.
- There is no $x[n-1]$, $x[n-2]$, etc., and no $x[n+1]$, etc.
- Therefore, the system is **memoryless** Ō£ō.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for verification.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the condition for a system to be BIBO stable. Is the system $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \sin(t)$ BIBO stable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output:

$$\text{If } \vert x(t) \vert \leq M < \infty \text{ for all } t, \text{ then } \vert y(t) \vert \leq N < \infty \text{ for all } t$$

For $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \sin(t)$:
- Assume $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$.
- Then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert \cdot \vert \sin(t) \vert \leq M \cdot 1 = M$.
- The bound $N = M$ is finite.

Therefore, the system is **BIBO stable** Ō£ō.

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the verification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain with suitable examples the properties of memory and invertibility for systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Memory and Memoryless Property:**

A system has **memory** if the output at any time depends on inputs from other times (past or future). A system is **memoryless** if the output depends only on the input at the **same** time.

**Examples:**

| **System** | **Memoryless?** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| $y(t) = 3x(t)$ | Yes Ō£ō | Output uses $x(t)$ only |
| $y(t) = x(t) + 2x(t-5)$ | No Ō£Ś | Output uses $x(t-5)$ (past) |
| $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ | Yes Ō£ō | Output uses $x[n]$ only |
| $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-3]$ | No Ō£Ś | Output uses $x[n-3]$ (past) |

**Invertibility Property:**

A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs. The system must be one-to-one, and an inverse system $T^{-1}$ must exist such that $T^{-1}\{T\{x\}\} = x$.

**Examples:**

| **System** | **Invertible?** | **Inverse (if exists)** |
|---|---|---|
| $y(t) = 4x(t)$ | Yes Ō£ō | $x(t) = y(t)/4$ |
| $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | No Ō£Ś | Loses sign information |
| $y[n] = x[n-2]$ | Yes Ō£ō | $x[n] = y[n+2]$ |
| $y(t) = \cos(x(t))$ | No Ō£Ś | Cosine is not one-to-one |

**Conclusion:** A system may be memoryless but non-invertible (e.g., $y(t) = x^2(t)$), or have memory but be invertible (e.g., $y[n] = x[n-2]$). These properties are **independent**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Test the time-invariance of the system $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $y(t) = T\{x(t)\} = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the time-shifted input:**

$$x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the response to the shifted input:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1(t) &= T\{x_1(t)\} = T\{x(t - t_0)\} \\
&= x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the time-shifted output:**

$$y(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t - t_0)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compare $y_1(t)$ and $y(t - t_0)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_1(t) &= x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t) \\
y(t - t_0) &= x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(t - t_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclusion:**

Since $\cos(t) \neq \cos(t - t_0)$ in general, we have $y_1(t) \neq y(t - t_0)$. The system is **time-varying** (not time-invariant).

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\cos(t)$ is an explicit function of time, which always makes the system time-varying. The system **is linear** (since $\cos(t)$ does not depend on $x$).

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Define BIBO stability. Test the BIBO stability of the systems: (i) $y[n] = e^{x[n]}$ and (ii) $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of BIBO Stability:**

A system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output:

$$\text{If } \vert x(\cdot) \vert \leq M < \infty, \text{ then } \vert y(\cdot) \vert \leq N < \infty$$

**Test for (i): $y[n] = e^{x[n]}$**

Assume $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$, so $-M \leq x[n] \leq M$.

Then:

$$e^{-M} \leq e^{x[n]} \leq e^{M}$$

So $\vert y[n] \vert = e^{x[n]} \leq e^M$, which is finite. **System is BIBO stable** Ō£ō.

**Test for (ii): $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$**

Assume $\vert x(\tau) \vert \leq M$ for all $\tau$.

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert y(t) \vert &= \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau \right\vert \\
&\leq \int_{-\infty}^{t} \vert x(\tau) \vert \, d\tau \\
&\leq \int_{-\infty}^{t} M \, d\tau = M \cdot \infty = \infty
\end{aligned}$$

Since the integral is over the unbounded interval $(-\infty, t]$, the output is **unbounded**. **System is BIBO unstable** Ō£Ś.

> [!NOTE]
> Pure integrators are a classic example of BIBO unstable systems because they accumulate input over infinite time.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Test the linearity and time-invariance of the system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + 3$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Linearity Test:**

Let $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ be two inputs with outputs $y_1[n]$ and $y_2[n]$.

$$y_1[n] = n \cdot x_1[n] + 3, \quad y_2[n] = n \cdot x_2[n] + 3$$

**Compute LHS** for scaled-sum input $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} &= n \cdot (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + 3 \\
&= a \cdot n x_1[n] + b \cdot n x_2[n] + 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute RHS** for $a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$:

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a(n x_1[n] + 3) + b(n x_2[n] + 3) = a n x_1[n] + b n x_2[n] + 3(a + b)$$

**Comparison:**

$$\text{LHS} = a n x_1[n] + b n x_2[n] + 3$$
$$\text{RHS} = a n x_1[n] + b n x_2[n] + 3(a + b)$$

LHS ŌēĀ RHS (unless $a + b = 1$, which is not general). **System is non-linear** Ō£Ś.

The constant term $+3$ violates the **homogeneity** condition (taking $a = 0$ on the LHS gives $3$, but the RHS gives $0$).

**Time-Invariance Test:**

**Step 1:** Original output $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + 3$.

**Step 2:** Apply time shift to input: $x_1[n] = x[n - n_0]$.

$$y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0] + 3$$

**Step 3:** Apply shift to original output:

$$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + 3$$

**Step 4:** Compare:

$$y_1[n] = n \cdot x[n - n_0] + 3$$
$$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + 3$$

**Step 5:** Since $n \neq n - n_0$ (for $n_0 \neq 0$), we have $y_1[n] \neq y[n - n_0]$.

**System is time-varying** Ō£Ś.

**Conclusion:** The system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + 3$ is **non-linear and time-varying**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 2: Properties of Systems**

- [x] A system is a transformation $T$ mapping input $x$ to output $y$ (CT: $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$; DT: $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$).
- [x] **Memoryless** ŌĆö output uses only the input at the same time (no shifts like $x(t-t_0)$).
- [x] **Invertible** ŌĆö distinct inputs give distinct outputs; inverse system $T^{-1}$ exists.
- [x] **Causal** ŌĆö output uses only present and past inputs ($x(\tau)$ for $\tau \leq t$); no future dependencies like $x(t+1)$.
- [x] **BIBO Stable** ŌĆö bounded input gives bounded output; check by bounding $\vert y \vert$ in terms of $\vert x \vert$.
- [x] **Time-Invariant** ŌĆö apply the **4-step TI test**: shift input, compute response, shift output, compare.
- [x] **Linear** ŌĆö apply the **Superposition Principle** $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$.
- [x] A constant offset (e.g., $y = x + 5$) **always breaks linearity**.
- [x] An explicit time-dependent coefficient (e.g., $t$ or $\cos(t)$ in the operator) **always breaks time-invariance** but **preserves linearity** if it doesn't depend on $x$.
- [x] Memoryless ŌćÆ Causal (always), but Causal ŌćÅ Memoryless.
- [x] The system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ is the **classic BIBO unstable** system.
- [x] The integrator $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ is **non-causal and BIBO unstable**.
- [x] The system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is **memoryless but non-invertible**.
- [x] The system $y[n] = x[n-n_0]$ is **causal (if $n_0 \geq 0$), TI, linear, invertible, BIBO stable** ŌĆö a "model" well-behaved system.
- [x] Always write $y(t_0)$ explicitly when testing **causality** and **time-invariance**.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark questions, **test multiple properties** in separate sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unilateral Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unilateral Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unilateral Laplace Transform ŌĆö Formal Definition

The **Unilateral Laplace Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
X_I(s) = \mathcal{L}_u\{x(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt
$$

where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lower limit of integration is $0^-$, NOT $0$. The "$0^-$" (read as "zero minus") is a small, infinitesimally negative value approaching zero from the left. This subtle distinction captures any **impulse functions or discontinuities exactly at $t = 0$**, which is critical when solving linear constant-coefficient differential equations (LCCDE) with non-zero initial conditions.

**Region of Convergence (ROC):** $\text{Re}\{s\} > \sigma_0$ (always right-half plane since integration starts at $0^-$).

## 1.2 Unilateral Z-Transform ŌĆö Formal Definition

The **Unilateral Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$
X_I(z) = \mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The summation starts at $n = 0$, NOT at $n = -\infty$. This means only the **causal part** (and values at $n=0$) of the signal are considered.

**Region of Convergence (ROC):** $\vert z \vert > r_0$ (always outside a circle for causal signals).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "One-Way Mirror" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are watching a movie (signal). The **bilateral transform** is like watching both the past reels and the future reels of the film ŌĆö you see everything from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
>
> The **unilateral transform** is like having a **firewall at $t = 0$** (or $n = 0$). You can only see what happens **at and after** time zero. The past is hidden. But here's the powerful part ŌĆö you get a **"memory card"** that stores the initial conditions of the system at $t = 0^-$.
>
> **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why engineers prefer unilateral transforms for solving real systems ŌĆö every physical system we build "starts" at some moment, and we always need to know **what state it was in when it started**.

## 1.4 Why Unilateral Transforms? (Physical Motivation)

| Aspect | Bilateral | Unilateral |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Conditions** | Cannot incorporate directly | **Naturally embedded** via derivatives of $x(t)\cdot u(t)$ |
| **LCCDE Solving** | Requires additional steps | Directly yields solution with initial rest |
| **Causal Systems** | Same as bilateral for causal | Specialized form for causal analysis |
| **Initial/Final Value Theorems** | Not applicable | **Directly applicable** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation from Bilateral Laplace Transform

Starting from the bilateral Laplace transform of a **causal signal** $x(t)\cdot u(t)$:

$$
X_B(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,u(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

If we instead start from $0^-$, we additionally capture the contribution from impulses at $t=0$ and the behavior immediately before. For non-impulsive signals:

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

But for signals containing impulses, e.g., $x(t) = \delta(t)$, the bilateral gives **1**, while the unilateral gives **1** (also) since integration starts just before the impulse.

## 2.2 Key Properties of Unilateral Laplace Transform

Let $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{L}_u} X_I(s)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{L}_u} Y_I(s)$.

**Property 1 ŌĆö Linearity:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{a\,x(t) + b\,y(t)\} = a\,X_I(s) + b\,Y_I(s)
$$

**Property 2 ŌĆö Time Shifting (Right Shift):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{x(t - t_0)\} = e^{-s t_0} X_I(s) \quad \text{for } t_0 > 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Left Shift** ($t_0 > 0$): $\mathcal{L}_u\{x(t + t_0)\}$ is **NOT** simply $e^{s t_0} X_I(s)$. Extra terms involving initial values appear.

**Property 3 ŌĆö Time Differentiation (THE MOST IMPORTANT PROPERTY FOR KTU):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = s\,X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 X_I(s) - s\,x(0^-) - x'(0^-)
$$

**Property 4 ŌĆö Time Integration:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\int_{0^-}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = \frac{X_I(s)}{s}
$$

**Property 5 ŌĆö Frequency Shifting:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{e^{at}\, x(t)\} = X_I(s - a)
$$

**Property 6 ŌĆö Frequency Differentiation:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{-t\, x(t)\} = \frac{dX_I(s)}{ds}
$$

## 2.3 Initial Value Theorem (IVT) & Final Value Theorem (FVT) ŌĆö Laplace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial Value Theorem (Laplace):**

$$
x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s\, X_I(s)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Value Theorem (Laplace):**

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s\, X_I(s)
$$

provided all poles of $sX_I(s)$ lie in the **open left half-plane** (strictly $\text{Re}\{s\} < 0$).

## 2.4 Key Properties of Unilateral Z-Transform

**Property 1 ŌĆö Time Delay (Right Shift):**

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X_I(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k]\, z^{-k} \quad (n_0 > 0)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **extra summation term** that contains initial conditions ŌĆö this is unique to unilateral Z-transform.

**Property 2 ŌĆö Time Advance (Left Shift):**

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n + n_0]\} = z^{n_0} X_I(z) - \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k]\, z^{n_0 - 1 - k}
$$

**Property 3 ŌĆö Time Differentiation:**

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{n\, x[n]\} = -z \frac{d X_I(z)}{dz}
$$

## 2.5 Initial & Final Value Theorems ŌĆö Z-Transform

$$
x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X_I(z)
$$

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) X_I(z)
$$

(valid only if all poles of $(z-1)X_I(z)$ lie **inside** the unit circle except possibly at $z=1$).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transform / Property | Unilateral Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unilateral Laplace | $X_I(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ |
| 2 | Unilateral Z | $X_I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ |
| 3 | First Derivative (L) | $sX_I(s) - x(0^-)$ |
| 4 | Second Derivative (L) | $s^2 X_I(s) - s\,x(0^-) - x'(0^-)$ |
| 5 | Integration (L) | $\dfrac{X_I(s)}{s}$ |
| 6 | Right Shift (L), $t_0 > 0$ | $e^{-s t_0} X_I(s)$ |
| 7 | Right Shift (Z), $n_0 > 0$ | $z^{-n_0} X_I(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k] z^{-k}$ |
| 8 | Left Shift (Z), $n_0 > 0$ | $z^{n_0} X_I(z) - \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k] z^{n_0-1-k}$ |
| 9 | IVT (Laplace) | $x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sX_I(s)$ |
| 10 | FVT (Laplace) | $\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sX_I(s)$ |
| 11 | IVT (Z) | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X_I(z)$ |
| 12 | FVT (Z) | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X_I(z)$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Unilateral Transforms are used in Industry:**
> 1. **Control Systems (Radar, Robotics):** Solving LCCDEs of RLC circuits and motor systems with non-zero initial capacitor voltages / inductor currents.
> 2. **Digital Filter Design:** Designing recursive filters (IIR) using difference equations where initial states of shift registers matter.
> 3. **Power Electronics:** Transient analysis of switching converters where capacitor voltages at switching instant are non-zero.
> 4. **Communication Systems:** Stability and overshoot analysis using IVT/FVT on transfer functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Derivative Property of Unilateral Laplace Transform

Starting with the integral definition of $X_I(s)$:

$$
X_I(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ (using integration by parts, $u = e^{-st}$, $dv = \frac{dx}{dt} dt$):

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx(t)}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

Apply integration by parts:

$$
\int u\, dv = u v - \int v\, du
$$

Let $u = e^{-st}$, $du = -s\, e^{-st}\, dt$, and $dv = \frac{dx}{dt} dt$, $v = x(t)$.

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt = \Big[x(t)\, e^{-st}\Big]_{0^-}^{\infty} - \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t)\,(-s)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

Assuming $x(t)\,e^{-st} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (ROC condition):

$$
= 0 - x(0^-) + s \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Derivative Property

Apply the first-derivative property twice in succession:

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}\right\} = \mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)\right\}
$$

Let $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$, so $y(0^-) = \dfrac{dx}{dt}\big\vert_{t=0^-} = x'(0^-)$.

$$
= s \cdot \mathcal{L}_u\{y(t)\} - y(0^-)
$$

Substituting $\mathcal{L}_u\{y(t)\} = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)$:

$$
= s \left[s X_I(s) - x(0^-)\right] - x'(0^-)
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 X_I(s) - s\, x(0^-) - x'(0^-)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Initial Value Theorem (Laplace)

Starting from the first-derivative property:

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

Take the limit as $s \to \infty$ on both sides. The LHS integral vanishes (by Riemann's lemma, since $e^{-st} \to 0$ for $t > 0$ as $s \to \infty$):

$$
\lim_{s \to \infty} \left[s X_I(s) - x(0^-)\right] = 0
$$

$$
\lim_{s \to \infty} s X_I(s) - x(0^-) = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s X_I(s)}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Final Value Theorem (Laplace)

Again, from the first-derivative property:

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

Now take the limit as $s \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{s \to 0} \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt = \lim_{s \to 0}\left[s X_I(s) - x(0^-)\right]
$$

LHS becomes $\int_{0^-}^{\infty} dx = \lim_{t\to\infty} x(t) - x(0^-)$:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) - x(0^-) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

Cancelling $x(0^-)$ from both sides:

$$
\boxed{\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition:** The FVT is valid **only** if all poles of $sX_I(s)$ are in the **open left half-plane** $\text{Re}(s) < 0$. If any pole lies on the imaginary axis (e.g., $s = 0$ or $s = j\omega$), the theorem fails.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Solving an LCCDE using Unilateral Laplace Transform

**Problem:** Solve $y'(t) + 3y(t) = x(t)$ with $x(t) = u(t)$, $y(0^-) = 2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Unilateral Laplace to both sides:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{y'(t)\} + 3 \mathcal{L}_u\{y(t)\} = \mathcal{L}_u\{u(t)\}
$$

$$
[s Y_I(s) - y(0^-)] + 3 Y_I(s) = \frac{1}{s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute initial condition $y(0^-) = 2$:**

$$
s Y_I(s) - 2 + 3 Y_I(s) = \frac{1}{s}
$$

$$
Y_I(s)(s + 3) = \frac{1}{s} + 2 = \frac{1 + 2s}{s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $Y_I(s)$:**

$$
Y_I(s) = \frac{1 + 2s}{s(s + 3)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Partial Fraction Expansion:**

$$
\frac{1 + 2s}{s(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+3}
$$

$$
1 + 2s = A(s+3) + B s
$$

At $s = 0$: $1 = 3A \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{1}{3}$

At $s = -3$: $1 - 6 = -3B \Rightarrow B = \dfrac{5}{3}$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Inverse Laplace:**

$$
Y_I(s) = \frac{1/3}{s} + \frac{5/3}{s + 3}
$$

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \left[\frac{1}{3} + \frac{5}{3} e^{-3t}\right] u(t)}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification using FVT:**

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = \frac{1}{3} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1+2s}{s(s+3)} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verification using IVT:**

$$
y(0^+) = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{5}{3} = 2 = \lim_{s \to \infty} s \cdot \frac{1+2s}{s(s+3)} = \lim_{s\to\infty} \frac{1+2s}{s+3} = 2 \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.6 Worked Example: Right Shift Property of Unilateral Z-Transform

**Problem:** Find $\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n - 2]\}$ where $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find unilateral Z-transform of $x[n]$:**

$$
X_I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n z^{-n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert > 0.5
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Right Shift Property for $n_0 = 2$:**

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n-2]\} = z^{-2} X_I(z) + x[0] z^{-1} + x[1] z^{0}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $x[0]$ and $x[1]$:**

$$
x[0] = (0.5)^0 = 1, \quad x[1] = (0.5)^1 = 0.5
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n-2]\} = \frac{z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + z^{-1} + 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n-2]\} = \frac{z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + z^{-1} + 0.5}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bilateral vs Unilateral Transform Domain Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Signal x(t)"] --> B["Bilateral Transform\nIntegral: -inf to +inf"]
    A --> C["Unilateral Transform\nIntegral: 0- to +inf"]
    B --> D["Full History View\nNo Initial Conditions"]
    C --> E["Forward Only View\nInitial Conditions Captured"]
    D --> F["Pure Frequency Analysis"]
    E --> G["LCCDE Solver\nIVT/FVT Apply"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Solving LCCDE via Unilateral Laplace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input LCCDE\na_n y^(n) + ... = b_m x^(m) + ..."] --> B["Apply Unilateral Laplace\nto Both Sides"]
    B --> C["Use Differentiation Properties\ns^k Y(s) - s^(k-1) y(0-) - ..."]
    C --> D["Substitute Initial Conditions\ny(0-), y'(0-), ..."]
    D --> E["Algebraically Solve for Y(s)"]
    E --> F["Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    F --> G["Inverse Laplace Transform"]
    G --> H["Final Time-Domain Solution y(t)"]
```

## 4.3 ROC Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIL["Bilateral ROC (Example)"]
        B1["Sigma greater than -a"]
        B2["Two-sided possible\ndepending on signal"]
    end
    subgraph UNI["Unilateral ROC (Example)"]
        U1["Sigma greater than sigma_0"]
        U2["Always Right Half Plane\nRe s greater than max pole real part"]
    end
    BIL --> UNI
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Initial Value Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["s-domain Signal X_I(s)"] --> B["Multiply by s\nto get sX_I(s)"]
    B --> C["Apply Limit\ns approaches infinity"]
    C --> D["x(0+)\nTime Domain Initial Value"]
    D --> E["Direct Readout\nof System Startup Behavior"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**Define the unilateral Laplace transform. Why is the lower limit taken as $0^-$ instead of $0$?**

**Model Answer:**

The unilateral Laplace transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
X_I(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt
$$

The lower limit is taken as **$0^-$** (an infinitesimally small value less than zero) instead of $0$ because of the following reasons:

1. **To capture impulse functions** occurring at $t = 0$. For example, if $x(t) = \delta(t)$, integrating from $0$ to $\infty$ gives 0, but integrating from $0^-$ gives 1.
2. **To consistently account for discontinuities** and abrupt changes exactly at $t = 0$.
3. **To simplify LCCDE solutions** with initial conditions $y(0^-)$ ŌĆö using $0^-$ ensures the transform property $\mathcal{L}_u\{\frac{dx}{dt}\} = sX_I(s) - x(0^-)$ holds cleanly.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**State and explain the Initial Value Theorem (IVT) for the unilateral Laplace transform.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** If $X_I(s)$ is the unilateral Laplace transform of $x(t)$, then:
>
> $$
> x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s\, X_I(s)
> $$
>
> **provided** the limit exists.

**Explanation:** The IVT allows direct computation of the signal's value at $t = 0^+$ without performing the full inverse Laplace transform. It is widely used in control systems to predict the **initial response** of a system right after an input is applied (e.g., initial surge current when a switch is closed).

**Example:** Given $X_I(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+2}$, then $x(0^+) = \lim_{s\to\infty} \dfrac{5s}{s+2} = 5$.

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### ŌØō QUESTION A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the unilateral Laplace transform property for the **first derivative** of a signal and use it to find $y(t)$ for the differential equation:

$$
\frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 5 \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 6 y(t) = x(t)
$$

where $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$, with initial conditions $y(0^-) = 1$, $y'(0^-) = 0$.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\left\{\frac{dx}{dt}\right\} = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Take unilateral Laplace transform of LCCDE:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_u\{y''(t)\} + 5 \mathcal{L}_u\{y'(t)\} + 6 \mathcal{L}_u\{y(t)\} = \mathcal{L}_u\{e^{-t} u(t)\}
$$

Applying the differentiation properties:

$$
[s^2 Y_I(s) - s\, y(0^-) - y'(0^-)] + 5[s Y_I(s) - y(0^-)] + 6 Y_I(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute initial conditions $y(0^-) = 1$, $y'(0^-) = 0$:**

$$
s^2 Y_I(s) - s - 0 + 5 s Y_I(s) - 5 + 6 Y_I(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}
$$

$$
(s^2 + 5s + 6) Y_I(s) = \frac{1}{s+1} + s + 5
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify RHS:**

$$
\frac{1}{s+1} + s + 5 = \frac{1 + (s+5)(s+1)}{s+1} = \frac{1 + s^2 + 6s + 5}{s+1} = \frac{s^2 + 6s + 6}{s+1}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $Y_I(s)$:**

$$
Y_I(s) = \frac{s^2 + 6s + 6}{(s+1)(s^2 + 5s + 6)} = \frac{s^2 + 6s + 6}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Partial Fraction Expansion:**

$$
Y_I(s) = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3}
$$

At $s = -1$: $A = \dfrac{1 - 6 + 6}{(1)(2)} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

At $s = -2$: $B = \dfrac{4 - 12 + 6}{(-1)(1)} = \dfrac{-2}{-1} = 2$

At $s = -3$: $C = \dfrac{9 - 18 + 6}{(-2)(-1)} = \dfrac{-3}{2}$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$
\boxed{y(t) = \left[\frac{1}{2} e^{-t} + 2 e^{-2t} - \frac{3}{2} e^{-3t}\right] u(t)}
$$

---

**(b)** State and prove the **Final Value Theorem** for unilateral Laplace transform. What is the condition for its validity? Apply FVT to compute $\lim_{t\to\infty} y(t)$ from the transfer function obtained in part (a).

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:**

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s)
$$

**Proof:** From the derivative property:

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} \frac{dx(t)}{dt}\, e^{-st}\, dt = s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

Take limit $s \to 0$ on both sides:

$$
\int_{0^-}^{\infty} dx(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) - x(0^-) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s) - x(0^-)
$$

Cancelling $x(0^-)$:

$$
\boxed{\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s)}
$$

**Validity Condition:** All poles of $s X_I(s)$ must lie strictly in the **open left half-plane** ($\text{Re}(s) < 0$). Poles on the imaginary axis invalidate FVT.

**Application:**

From part (a): $Y_I(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + 6s + 6}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$. All poles are at $-1, -2, -3$, all strictly negative. FVT is valid.

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s^2 + 6s + 6}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{0}{6} = 0
$$

This matches our time-domain solution: $y(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

### ŌØō QUESTION B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the right-shift property of the **unilateral Z-transform** and use it to determine the response $y[n]$ of a system described by:

$$
y[n] - 0.5\, y[n-1] = x[n]
$$

where $x[n] = u[n]$, $y[-1] = 4$. Solve for $y[n]$ for $n \geq 0$.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Right-Shift Property:**

Starting from the unilateral Z-transform definition:

$$
X_I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}
$$

For $x[n - n_0]$ with $n_0 > 0$:

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, z^{-n}
$$

Let $m = n - n_0$, then $n = m + n_0$:

$$
= \sum_{m = -n_0}^{\infty} x[m]\, z^{-(m + n_0)} = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-n_0}^{\infty} x[m]\, z^{-m}
$$

Split the sum:

$$
= z^{-n_0}\left[\sum_{m=-n_0}^{-1} x[m]\, z^{-m} + \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} x[m]\, z^{-m}\right]
$$

$$
= z^{-n_0}\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k - n_0]\, z^{-(k-n_0)} + X_I(z)\right]
$$

Re-indexing with $k = n_0 - m - 1$ gives the **canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X_I(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k - n_0]\, z^{-k}}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply unilateral Z-transform to the difference equation:**

For $y[n]$: $\mathcal{Z}_u\{y[n]\} = Y_I(z)$

For $y[n-1]$ (right shift with $n_0 = 1$):

$$
\mathcal{Z}_u\{y[n-1]\} = z^{-1} Y_I(z) + y[-1]\, z^{0} = z^{-1} Y_I(z) + 4
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö LHS becomes:**

$$
Y_I(z) - 0.5[z^{-1} Y_I(z) + 4] = X_I(z)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $X_I(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$:**

$$
Y_I(z) - 0.5 z^{-1} Y_I(z) - 2 = \frac{z}{z-1}
$$

$$
Y_I(z)(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) = \frac{z}{z-1} + 2 = \frac{z + 2(z-1)}{z-1} = \frac{3z - 2}{z-1}
$$

$$
Y_I(z) \cdot \frac{z - 0.5}{z} = \frac{3z - 2}{z-1}
$$

$$
Y_I(z) = \frac{z(3z - 2)}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Partial Fraction:**

$$
\frac{Y_I(z)}{z} = \frac{3z - 2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)} = \frac{A}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B}{z - 1}
$$

At $z = 0.5$: $A = \dfrac{1.5 - 2}{0.5 - 1} = \dfrac{-0.5}{-0.5} = 1$

At $z = 1$: $B = \dfrac{3 - 2}{1 - 0.5} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2$

$$
Y_I(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{2z}{z - 1}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Inverse Z-Transform:**

$$
\boxed{y[n] = \left[(0.5)^n + 2\right] u[n]}
$$

---

**(b)** Verify your answer to part (a) using the Initial Value Theorem and Final Value Theorem of Z-transform.

**Model Solution:**

**IVT Verification:**

$$
y[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} Y_I(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \left[\frac{z}{z-0.5} + \frac{2z}{z-1}\right] = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

Check from closed-form: $y[0] = (0.5)^0 + 2 = 3$ Ō£ō

**FVT Verification:**

First check validity: poles of $(z-1)Y_I(z) = \dfrac{z(3z-2)}{(z-0.5)(z-1)} \cdot (z-1) = \dfrac{z(3z-2)}{z-0.5}$ ŌĆö only pole at $z = 0.5$ which is inside the unit circle. Ō£ō Valid.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} y[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{z(3z - 2)}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{z(3z - 2)}{z - 0.5} = \frac{1 \cdot 1}{0.5} = 2
$$

Check from closed-form: $\lim_{n \to \infty} y[n] = \lim_{n\to\infty} [(0.5)^n + 2] = 0 + 2 = 2$ Ō£ō

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using $0$ instead of $0^-$**: Forgetting the lower limit convention loses full marks in derivation questions. Always write $0^-$.
> 2. **Missing initial condition terms in shift properties**: For both Laplace (left shift) and Z-transform (right/left shift), students often write $z^{-n_0} X_I(z)$ or $e^{-st_0} X_I(s)$ without the additional summation/initial-value terms. This is the **#1 error** in KTU answer sheets ŌĆö expect to lose **3 to 4 marks** per occurrence.
> 3. **Applying FVT without checking pole locations**: If asked to compute steady-state value, always **verify ROC/pole positions first** and state explicitly that the FVT is valid.
> 4. **Confusing $\mathcal{L}_u$ with $\mathcal{L}_b$**: The unilateral transform of $\delta(t)$ is $1$, same as bilateral ŌĆö but the unilateral transform of $u(t)$ **assumes causality**, so always multiply by $u(t)$ in your final time-domain answer.
> 5. **Right shift of Z-transform sign error**: The summation $\sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k] z^{-k}$ has the **opposite sign convention** compared to the left shift. Re-derive if in doubt.

---

## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Unilateral Transforms:**
>
> Ō£ģ **Unilateral Laplace**: $X_I(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ ŌĆö lower limit $0^-$ captures impulses & initial conditions.
>
> Ō£ģ **Unilateral Z-Transform**: $X_I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ ŌĆö only causal samples considered.
>
> Ō£ģ **Key Derivative Property**: $\mathcal{L}_u\{x'(t)\} = sX_I(s) - x(0^-)$ ŌĆö this is the **foundation** for solving LCCDEs with initial conditions.
>
> Ō£ģ **Z-Transform Right Shift**: $\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X_I(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k - n_0] z^{-k}$ ŌĆö don't forget the **extra summation**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Z-Transform Left Shift**: $\mathcal{Z}_u\{x[n + n_0]\} = z^{n_0} X_I(z) - \sum_{k=0}^{n_0 - 1} x[k] z^{n_0 - 1 - k}$.
>
> Ō£ģ **IVT (Laplace)**: $x(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s X_I(s)$ ŌĆö always valid if $X_I(s)$ is proper rational.
>
> Ō£ģ **FVT (Laplace)**: $\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s X_I(s)$ ŌĆö **only if all poles of $sX_I(s)$ are in OLHP**.
>
> Ō£ģ **IVT (Z)**: $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X_I(z)$.
>
> Ō£ģ **FVT (Z)**: $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X_I(z)$ ŌĆö **only if all poles of $(z-1)X_I(z)$ are inside the unit circle**.
>
> Ō£ģ **ROC always right-half plane (Laplace)** or **outside a circle (Z)** for unilateral transforms of causal signals.
>
> Ō£ģ **LCCDE solving strategy**: Transform ŌåÆ Substitute initial conditions ŌåÆ Solve algebraically ŌåÆ Partial fractions ŌåÆ Inverse transform.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Domain Representation of Discrete Time Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Frequency Domain Representation of Discrete Time Signals # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Frequency Domain Representation ŌĆö The Big Picture In the time domain, a discrete-time signal is described by the sequence $x[n]$, where $n$ is an integer index. While the time domain tells us **when** a signal value occurs, the frequency domain tells us **how fast** the signal is oscillating. The frequency domain representation answers the question: > *"What pure sinusoids (and at what amplitudes/phases) make up this signal?"* For discrete-time signals, the two most important frequency-domain tools are: 1. **Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** ŌĆö A mathematical, idealised, continuous-frequency representation. 2. **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** ŌĆö A practical, computer-friendly, discrete-frequency representation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DTFT is the theoretical Fourier tool for discrete-time signals, while the DFT is the *numerically computable* version of the DTFT. Every DFT is essentially a *sampled DTFT* on a single period. --- ## 1.2 Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) ŌĆö Formal Definition For a discrete-time, non-periodic (aperiodic) sequence $x[n]$, the **Discrete Time Fourier Transform** is defined as: $$ X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} $$ The **Inverse Discrete Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT)** is given by: $$ x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega $$ Here: - $\omega$ is the **normalized angular frequency** in **radians/sample** (always bounded between $-\pi$ and $\pi$). - $X(e^{j\omega})$ is a **complex-valued, continuous, and periodic** function of $\omega$ with period $2\pi$. > [!NOTE] > **Existence Conditions (Dirichlet Conditions) for DTFT:** > 1. $x[n]$ must be **absolutely summable**: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ > 2. $x[n]$ must have a **finite number of maxima, minima, and discontinuities** in any finite interval. > 3. $x[n]$ must be **square summable** for energy signals. --- ## 1.3 Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) ŌĆö Formal Definition For a finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$, i.e., $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $n \geq N$, the **N-point Discrete Fourier Transform** is defined as: $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j \frac{2\pi}{N} kn} \quad ; \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1 $$ The **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is given by: $$ x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, e^{j \frac{2\pi}{N} kn} \quad ; \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1 $$ Here $k$ is the **discrete frequency index** and $X[k]$ is also a periodic sequence with period $N$. --- ## 1.4 The Twiddle Factor A key building block of the DFT is the **Twiddle Factor**, denoted as: $$ W_N = e^{-j \frac{2\pi}{N}} $$ Then $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j \frac{2\pi}{N} kn}$, and the DFT/IDFT can be compactly written as: $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} \quad ; \quad x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-kn} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Twiddle Factor satisfies $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$, so DFT outputs are inherently **periodic with period $N$**. The principal value of the Twiddle Factor is $W_N^{0} = 1$. --- ## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **Analogy ŌĆö "The Recipe Book" ­¤Ź░** > > Imagine a complex cake (your signal $x[n]$). The time-domain recipe just tells you: *which ingredient (value) goes in, in which order (at time $n$)*. The DTFT/DFT, on the other hand, asks: *what underlying pure ingredients (sinusoids of frequency $\omega$ or $k$) combine to recreate this cake?* > > - The **DTFT** is the *theoretical chef's perfect recipe* ŌĆö infinitely many pure ingredients (continuous spectrum). > - The **DFT** is the *practical printed cookbook version* ŌĆö only $N$ ingredients sampled at $N$ specific frequencies. > > Just as a sample of the cookbook recipe still lets you bake a recognisable cake, the DFT lets a computer **numerically** recover the spectrum of a finite signal. --- ## 1.6 Why Frequency Domain? ŌĆö Engineering Motivation | Domain | Question Answered | Tools | |:------:|:------------------|:------| | Time ($n$) | *When* does the signal change? | Convolution, Difference Eq. | | Frequency ($\omega$ or $k$) | *How fast* is the signal oscillating? | DTFT, DFT, Z-Transform | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** > - **Audio Processing:** MP3 / AAC encoders use a Modified DFT (MDCT) to split audio into frequency bands. > - **Image Processing:** The 2D-DFT is the basis of JPEG compression. > - **Telecommunications:** OFDM (used in 4G/5G/WiFi) uses the DFT to multiplex subcarriers. > - **Vibration Analysis:** Mechanical fault detection in rotating machines uses DFT-based spectral analysis. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Connection: DTFT ŌåÆ DFT To go from the **theoretical DTFT** to the **practical DFT**, we apply **frequency-domain sampling**: 1. **Truncate** $x[n]$ to a finite length $N$ (multiply by a rectangular window of length $N$). 2. **Sample** the continuous DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ at $N$ equally spaced frequencies $\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$. 3. The samples $X[k] = X(e^{j\omega})\big\vert_{\omega = 2\pi k/N}$ are the DFT values. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Theorem:** The DFT samples are exactly the DTFT samples *scaled* by a factor of $1/N$ when derived from periodic sequences, but for finite-length sequences, the relationship is: $X_{DFT}[k] = X_{DTFT}(e^{j 2\pi k / N})$. --- ## 2.2 DTFT Properties ŌĆö Detailed Analysis | Sl. No. | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | Engineering Significance | |:------:|:---------|:------------|:----------------|:------------------------| | 1 | **Linearity** | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b X_2(e^{j\omega})$ | Superposition of LTI systems | | 2 | **Time Shift** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ | Delay in digital filters | | 3 | **Frequency Shift** | $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n]$ | $X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ | Modulation in communications | | 4 | **Time Reversal** | $x[-n]$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ | Used in convolution symmetry | | 5 | **Convolution** | $x[n] * h[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) H(e^{j\omega})$ | LTI system analysis | | 6 | **Multiplication** | $x[n] \cdot y[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$ | Windowing / Filtering | | 7 | **Differentiation in Freq.** | $n x[n]$ | $j \frac{d X(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$ | Hilbert transforms | | 8 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ | Energy conservation | | 9 | **Conjugate Symmetry** | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ | Real signals ŌåÆ symmetric spectrum | | 10 | **Periodicity** | ŌĆö | $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | Fundamental to DFT sampling | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL INSIGHT:** Property 10 (Periodicity) is the most exploited property in KTU problems. It directly permits the DTFT to be sampled in $[0, 2\pi]$ instead of $(-\pi, \pi)$. --- ## 2.3 DFT Properties ŌĆö Detailed Analysis | Sl. No. | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | |:------:|:---------|:------------|:----------------| | 1 | **Linearity** | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1[k] + b X_2[k]$ | | 2 | **Circular Time Shift** | $x[(n - n_0) \mod N]$ | $W_N^{k n_0} X[k]$ | | 3 | **Circular Frequency Shift** | $W_N^{-k_0 n} x[n]$ | $X[(k - k_0) \mod N]$ | | 4 | **Circular Convolution** | $x_1[n] \text{ (N) } x_2[n]$ | $X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$ | | 5 | **Multiplication** | $x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | $\frac{1}{N} \big( X_1[k] \text{ (N) } X_2[k] \big)$ | | 6 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | | 7 | **Duality** | $X[n]$ | $N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$ | | 8 | **Conjugate Symmetry** | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*[(-k) \mod N]$ | | 9 | **Time Reversal** | $x[(-n) \mod N]$ | $X[(-k) \mod N]$ | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **linear convolution** ($*$) with **circular convolution** ($\text{(N)}$). DFT multiplication corresponds to **circular** convolution, not linear. To get linear convolution using DFT, both sequences must be **zero-padded to length $\geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$**. --- ## 2.4 Properties of the Twiddle Factor $W_N$ These identities are essential for KTU problem-solving: 1. **Periodicity:** $W_N^{k + N} = W_N^k$ 2. **Symmetry:** $W_N^{k + N/2} = -W_N^k$ (for even $N$) 3. **Inversion:** $W_N^{-1} = W_N^{N-1} = W_N^{*}$ 4. **Roots of Unity:** $W_N^N = 1$ 5. **Orthogonality:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } m \equiv n \pmod N \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Frequency-Domain Tool Used | |:------------|:---------------------------| | Speech Recognition (Siri, Alexa) | Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) | | OFDM in 4G/5G | IDFT/DFT (256/512/1024-point) | | Spectrum Analyzers | DFT via FFT algorithm | | Audio Equalizers | Filter banks using DFT bins | | Vibration Monitoring of Motors | Magnitude Spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert$ | | Radar Signal Processing | Matched filtering in frequency domain | --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Master Formula Sheet (Quick Revision) | Concept | Equation | Key Note | |:--------|:---------|:---------| | DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | Continuous, Periodic in $\omega$ | | Inverse DTFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$ | Integration limits $\pm\pi$ | | N-point DFT | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$ | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | | Inverse DFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$ | Scaling factor $1/N$ | | Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Unit magnitude | | Circular Convolution | $y[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] x_2[(n-m) \mod N]$ | Periodic extension | | Linear Conv. via DFT | Zero-pad to $L \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$ | $y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X_1[k] X_2[k]\}$ | | Parseval's (DTFT) | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ | Energy conservation | | Parseval's (DFT) | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Discrete energy | | Time Shift (DTFT) | $x[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ | Linear phase property | | DFT of Shifted Impulse | $\delta[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow W_N^{k n_0}$ | Used in IDFT proofs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pro Tip for KTU:** Always write the Twiddle Factor form for **every** DFT step. Substituting $W_N$ simplifies the algebra drastically and examiners award partial credit more generously. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From DTFT to DFT (Frequency Sampling Theorem) **Statement:** For a finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$, the N-point DFT values $X[k]$ are exactly the samples of its DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ taken at $N$ equally spaced frequencies on the unit circle. **Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the DTFT definition.** $$ X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the finite-length constraint.** Since $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $n \geq N$, the infinite sum collapses to a finite sum from $n = 0$ to $N-1$: $$ X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Sample the frequency variable at discrete points.** Sample $\omega$ at $N$ equally spaced points: $\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$. $$ X(e^{j\omega_k}) = X(e^{j 2\pi k / N}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j \frac{2\pi k}{N} n} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the Twiddle Factor substitution.** Using the definition $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$: $$ X[k] \triangleq X(e^{j 2\pi k / N}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} $$ This is **exactly the N-point DFT**. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** This is the *theoretical license* that allows DSP engineers to use DFT (via FFT) in place of DTFT for any finite-length signal. Without this link, computers couldn't compute Fourier transforms at all. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: IDFT from the Forward DFT **Statement:** Given $X[k]$ (the DFT), we can recover $x[n]$ via $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Start by computing the candidate reconstruction.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the definition of $X[k]$.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] W_N^{km} \right) W_N^{-kn} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Interchange the order of summation.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} \right) $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the orthogonality property of the Twiddle Factor.** $$ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = \begin{cases} N, & m \equiv n \pmod N \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Since $x[n]$ is non-zero only for $0 \leq m \leq N-1$, the condition $m = n$ (within the principal range) is the only surviving term: $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \cdot x[n] \cdot N = x[n] $$ Hence $y[n] = x[n]$, proving the IDFT formula. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Circular Convolution from DFT Multiplication **Statement:** If $X_1[k]$ and $X_2[k]$ are the $N$-point DFTs of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ respectively, then the IDFT of the product $Y[k] = X_1[k] X_2[k]$ is the **circular convolution** $x_1[n] \, \text{(N)} \, x_2[n]$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Take the inverse DFT of $Y[k] = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_1[k] X_2[k] \, W_N^{-kn} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the DFT of $X_2[k]$.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_2[m] W_N^{km} \right) X_1[k] W_N^{-kn} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Interchange summation and group by $x_2[m]$.** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_2[m] \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_1[k] W_N^{k(m-n)} \right) $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Recognize the inner sum as $N$ times the IDFT of $X_1[k]$ evaluated at index $(m-n) \mod N$.** The inner sum: $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_1[k] W_N^{k(m-n)} = N \cdot x_1[(m-n) \mod N]$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back and obtain the circular convolution formula.** $$ y[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_2[m] \, x_1[(n - m) \mod N] $$ This is the **circular convolution** $x_1[n] \, \text{(N)} \, x_2[n]$. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequency-Domain Convolution Theorem:** $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ (linear) $\xrightarrow{DFT} X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$ *only when* both sequences are zero-padded to length $L \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö 4-point DFT of a Rectangular Pulse **Problem:** Compute the 4-point DFT of $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify $N = 4$, so the Twiddle Factor is:** $$ W_4 = e^{-j 2\pi/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j $$ So $W_4^0 = 1$, $W_4^1 = -j$, $W_4^2 = -1$, $W_4^3 = j$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $X[0]$:** $$ X[0] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{0 \cdot n} = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 1 = 4 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $X[1]$:** $$ X[1] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{n} = 1(1) + 1(-j) + 1(-1) + 1(j) = 0 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $X[2]$:** $$ X[2] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{2n} = 1(1) + 1(-1) + 1(1) + 1(-1) = 0 $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $X[3]$:** $$ X[3] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{3n} = 1(1) + 1(j) + 1(-1) + 1(-j) = 0 $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Final Result:** $$ X[k] = \{4, \, 0, \, 0, \, 0\} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** A rectangular pulse in time produces a **Dirichlet (sinc-like)** spectrum in frequency. For length-$N$ constant sequence, the DFT is $\{N, 0, 0, \dots, 0\}$. This is a **highly valued exam observation** in KTU. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Linearity of Linear and Circular Convolution **Lengths:** Let $x_1[n]$ have length $L_1$ and $x_2[n]$ have length $L_2$. - **Linear convolution** $x_1 * x_2$ has length $L = L_1 + L_2 - 1$. - **Circular (N-point) convolution** $x_1 \text{ (N) } x_2$ has length $N$. If $N = L$, then the **first $L-1$ samples** of linear and circular convolutions are identical. The last sample of circular convolution equals the **time-aliased (overlapped) sum**: $$ y_{circ}[L-1] = y_{lin}[L-1] + y_{lin}[-1] = y_{lin}[L-1] + y_{lin}[L_1 + L_2 - 2] \;\; \text{(overlap addition)} $$ Hence, to make circular convolution equal to linear convolution, **the DFT length $N$ must satisfy:** $$ N \geq L_1 + L_2 - 1 $$ This is the fundamental **zero-padding condition** used universally in KTU DSP problems. $\blacksquare$ # 4.
