KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam Model Questions ŌĆö Module 2** ### Q1. Define DTFT and state the conditions for its existence. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of a discrete-time, non-periodic sequence $x[n]$ is defined as: $$ X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} $$ **Conditions for Existence (Dirichlet Conditions):** 1. The sequence $x[n]$ must be **absolutely summable**: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ 2. The sequence $x[n]$ must have a **finite number of discontinuities and finite extrema** in any finite interval. 3. The sequence must be **square summable** (finite energy): $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Conditions (1.5). --- ### Q2. What is the Twiddle Factor? List any four of its important properties. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Modified]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Twiddle Factor** is a complex exponential that forms the kernel of the DFT: $$ W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N} $$ **Properties:** 1. **Unit Magnitude:** $\vert W_N \vert = 1$ for all $N$ and $k$. 2. **Periodicity:** $W_N^{k + N} = W_N^k$ (DFT output is periodic with period $N$). 3. **Symmetry:** $W_N^{k + N/2} = -W_N^k$ (for even $N$). 4. **Orthogonality:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = N$ if $m \equiv n \pmod N$, else $0$. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Any 4 properties (2). --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Each Part B question carries 14 marks. As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer ONE of two internal choices, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.** --- ### ­¤öĄ QUESTION A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Pattern]` **(a) (7 Marks)** For the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, compute the 4-point DFT $X[k]$ and plot its magnitude spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert$. #### Model Solution (7 Marks): **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify $N = 4$. Compute the Twiddle Factor:** $$ W_4 = e^{-j 2\pi/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j $$ The values: $W_4^0 = 1, \; W_4^1 = -j, \; W_4^2 = -1, \; W_4^3 = j$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the DFT formula for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$:** $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{kn} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $X[0]$:** $$ X[0] = x[0] + x[1] + x[2] + x[3] = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10 $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $X[1]$:** $$ X[1] = 1(1) + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = -2 + 2j $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $X[2]$:** $$ X[2] = 1(1) + 2(-1) + 3(1) + 4(-1) = -2 $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $X[3]$:** $$ X[3] = 1(1) + 2(j) + 3(-1) + 4(-j) = -2 - 2j $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Magnitude spectrum:** $$ \vert X[0] \vert = 10, \quad \vert X[1] \vert = 2\sqrt{2}, \quad \vert X[2] \vert = 2, \quad \vert X[3] \vert = 2\sqrt{2} $$ > **Mark Distribution:** Twiddle Factor (1.5) + 4 DFT values (4 ├Ś 1 = 4) + Magnitude (1.5). --- **(b) (7 Marks)** State and prove the **circular convolution property** of the DFT. Using this property, find the circular convolution of the sequences $x_1[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ using the DFT method. #### Model Solution (7 Marks): **Step 1 ŌĆö Statement of the Property:** If $X_1[k]$ and $X_2[k]$ are the N-point DFTs of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, then the IDFT of $X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$ is the **circular convolution** $x_1[n] \text{ (N) } x_2[n]$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Proof (as derived in Section 3.3 of these notes):** $$ y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X_1[k] X_2[k]\} = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_2[m] \, x_1[(n-m) \mod N] $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute DFT of $x_1[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$:** From the worked example, $X_1[k] = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute DFT of $x_2[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:** From part (a), $X_2[k] = \{10, -2+2j, -2, -2-2j\}$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply point-wise:** $$ Y[k] = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k] = \{40, \; 0, \; 0, \; 0\} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Take 4-point IDFT of $Y[k]$:** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} Y[k] W_4^{-kn} $$ For $n = 0$: $y[0] = \frac{1}{4}(40 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 10$. For $n = 1, 2, 3$: All terms have at least one factor of $0$, so $y[n] = 0$. **Step 7 ŌĆö Final Result:** $$ y[n] = \{10, \, 0, \, 0, \, 0\} $$ > **Mark Distribution:** Statement (1) + Proof (2) + DFT computation (2) + IDFT (2). --- ### ­¤¤ó QUESTION B ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Pattern]` **(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the relationship between the **DTFT and DFT** for a finite-length sequence. Hence show that the DFT samples the DTFT at $N$ equally spaced frequencies on the unit circle. #### Model Solution (7 Marks): The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key highlights for the answer script: **Step 1 ŌĆö DTFT of a finite-length sequence** (length $N$): $$ X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency sampling at $\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$:** $$ X[k] = X(e^{j 2\pi k / N}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substituting $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$:** $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} $$ This is **exactly the N-point DFT**. Hence the DFT samples the DTFT at $N$ equally spaced points on the unit circle. $\blacksquare$ > **Mark Distribution:** DTFT for finite length (2) + Sampling logic (2) + Twiddle substitution (1.5) + Conclusion (1.5). --- **(b) (7 Marks)** Compute the linear convolution of the sequences $x_1[n] = \{1, 2\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ using the **DFT method** (with proper zero-padding). #### Model Solution (7 Marks): **Step 1 ŌĆö Determine zero-padding length:** $x_1$ has length $L_1 = 2$, $x_2$ has length $L_2 = 4$. For linear convolution via DFT, pad to $N = L_1 + L_2 - 1 = 2 + 4 - 1 = 5$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Zero-pad both sequences to length 5:** $$ x_1[n] = \{1, 2, 0, 0, 0\}, \quad x_2[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 0\} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute 5-point DFT of $x_1[n]$:** Using $W_5 = e^{-j 2\pi/5}$: $$ X_1[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{4} x_1[n] W_5^{kn} = 1 + 2 W_5^k \quad ; \quad k = 0,1,2,3,4 $$ Explicitly: - $X_1[0] = 3$ - $X_1[1] = 1 + 2 e^{-j 2\pi/5}$ - $X_1[2] = 1 + 2 e^{-j 4\pi/5}$ - $X_1[3] = 1 + 2 e^{-j 6\pi/5} = 1 + 2 e^{j 4\pi/5}$ - $X_1[4] = 1 + 2 e^{-j 8\pi/5} = 1 + 2 e^{j 2\pi/5}$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute 5-point DFT of $x_2[n]$:** $$ X_2[k] = 1 + 2 W_5^k + 3 W_5^{2k} + 4 W_5^{3k} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply point-wise: $Y[k] = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$.** **Step 6 ŌĆö Compute 5-point IDFT:** $$ y[n] = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{k=0}^{4} Y[k] W_5^{-kn} $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Verify via direct linear convolution:** $$ y[n] = x_1 * x_2 = \{1, \; 4, \; 7, \; 10, \; 8, \; 0\} \;\; \text{(length 5)} $$ > **Mark Distribution:** Zero-pad justification (1.5) + DFT (3) + IDFT logic (2) + Final answer (0.5). > [!WARNING] > **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ factor in IDFT:** This single omission costs **2 full marks**. Always carry the $1/N$ throughout. > 2. **Computing DFT with $W_N^{-kn}$ instead of $W_N^{kn}$:** This is a sign convention error that flips the entire spectrum. Stick to $W_N^{kn}$ for forward DFT. > 3. **Not stating the zero-padding condition $N \geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$:** Examiners explicitly test this concept. Always state it before solving. > 4. **Confusing circular index arithmetic with linear:** $(n-m) \mod N$ means **wrap around**, not zero-out. E.g., for $N=4$, index $-1$ becomes $3$, not $0$. > 5. **Skipping unit checks on Twiddle Factor:** $W_4 = -j$ and $W_8 = e^{-j\pi/4}$ are often mis-evaluated. Memorize: $W_N^{N/2} = -1$ for even $N$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > ­¤ōī **Use this as a last-minute KTU exam revision sheet.** - Ō£ģ **DTFT** converts a discrete-time sequence into a **continuous, periodic** spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$, periodic with period $2\pi$. - Ō£ģ **DFT** is the **N-point sampled version** of the DTFT, evaluated at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$. - Ō£ģ **Twiddle Factor** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ is a complex number on the **unit circle** with unit magnitude. - Ō£ģ **DFT is periodic with period N**; **DTFT is periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$. - Ō£ģ **Parseval's Theorem** (DTFT): $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$. - Ō£ģ **Parseval's Theorem** (DFT): $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$. - Ō£ģ **DFT of $x[n] = \delta[n]$** is $X[k] = 1$ for all $k$. - Ō£ģ **DFT of $x[n] = 1$ (constant)** is $X[k] = N\delta[k]$. - Ō£ģ **DFT of a rectangular pulse** of length $L$ (with $L < N$) is a **Dirichlet kernel**: $X[k] = \frac{\sin(\pi k L/N)}{\sin(\pi k / N)} e^{-j\pi k (L-1)/N}$. - Ō£ģ **Circular convolution** is what DFT multiplication produces. **Linear convolution** = circular convolution only when sequences are zero-padded to length $\geq L_1 + L_2 - 1$. - Ō£ģ **Conjugate symmetry:** For **real** $x[n]$, $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ ŌĆö the DFT spectrum is symmetric. - Ō£ģ **DFT of $x[(n-n_0) \mod N]$** equals $W_N^{k n_0} X[k]$ (circular time shift property). - Ō£ģ **DFT of $W_N^{-k_0 n} x[n]$** equals $X[(k-k_0) \mod N]$ (circular frequency shift property). - Ō£ģ **Time reversal:** DFT of $x[(-n) \mod N] = X[(-k) \mod N]$. - Ō£ģ **DFT is always linear:** $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] \leftrightarrow a X_1[k] + b X_2[k]$. - Ō£ģ **Remember the integration range** for IDTFT: $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ (one full period). - Ō£ģ **The DFT is the bridge** between continuous theoretical transforms and real-world DSP hardware.

topics.title: Discrete Time Fourier Series and its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Time Fourier Series and its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **DTFS Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> The **Discrete Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** is the mathematical representation of a periodic discrete-time signal $x[n]$ as a *finite* sum (or weighted sum) of harmonically related complex exponentials of the form $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$, where the frequencies of these exponentials are integer multiples of the **fundamental digital frequency** $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$, with $N$ being the fundamental period of the signal.

For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ with fundamental period $N$ (i.e., $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$), the DTFS pair is given by:

### **Synthesis Equation (Inverse DTFS):**

$$
x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{\,j k \Omega_0 n}, \quad \Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}
$$

### **Analysis Equation (DTFS Coefficient):**

$$
a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{\,-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

The summation interval $\langle N \rangle$ denotes summation over **any contiguous set of $N$ samples** (e.g., $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction from CTFS (Continuous-Time Fourier Series):**
> - In CTFS, the Fourier coefficients $a_k$ are *non-periodic* in $k$ and there are *infinitely many* distinct harmonics.
> - In **DTFS**, the coefficients $a_k$ are **periodic with period $N$** (i.e., $a_{k+N} = a_k$), and only $N$ distinct complex exponentials $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ exist in a single period.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Coin Collection" Intuition

Imagine you have a **slot machine (one full period of $N$ samples)** that emits a repeating pattern of sound $x[n]$. You want to know **how much of each pure musical note** is hidden inside this pattern.

- The signal $x[n]$ is your **chord** (a mixture of pure tones).
- The complex exponentials $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ are the **pure musical notes** at harmonically related pitches.
- The DTFS coefficients $a_k$ tell you the **"volume knob" (amplitude and phase)** of each pure note $k$ that, when all played together, reconstruct the original chord $x[n]$.

Just as you only need **$N$ different keys** on a small keyboard (because adding 12 keys just repeats the same notes one octave higher), in DTFS you only need **$N$ unique coefficients** $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{N-1}$ ŌĆö the rest ($a_N, a_{2N}, \dots$) are exact copies.

## 1.3 Existence & Dirichlet Conditions for DTFS

For a DTFS representation to exist, the signal $x[n]$ must be **periodic with period $N$** and **absolutely summable over one period**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dirichlet Conditions for DTFS:**
> 1. **Periodicity:** $x[n] = x[n+N]$ for all integer $n$.
> 2. **Absolute Summability:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$.
>
> *Note: Unlike CTFS, these conditions are mild. The bounded variation requirement is automatically satisfied since $x[n]$ is defined only at discrete points.*

## 1.4 Why DTFS is Powerful in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application of DTFS |
|---|---|
| **Digital Audio** | Decomposing periodic audio tones (musical notes) into pure frequency components. |
| **OFDM Communication** | The receiver uses the DTFS principle to extract subcarrier data. |
| **Digital Control Systems** | Analyzing the response of periodic sampled systems. |
| **Image Processing** | Periodic textures (e.g., fabric, bricks) are analyzed via 2D-DFT, a direct descendant of DTFS. |
| **Vibration Analysis** | Diagnosing faults in rotating machinery by examining periodic vibration harmonics. |

> **Standard Metric:** The fundamental digital frequency is always $\mathbf{\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}}$ **radians/sample**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the DTFS Equations

The DTFS is a **two-way bridge** between the time domain and the discrete frequency domain.

### **Step 1 ŌĆö Projection (Analysis):**
The coefficient $a_k$ is computed by taking the *inner product* of $x[n]$ with the complex exponential $e^{-jk\Omega_0 n}$ over one full period $N$. The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ normalizes the result, much like taking an average.

### **Step 2 ŌĆö Reconstruction (Synthesis):**
Once all $N$ coefficients $a_k$ are known, we reassemble the signal by *linearly combining* $N$ complex exponentials, each weighted by its respective coefficient $a_k$.

### **Why only $N$ coefficients?**
Because the complex exponentials $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ are themselves **periodic in $k$ with period $N$**:

$$
e^{j(k+N)\Omega_0 n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n} \cdot e^{jN\Omega_0 n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n} \cdot e^{j2\pi n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n}
$$

So the "harmonics" at $k = N, 2N, \dots$ are *identical* to those at $k = 0, 1, \dots$. This is why **DTFS has a compact, finite-dimensional frequency representation**, making it ideal for digital computation (basis of the DFT and FFT).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the ONLY reference you need during valuation for solving any DTFS problem in KTU 2024.**

### **A. Core DTFS Equations**

| Equation Type | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Synthesis | $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{\,j k \Omega_0 n}$ | Builds signal from harmonics. |
| Analysis | $a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{\,-j k \Omega_0 n}$ | Extracts coefficient $a_k$. |
| Fundamental Frequency | $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | Lowest distinct angular frequency. |
| Periodicity of $a_k$ | $a_{k+N} = a_k$ | Coefficients repeat every $N$. |
| Trigonometric Form | $x[n] = a_0 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N/2-1} \text{Re}\{a_k e^{jk\Omega_0 n}\} + (-1)^n a_{N/2}$ | For $N$ even. |

### **B. Properties of DTFS ŌĆö Quick Reference**

| Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain ($a_k$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | $A x[n] + B y[n]$ | $A a_k + B b_k$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $a_k \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}$ |
| **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{j M \Omega_0 n} \, x[n]$ | $a_{k-M}$ |
| **Time Reversal** | $x[-n]$ | $a_{-k}$ |
| **Conjugation** | $x^*[n]$ | $a_{-k}^*$ |
| **Conjugate Symmetry** (real $x[n]$) | $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ | $a_{-k} = a_k^*$ |
| **Multiplication** | $x[n] \cdot y[n]$ | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{\ell=\langle N \rangle} a_\ell \, b_{k-\ell}$ (Periodic Conv.) |
| **Periodic Convolution** | $\sum_{\ell=\langle N \rangle} x[\ell] \, y[n-\ell]$ | $N \cdot a_k \, b_k$ |
| **First Difference** | $x[n] - x[n-1]$ | $a_k \left(1 - e^{-j k \Omega_0}\right)$ |
| **Running Sum** | $\sum_{m=-\infty}^{n} x[m]$ (with $a_0=0$) | $\frac{a_k}{1 - e^{-j k \Omega_0}}$ |
| **Parseval's Relation** | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} \vert a_k \vert^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention in KTU:** Time shifting property uses $e^{-jk\Omega_0 n_0}$ (some textbooks use $e^{+jk\Omega_0 n_0}$). Always verify the sign convention from the previous year's answer key. The negative sign convention is **standard at KTU**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of DTFS Properties

- **Time Shifting** $\rightarrow$ Used in radar signal processing to align echoes.
- **Parseval's Relation** $\rightarrow$ Enables energy calculations in the frequency domain (used in audio compression, MP3 encoding).
- **Multiplication Property** $\rightarrow$ Models amplitude modulation in digital communication systems.
- **Conjugate Symmetry** $\rightarrow$ Exploited by real-valued FFT algorithms to halve memory and computation (e.g., in $N=2^k$ FFT routines used in 5G baseband processing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Analysis Equation

**Problem Setup:** We want to prove that the coefficient $a_k$ can be extracted as given by the analysis equation.

**Step 1:** Begin with the synthesis equation and multiply both sides by $e^{-j r \Omega_0 n}$, then sum over one full period $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j r \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \, e^{j k \Omega_0 n} \right] e^{-j r \Omega_0 n}
$$

**Step 2:** Swap the order of summation (allowed because both sums are finite):

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j r \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j (k-r) \Omega_0 n} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the inner sum, which is a geometric series. Using $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$:

$$
S = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j (k-r) \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ e^{j 2\pi (k-r) / N} \right]^n
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ (for $r \neq 1$):

$$
S = \frac{1 - e^{j 2\pi (k-r)}}{1 - e^{j 2\pi (k-r)/N}}
$$

**Step 5:** Observe that $e^{j 2\pi (k-r)} = 1$ whenever $k - r$ is an integer (which it always is). So the numerator becomes $1 - 1 = 0$.

**Step 6:** Analyze the denominator. When $k = r$ (mod $N$), the denominator is also $1 - 1 = 0$, giving a $\frac{0}{0}$ indeterminate form. We must use **L'H├┤pital's rule** or recognize the limit:

$$
\lim_{k \to r} \frac{1 - e^{j 2\pi (k-r)/N}}{1 - e^{j 2\pi (k-r)/N}} \cdot \frac{e^{j 2\pi (k-r)/N}}{1}
$$

Alternatively, the standard orthogonality result is:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j (k-r) \Omega_0 n} = \begin{cases} N, & k - r = mN, \ m \in \mathbb{Z} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

That is:

$$
S = N \cdot \delta_{k, r \ (\text{mod } N)}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back. The right-hand side collapses because the sum over $k$ picks out only the term where $k = r$:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j r \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \cdot N \cdot \delta_{k, r} = N \, a_r
$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $a_r$ and renaming the dummy index $r \to k$:

$$
\boxed{\, a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n} \,}
$$

This completes the proof of the analysis equation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Shifting Property

**Claim:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} a_k$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} a_k \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the DTFS coefficient of the shifted signal $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$:

$$
b_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n - n_0] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$. Since $x$ has period $N$, the summation range remains over one full period:

$$
b_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=-n_0}^{N-1-n_0} x[m] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 (m + n_0)}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the periodicity of $x$ to shift the summation limits to $m = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$:

$$
b_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 m} \cdot e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the sum equals $N \cdot a_k$:

$$
b_k = e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 m}}_{= a_k}
$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$
\boxed{\, b_k = a_k \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0} \,}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Multiplication Property (Periodic Convolution)

**Claim:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} a_k$ and $y[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} b_k$, then $z[n] = x[n] y[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\ell=\langle N \rangle} a_\ell \, b_{k-\ell}$.

**Step 1:** Write the DTFS coefficient of the product:

$$
c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, y[n] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand $x[n]$ using its synthesis equation:

$$
c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{\ell=0}^{N-1} a_\ell \, e^{j \ell \Omega_0 n} \right] y[n] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}
$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation:

$$
c_k = \sum_{\ell=0}^{N-1} a_\ell \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n] \, e^{-j (k-\ell) \Omega_0 n} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** The bracketed term is exactly $b_{k-\ell}$ (by definition of the DTFS coefficient). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\, c_k = \sum_{\ell=0}^{N-1} a_\ell \, b_{k-\ell} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Periodic Convolution** (or circular convolution) of the coefficient sequences. Unlike linear convolution, periodic convolution wraps around modulo $N$. The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ comes from the standard $1/N$ normalization of DTFS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example

**Example:** Find the DTFS coefficients of $x[n] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4} n\right)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the period:** The signal has digital frequency $\Omega = \frac{\pi}{4}$. Setting $\Omega = \frac{2\pi}{N} m$ for the smallest positive integer $m = 1$:

$$
N = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express in exponential form:**

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2} e^{j \pi n / 4} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-j \pi n / 4}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Match with the synthesis equation $x[n] = \sum_k a_k e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ where $\Omega_0 = 2\pi/8 = \pi/4$:**

- $a_1 = \frac{1}{2}$
- $a_{-1} = \frac{1}{2}$
- $a_7 = a_{-1} = \frac{1}{2}$ (using periodicity $a_{k+N} = a_k$)
- All other $a_k = 0$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Coefficient Set (one period, e.g., $k = 0, 1, \dots, 7$):**

$$
a_0 = 0, \ a_1 = \frac{1}{2}, \ a_2 = 0, \ a_3 = 0, \ a_4 = 0, \ a_5 = 0, \ a_6 = 0, \ a_7 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DTFS Analysis-Synthesis Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x[n] (Periodic Signal)"] --> B["Multiply by e⁻⁹Ω₀n"]
    B --> C["Sum Over One Period n=0 to N-1"]
    C --> D["Multiply by 1/N"]
    D --> E["aₗ (DTFS Coefficients)"]
    E --> F["Multiply by e⁹ₗΩ₀n"]
    F --> G["Sum Over k=0 to N-1"]
    G --> H["x[n] (Reconstructed)"]
    A -.Reconstructed.-> H
```

## 4.2 DTFS Property Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["x1[n]"] --> SUM1["+"]
    P2["x2[n]"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> A1["a1_k"]
    SUM2["+"] --> A2["a2_k"]
    A1 --> OUT1["a_k = A a1_k + B a2_k"]
    A2 --> OUT1
    SHIFT["x[n - n0]"] --> SHIFTOUT["a_k e⁻⁹κΩ₀n0"]
    FREQ["e⁹ΜΩ₀n x[n]"] --> FREQOUT["a_ₗ-M"]
    CONV["x[n] * y[n]"] --> CONVOUT["1/N sum a_L b_ₗ-L"]
    PARSE["Energy Time Domain"] --> PARSE2["= sum |a_k|²"]
    PARSE2 --> PARSE3["Energy Frequency Domain"]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: DTFS in the Larger Signal Analysis Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["Signal x[n]"] --> S1{"Is x[n] Periodic?"}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2["Period N"]
    S1 -- No --> S3["DTFT"]
    S2 --> DTFS["DTFS aₗ (N-point)"]
    S2 --> DFS["DFS aₗ (infinite, periodic)"]
    DTFS --> DFT["DFT X[k] = N aₗ"]
    DFS --> DTFT2["DTFT XΩ (Line Spectrum)"]
    DFT --> FFT["FFT O(N log N)"]
    note1["DTFS = DFT up to a scaling factor"]
    DFT -.- note1
    note2["DTFS is the foundation of all digital spectrum analysis"]
    FFT -.- note2
```

## 4.4 Spectral Magnitude Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    K0["k=0: |a0|"]
    K1["k=1: |a1|=0.5"]
    K7["k=7: |a7|=0.5"]
    K234["k=2..6: |ak|=0"]
    K0 ~~~ K1 ~~~ K234 ~~~ K7
```

*Schematic for the example $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4)$ showing only two non-zero spectral lines at $k = 1$ and $k = 7$ (which equals $k = -1$ modulo 8).*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mark Loss Hotspot 1 (2 marks):** Forgetting that $a_k$ is **periodic with period $N$**. Always state $a_{k+N} = a_k$ explicitly.
> - **Mark Loss Hotspot 2 (2 marks):** Using wrong sign convention in time-shifting ($e^{+j k \Omega_0 n_0}$ vs $e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}$). KTU uses the **negative sign convention**.
> - **Mark Loss Hotspot 3 (3 marks):** Not explicitly defining the summation range $\langle N \rangle$ or assuming $n = 0$ to $\infty$ for a periodic signal ŌĆö this is **wrong**; use one full period.
> - **Mark Loss Hotspot 4 (1 mark):** Mixing up the **multiplication** property (periodic convolution with $1/N$ factor) and the **convolution** property (no $1/N$ factor but a factor of $N$).

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define the Discrete Time Fourier Series (DTFS). What is the fundamental relationship between the period of a signal and its fundamental digital frequency?**

**Model Answer:**
> The DTFS represents a periodic discrete-time signal $x[n]$ with period $N$ as a weighted sum of $N$ harmonically related complex exponentials $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$:
> $$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$$
> The fundamental digital frequency is $\Omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ radians/sample. As $N$ increases, $\Omega_0$ decreases, meaning the harmonics become more closely spaced in the digital frequency domain.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the periodicity property of DTFS coefficients.**

**Model Answer:**
> The DTFS coefficients are inherently **periodic in $k$ with period $N$**:
> $$a_{k+N} = a_k$$
> This arises because the complex exponentials $e^{j(k+N)\Omega_0 n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n} e^{jN\Omega_0 n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ are themselves periodic in $k$. As a result, only $N$ distinct coefficients ($a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{N-1}$) uniquely characterize a periodic signal. The remaining coefficients are simply repetitions. This is in stark contrast to CTFS, where $a_k \to 0$ as $|k| \to \infty$ and there are infinitely many distinct harmonics.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 2 Choice Format (Standard KTU Pattern):**
> Either Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the DTFS coefficients for the periodic signal $x[n]$ with one period defined as:
$$x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad \text{over } n = 0, 1, 2, 3 \quad (\text{so } N = 4)$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A signal $y[n]$ is the time-shifted version $y[n] = x[n-2]$. Using the **time-shifting property of DTFS**, find the DTFS coefficients of $y[n]$ in terms of those of $x[n]$. Verify your result by direct computation for the first two coefficients.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Direct DTFS Computation**

**Step 1:** Fundamental frequency: $\Omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N} = \dfrac{2\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad/sample.

**Step 2:** Apply the analysis equation for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$:

$$
a_k = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \, e^{-j k (\pi/2) n}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö $a_0$ (DC component):**
$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{4} (1 + 2 + 3 + 4) = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö $a_1$ (First harmonic):**
$$
a_1 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 \cdot e^{0} + 2 \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} + 3 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 4 \cdot e^{-j3\pi/2} \right]
$$
$$
a_1 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) \right] = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 - 3 + j(-2 + 4) \right] = \frac{-2 + 2j}{4} = -0.5 + 0.5j
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö $a_2$ (Second harmonic):**
$$
a_2 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 \cdot e^{0} + 2 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 3 \cdot e^{-j2\pi} + 4 \cdot e^{-j3\pi} \right]
$$
$$
a_2 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 \right] = \frac{-2}{4} = -0.5
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö $a_3$ (Third harmonic):**
$$
a_3 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 \cdot e^{0} + 2 \cdot e^{-j3\pi/2} + 3 \cdot e^{-j3\pi} + 4 \cdot e^{-j9\pi/2} \right]
$$
$$
e^{-j3\pi/2} = j, \quad e^{-j3\pi} = -1, \quad e^{-j9\pi/2} = -j
$$
$$
a_3 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j \right] = \frac{-2 - 2j}{4} = -0.5 - 0.5j
$$

#### **Final Result (Part a):**
$$
\boxed{a_0 = 2.5, \quad a_1 = -0.5 + 0.5j, \quad a_2 = -0.5, \quad a_3 = -0.5 - 0.5j}
$$

> **Verification of Conjugate Symmetry:** $a_3 = a_{-1} = a_1^*$ Ō£ō (since $x[n]$ is real-valued, $a_{-k} = a_k^*$).

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Time Shifting Property**

**Step 1:** State the property:
$$
\text{If } x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} a_k, \quad \text{then } y[n] = x[n-n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}} b_k = a_k \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}
$$

For $n_0 = 2$ and $\Omega_0 = \pi/2$:
$$
b_k = a_k \, e^{-j k \pi}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $b_k$ symbolically:
- $b_0 = a_0 \cdot e^{0} = a_0 = 2.5$
- $b_1 = a_1 \cdot e^{-j\pi} = (-0.5 + 0.5j)(-1) = 0.5 - 0.5j$
- $b_2 = a_2 \cdot e^{-j2\pi} = -0.5 \cdot 1 = -0.5$
- $b_3 = a_3 \cdot e^{-j3\pi} = (-0.5 - 0.5j)(-1) = 0.5 + 0.5j$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verification by direct computation of $b_1$:**

The shifted signal $y[n] = x[n-2]$ has one period:
$$y[n] = \{3, 4, 1, 2\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3$$
(Shifted right by 2 positions cyclically.)

$$
b_1 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 3 \cdot e^{0} + 4 \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} + 1 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 2 \cdot e^{-j3\pi/2} \right]
$$
$$
b_1 = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 3 - 4j - 1 + 2j \right] = \frac{2 - 2j}{4} = 0.5 - 0.5j
$$

Ō£ō **Matches the property-derived result.**

#### **Final Result (Part b):**
$$
\boxed{b_0 = 2.5, \quad b_1 = 0.5 - 0.5j, \quad b_2 = -0.5, \quad b_3 = 0.5 + 0.5j}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a periodic signal $x[n]$ with period $N = 5$, the DTFS coefficients are given as:
$$a_k = \{2, 1+j, 1, 1-j, 0\}$$
Find the **average power** of the signal $x[n]$ using **Parseval's relation**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider two periodic signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$, both with period $N = 6$, whose DTFS coefficients are $a_k$ and $b_k$ respectively. If $z[n] = x[n] \cdot y[n]$, find the DTFS coefficient $c_2$ of $z[n]$ at $k = 2$ in terms of $a_k$ and $b_k$. **Explain the significance** of the $1/N$ factor that appears.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Parseval's Relation**

**Step 1:** State Parseval's relation for DTFS:
$$
\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert a_k \vert^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the squared magnitudes:
- $\vert a_0 \vert^2 = 2^2 = 4$
- $\vert a_1 \vert^2 = \vert 1 + j \vert^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$
- $\vert a_2 \vert^2 = 1^2 = 1$
- $\vert a_3 \vert^2 = \vert 1 - j \vert^2 = 2$
- $\vert a_4 \vert^2 = 0^2 = 0$

**Step 3:** Sum:
$$
P = \sum_{k=0}^{4} \vert a_k \vert^2 = 4 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 0 = 9
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Average Power} = P = 9 \ \text{units}}
$$

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Multiplication Property**

**Step 1:** State the multiplication property of DTFS:
$$
c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\ell=0}^{N-1} a_\ell \, b_{k-\ell}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N = 6$ and $k = 2$:
$$
c_2 = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{\ell=0}^{5} a_\ell \, b_{2-\ell}
$$

**Step 3:** Expanded form:
$$
c_2 = \frac{1}{6} \left[ a_0 b_2 + a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_0 + a_3 b_{-1} + a_4 b_{-2} + a_5 b_{-3} \right]
$$

Using $b_{k+N} = b_k$ (periodicity of $b$):
$$
c_2 = \frac{1}{6} \left[ a_0 b_2 + a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_0 + a_3 b_5 + a_4 b_4 + a_5 b_3 \right]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Significance of the $1/N$ factor:**

> The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ arises directly from the **analysis equation**, which normalizes the inner product by the period length $N$. When two signals are multiplied in the time domain, the resulting DTFS coefficients are the **periodic convolution** of the two coefficient sequences, divided by $N$. The factor preserves the **Parseval energy equivalence** and ensures that the **orthogonality normalization** of the basis functions $e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ is correctly accounted for. Without this $1/N$ factor, the duality between multiplication and convolution would be violated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for DTFS (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

- **Synthesis:** $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{j k \Omega_0 n}$, $\Omega_0 = 2\pi/N$.
- **Analysis:** $a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n}$.
- **$a_k$ is periodic** with period $N$: $a_{k+N} = a_k$. Only $N$ distinct values.
- **Real signals** satisfy $a_{-k} = a_k^*$ (conjugate symmetry).
- **Time shift:** $x[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow a_k e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}$ (negative sign convention at KTU).
- **Frequency shift:** $e^{j M \Omega_0 n} x[n] \leftrightarrow a_{k-M}$.
- **Multiplication** $\leftrightarrow$ **Periodic Convolution** of coefficients **divided by $N$**.
- **Periodic Convolution** of signals $\leftrightarrow$ $N \cdot a_k b_k$ (no $1/N$, but multiplied by $N$).
- **Parseval's:** $\frac{1}{N} \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum \vert a_k \vert^2$ (power/energy equivalence).
- **DC component** $a_0 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]$ (signal mean).
- **Time reversal:** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow a_{-k}$.
- **Conjugation:** $x^*[n] \leftrightarrow a_{-k}^*$.
- **First difference:** $x[n] - x[n-1] \leftrightarrow a_k(1 - e^{-jk\Omega_0})$.
- **Real cosine** $\cos(\Omega_0 n)$ for $\Omega_0 = 2\pi m/N$ has only two non-zero coefficients: $a_m = a_{N-m} = 1/2$.
- **Real sine** $\sin(\Omega_0 n)$ for $\Omega_0 = 2\pi m/N$ has $a_m = \frac{1}{2j}$, $a_{N-m} = -\frac{1}{2j}$.
- **Aliasing rule:** Adding $N$ to the harmonic index gives the same exponential: $e^{j(k+N)\Omega_0 n} = e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always specify the summation range (e.g., $n = 0$ to $N-1$) explicitly ŌĆö partial credit depends on it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and its Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and its Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is a frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic signal $x[n]$. Unlike the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) which produces a discrete spectrum, the DTFT yields a **continuous and periodic** function of the angular frequency variable $\omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DTFT maps a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ (defined on integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$) into a **continuous, periodic** frequency-domain function $X(e^{j\omega})$ with period $2\pi$. This dual nature (discrete in time, continuous and periodic in frequency) is the most testable concept in KTU examinations.

**The Analysis Equation (Forward DTFT):**

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

**The Synthesis Equation (Inverse DTFT):**

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The integral in the inverse transform is taken over **any** contiguous interval of length $2\pi$ (typically $-\pi$ to $\pi$), since $X(e^{j\omega})$ is periodic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The DNA Fingerprint of a Signal"

Imagine $x[n]$ as a **musical chord** played on a piano. To the human ear, it sounds like one unified tone, but in reality, it is a combination of many pure sinusoidal notes. The **DTFT acts like a perfectly tuned audio spectrum analyzer**:

- It listens to the entire discrete signal.
- It decomposes it into an **infinite continuum of complex sinusoids** $e^{j\omega n}$ ŌĆö one for every possible frequency $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- The resulting spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ tells us *exactly* "how much" of each frequency is present in the chord.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** In the time domain, we deal with a sequence of numbers separated by integer steps. In the frequency domain, we get a **smooth, unbroken curve** that repeats every $2\pi$ radians ŌĆö like a seamless ribbon pattern on a loom.

---

## 1.3 Existence Condition (Sufficient Condition)

For the DTFT to converge, the sequence $x[n]$ must be **absolutely summable**:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty
$$

Equivalently, $x[n]$ must be **square summable** ($l^2$ ŌĆö i.e., finite energy) for the transform to exist in the mean-square sense.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse DTFT with DFT. Remember: **DFT is a *sampled version* of DTFT.** DFT = DTFT evaluated at $N$ equally spaced points $\omega_k = 2\pi k/N$ within one period.

---

## 1.4 Physical Interpretation of $X(e^{j\omega})$

In general, $X(e^{j\omega})$ is a **complex-valued** function. It can be expressed in polar form:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \cdot e^{j\angle X(e^{j\omega})}
$$

- $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ ŌåÆ **Magnitude Spectrum** (how strong each frequency is).
- $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ ŌåÆ **Phase Spectrum** (how each frequency is time-shifted).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the DTFT ŌĆö Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Input the discrete-time signal** $x[n]$.
- The signal is defined only at integer instants $n = \ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

**Step 2: Choose a test frequency** $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- We ask: "How well does the complex sinusoid $e^{j\omega n}$ correlate with $x[n]$?"

**Step 3: Compute the inner product** (correlation):
- $X(e^{j\omega}) = \langle x[n], e^{j\omega n} \rangle$ ŌĆö this gives a complex number.

**Step 4: Repeat for all $\omega$** in $[-\pi, \pi]$:
- Since $\omega$ is a continuous variable, we obtain an *uncountably infinite* set of complex numbers ŌĆö a function.

**Step 5: Exploit periodicity**:
- Because $e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}$, we get $X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$. The spectrum is **$2\pi$-periodic**.

**Why does this matter?**
- It tells us that to fully specify $X(e^{j\omega})$, we need only **one period** (say $[-\pi, \pi]$).
- The inverse transform integrates over any $2\pi$-wide interval.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Essential DTFT Properties

| **Property** | **Time Domain** | **Frequency Domain** |
|---------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Linearity** | $a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]$ | $a\,X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b\,X_2(e^{j\omega})$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x[n]$ | $X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ |
| **Time Reversal** | $x[-n]$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| **Conjugation** | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| **Convolution in Time** | $x[n] * h[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})$ |
| **Multiplication in Time** | $x[n] \cdot y[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\, X(e^{j\omega}) \circledast Y(e^{j\omega})$ |
| **Differentiation in Freq.** | $n\,x[n]$ | $j\, \dfrac{d}{d\omega}\, X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, d\omega$ |
| **Symmetry (Real $x[n]$)** | $x[n] = x^*[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\circledast$ denotes **periodic convolution** over an interval of length $2\pi$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DTFT is the mathematical backbone of:

1. **Digital Filter Design** ŌĆö FIR and IIR filters are characterized by their frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$.
2. **Speech Processing** ŌĆö Vowel recognition relies on the magnitude spectrum of speech segments.
3. **OFDM Communication Systems** ŌĆö Subcarrier allocation uses periodic spectral properties.
4. **Audio Compression (MP3, AAC)** ŌĆö Modified Discrete Cosine Transform (MDCT) is built on DTFT theory.
5. **Spectral Analysis in RADAR & SONAR** ŌĆö Doppler shifts analyzed via DTFT-based spectrum estimation.

---

## 2.4 Convergence Behavior ŌĆö Key Insight

| **Type of Signal** | **DTFT Behavior** |
|---------------------|--------------------|
| Finite-duration, bounded | Uniform convergence |
| Absolutely summable | DTFT exists and is continuous |
| Square summable (finite energy) | DTFT exists in mean-square sense |
| Periodic discrete signal | DTFT is an **impulse train** (not a function) |
| Unit step / non-summable | DTFT contains **generalized functions** (Dirac deltas) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Signals like $u[n]$ (unit step) or $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ do not have ordinary DTFTs. They require **generalized functions (distributions)** ŌĆö you may have to express their DTFT using Dirac delta impulses. This is a frequently tested advanced question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$.

### Proof:

By definition of the DTFT applied to the shifted signal $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

Substitute $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$ and $dn = dm$:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega (m + n_0)}
$$

Split the exponential:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m}
$$

The summation is exactly $X(e^{j\omega})$:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})
$$

**Interpretation:** A time shift of $n_0$ samples multiplies the spectrum by a **linear-phase term** $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ ŌĆö it only modifies the phase, not the magnitude.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Convolution in Time Domain

**Statement:** $x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})$.

### Proof:

Start with the discrete-time convolution definition:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n - k]
$$

Take the DTFT of $y[n]$:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n - k] \right] e^{-j\omega n}
$$

Swap the order of summation (justified by absolute summability):

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n - k]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right]
$$

Apply the time-shifting property (with shift $k$) to the inner sum:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n - k]\, e^{-j\omega n} = e^{-j\omega k}\, H(e^{j\omega})
$$

Substitute back:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, e^{-j\omega k} \cdot H(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})\, H(e^{j\omega})\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parseval's Theorem for DTFT

**Statement:** Energy is conserved between the time domain and frequency domain.

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, d\omega
$$

### Proof:

Start with the right-hand side and use the inverse DTFT:

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, X^*(e^{j\omega})\, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{j\omega n} \right]^* d\omega
$$

Conjugate the bracket and recognize:

$$
= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m]\, e^{-j\omega m}\, d\omega
$$

Swap the integral and sum:

$$
= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m] \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{-j\omega m}\, d\omega \right]
$$

By the inverse DTFT definition, the term in brackets is $x[m]$:

$$
= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m]\, x[m] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[m] \vert^2
$$

**Result proved.** $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** Parseval's theorem allows engineers to compute signal energy in the frequency domain ŌĆö extremely useful when analytic time-domain expressions are difficult.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute the DTFT of $x[n] = a^n\, u[n]$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$.

### Solution:

Apply the forward DTFT:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

Factor out the first term ($n=0$):

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( a\, e^{-j\omega} \right)^n
$$

This is a geometric series. It converges if $\vert a\, e^{-j\omega} \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Magnitude and Phase:**
> - $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$
> - $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a\sin\omega}{1 - a\cos\omega}\right)$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DTFT Analysis-Synthesis Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **complete forward and inverse DTFT pipeline** along with all supporting property transformations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Discrete Time Signal x of n"] --> B["DTFT Analysis Sum"]
    B --> C["Continuous Periodic Spectrum X of e to j omega"]
    C --> D["Property Transformations"]
    D --> E["Modified Spectrum Y of e to j omega"]
    E --> F["Inverse DTFT Integral"]
    F --> G["Reconstructed Signal y of n"]

    H["Time Domain x of n"] --> I["Time Shifting"]
    I --> J["Multiply by e to minus j omega n0"]
    J --> K["Frequency Domain X of e to j omega"]

    L["Frequency Domain X of e to j omega"] --> M["Frequency Shifting"]
    M --> N["Replace omega by omega minus omega0"]
    N --> O["Modulated Signal e to j omega0 n x of n"]

    P["x1 of n and x2 of n"] --> Q["Convolution Operation"]
    Q --> R["Product in Frequency X1 times X2"]

    S["Parsevals Energy Balance"] --> T["Sum of x of n squared"]
    S --> U["One over 2pi times integral of X magnitude squared"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above unifies the **forward mapping**, **inverse mapping**, and the **inter-domain property transformations** that govern the DTFT. Each block corresponds to a mathematical operation defined in the KTU Formula Sheet.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**Q: Define Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT). State the conditions for its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The DTFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

with the inverse:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

**Condition for existence:** A sufficient condition is that $x[n]$ be **absolutely summable**, i.e.,

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty
$$

Equivalently, finite-energy signals ($\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$) have a DTFT that converges in the mean-square sense. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Q: State and prove the time-shifting property of the DTFT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$. **(1 mark)**

**Proof:** Apply the DTFT to $x[n - n_0]$:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

Substitute $m = n - n_0$:

$$
= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega(m + n_0)} = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m} = e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the DTFT of the signal $x[n] = a^n\, u[n]$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$. Plot the magnitude spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the convolution property of the DTFT, find the DTFT of the signal $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ and $h[n] = b^n u[n]$, with $\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert < 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

### Part (a) Solution (7 marks):

Apply the DTFT definition:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Since $\vert a \vert < 1$, this geometric series converges to:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

Express in polar form:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a \cos\omega + j a \sin\omega}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - a\cos\omega)^2 + (a\sin\omega)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Magnitude spectrum:** Peak at $\omega = 0$ (value $= 1/(1-a)$), minimum near $\omega = \pm\pi$ (value $= 1/(1+a)$), periodic with period $2\pi$. **(1 mark for the plot description)**

### Part (b) Solution (7 marks):

By the **convolution property** of the DTFT:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega}) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Substitute the DTFTs computed:

$$
X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}, \quad H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - b e^{-j\omega}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Multiply:

$$
Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - b e^{-j\omega})} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

This is the DTFT of a causal two-pole system. **(1 mark for final boxed answer)**

$$
\boxed{\,Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - b e^{-j\omega})}\,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and prove Parseval's theorem for the DTFT. Discuss its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal has DTFT $X(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$. Find the energy of the signal using Parseval's theorem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Part (a) Solution (7 marks):

**Statement:** For sequences $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ with DTFTs $X(e^{j\omega})$ and $Y(e^{j\omega})$:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, y^*[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, Y^*(e^{j\omega})\, d\omega \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Special case** (when $x[n] = y[n]$, real-valued): The total energy of the signal can be computed as:

$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, d\omega \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

**Proof:** Start with the inverse DTFT representation of $x[n]$:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

Multiply both sides by $x^*[n]$ and sum over $n$:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[n]\, e^{j\omega n} \right] d\omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Recognize the inner sum as $X^*(e^{j\omega})$:

$$
= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, X^*(e^{j\omega})\, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, d\omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**Physical significance:** Energy in the discrete-time domain equals the area under the energy density spectrum $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 / (2\pi)$ over one full period. This enables frequency-domain energy analysis without recovering $x[n]$. **(1 mark)**

### Part (b) Solution (7 marks):

Given $X(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$, the magnitude squared is:

$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot X^*(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega})(1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega})}
$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$
(1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega})(1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega}) = 1 - 0.5(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + 0.25 = 1.25 - \cos\omega \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

So:

$$
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1.25 - \cos\omega} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

Apply Parseval's theorem:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \frac{d\omega}{1.25 - \cos\omega} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

Use the standard integral $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d\theta}{a - b\cos\theta} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{a^2 - b^2}}$ for $a > b > 0$:

Here $a = 1.25$, $b = 1$:

$$
\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \frac{d\omega}{1.25 - \cos\omega} = 2 \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{d\omega}{1.25 - \cos\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{1.25^2 - 1}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

Compute:

$$
\sqrt{1.5625 - 1} = \sqrt{0.5625} = 0.75
$$

So:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{0.75} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E = \dfrac{4}{3} \approx 1.3333 \text{ units of energy}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly use the **DFT Parseval formula** $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert X[k] \vert^2$ instead of the **DTFT Parseval formula** $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2\, d\omega$. The normalization factor is **$1/(2\pi)$**, **not** $1/N$. Loss of 2 marks if confused.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DTFT** maps discrete-time $x[n]$ to a **continuous, $2\pi$-periodic** spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$.
- Existence requires **absolute summability** (sufficient) or **finite energy** (mean-square convergence).
- **DFT = Sampled DTFT:** $X[k] = X(e^{j\omega}) \big\vert_{\omega = 2\pi k / N}$.
- **Time shift** ŌåÆ Linear phase factor $e^{-j\omega n_0}$.
- **Frequency shift** ŌåÆ Modulation by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ in time.
- **Convolution in time** Ōåö **Multiplication in frequency**.
- **Multiplication in time** Ōåö **Periodic convolution in frequency** (over a $2\pi$ interval).
- **Parseval's theorem** uses normalization $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ for the frequency integral.
- For **real signals**, the magnitude spectrum is **even** and the phase spectrum is **odd** (conjugate symmetry).
- **Differentiation in frequency** Ōåö Multiplication by $n$ in time.
- For **periodic signals**, the DTFT is an **impulse train**: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}\sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k / N)$.
- **Key pairing to memorize:** $a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ (valid for $\vert a \vert < 1$).
- **Anti-causal pairing:** $-a^n u[-n-1] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ (valid for $\vert a \vert > 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** For 14-mark questions, expect a 2-property chain (e.g., "use time-shifting followed by convolution"). Practice derivations thoroughly ŌĆö marks are split: **2 marks for statement, 4 marks for derivation, 1 mark for interpretation/result**.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 ŌĆö THE DISCRETE FOURIER TRANSFORM (DFT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT):** The DFT is the primary **numerical, finite-length, and computable** transformation that converts a finite-duration discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ into another finite-length discrete-frequency sequence $X[k]$ of length $N$. It is mathematically defined as the **sampled version of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** at exactly $N$ uniformly spaced angular frequencies $\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$.

The **N-point DFT** of a finite-length sequence $x[n]$, $0 \le n \le N-1$, is defined by:

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

The corresponding **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** that recovers $x[n]$ from $X[k]$ is given by:

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

Both $x[n]$ and $X[k]$ are assumed to be **periodic with period $N$** outside their principal interval, although only one period is retained in practice.

### 1.2 The Twiddle Factor

The complex exponential $e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}k}$ is so fundamental to the DFT that it is given a special symbol called the **twiddle factor**:

$$
W_N = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> Using the twiddle factor, the DFT and IDFT are written compactly as:
> $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} \quad \text{and} \quad x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-kn} $$

The twiddle factor is a **primitive $N^{th}$ root of unity**, satisfying $W_N^N = 1$ and $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ (periodicity).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]  
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Musical Chord Analyzer:** Imagine a guitar string that plays a complex chord. Your ear cannot easily tell which notes (frequencies) are present and how loud each is. The **DFT acts like a perfect "frequency prism"** that takes the chord (a time-domain signal) and breaks it into exactly $N$ distinct frequency "bins" $X[0], X[1], \ldots, X[N-1]$. Each bin tells you the **strength and phase** of a specific frequency component. Unlike a real prism (which uses light), the DFT is purely mathematical and works on any discrete signal ŌĆö be it audio, images, sensor data, or vibration readings. Crucially, since a computer cannot deal with infinite continuous spectra, the DFT gives the computer a **finite, computable "fingerprint"** of the signal.

### 1.4 Why the DFT Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The DFT is the cornerstone of Digital Signal Processing (DSP).** It is the only Fourier transform that a digital computer can compute directly (via the Fast Fourier Transform ŌĆö FFT algorithm). It serves as the bridge between theoretical continuous-time/continuous-frequency analysis and practical discrete-time/discrete-frequency implementation. The KTU 2024 scheme tests DFT computation, properties, and its relationship to other transforms heavily.

### 1.5 Relationship to Other Transforms

| Transform | Domain | Time | Frequency | Computable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fourier Transform (FT) | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | No |
| Fourier Series (FS) | Continuous | Continuous | Discrete | No |
| DTFT | Discrete | Discrete (aperiodic) | Continuous (periodic) | No |
| DFS | Discrete | Discrete (periodic) | Discrete (periodic) | No (ideal) |
| **DFT** | **Discrete** | **Discrete (finite)** | **Discrete (finite)** | **YES** |

The DFT is essentially a **windowed, sampled, and periodized** version of the DTFT, and a **single period** of the DFS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DFT as a Linear Transformation

The DFT can be elegantly viewed as a **matrix multiplication** operation. Defining the $N \times N$ **DFT matrix** $\mathbf{W}$ with elements $W_N^{kn}$:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W} \cdot \mathbf{x}
$$

where $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], x[1], \ldots, x[N-1]]^T$ and $\mathbf{X} = [X[0], X[1], \ldots, X[N-1]]^T$.

$$
\mathbf{W} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & W_N & W_N^2 & \cdots & W_N^{N-1} \\
1 & W_N^2 & W_N^4 & \cdots & W_N^{2(N-1)} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & W_N^{N-1} & W_N^{2(N-1)} & \cdots & W_N^{(N-1)^2}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is **symmetric** and **unitary** (up to a scaling factor of $\sqrt{N}$), which is the key mathematical reason the IDFT works.

## 2.2 Computational Complexity

- **Direct DFT computation:** $O(N^2)$ complex multiplications and $O(N^2)$ complex additions.
- **FFT (Cooley-Tukey, radix-2):** $O(N \log_2 N)$ operations ŌĆö a massive reduction for large $N$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Yield DFT Formulas** ŌĆö memorize these for KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Property | Time Domain $x[n]$ | Frequency Domain $X[k]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DFT Definition** | $x[n]$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$ |
| 2 | **IDFT Definition** | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$ | $X[k]$ |
| 3 | **Linearity** | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1[k] + b X_2[k]$ |
| 4 | **Periodicity** | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | $X[k+N] = X[k]$ |
| 5 | **Circular Time Shift** | $x[\langle n-n_0 \rangle_N]$ | $W_N^{k n_0} X[k]$ |
| 6 | **Circular Frequency Shift** | $W_N^{-k n_0} x[n]$ | $X[\langle k-k_0 \rangle_N]$ |
| 7 | **Time Reversal** | $x[\langle -n \rangle_N]$ | $X[\langle -k \rangle_N]$ |
| 8 | **Conjugate Symmetry (real $x$)** | $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ | $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$, $X[0] \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 9 | **Duality** | $X[n]$ | $N \cdot x[\langle -k \rangle_N]$ |
| 10 | **Circular Convolution** | $x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n]$ | $X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$ |
| 11 | **Multiplication** | $x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | $\frac{1}{N} X_1[k] \circledast X_2[k]$ |
| 12 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key DFT Computations to Remember:**
> - $W_N^k = e^{-j 2\pi k / N} = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi k}{N}\right) - j\sin\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$
> - $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^k$ when $N$ is even
> - $W_N^0 = W_N^N = 1$
> - $W_N^{N/4} = -j$, $W_N^{3N/4} = j$ (for $N$ divisible by 4)
> - $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{kn} = N$ if $n = mN$, else $0$ (orthogonality)

## 2.4 The Importance of the "Modulo-$N$" Operator

> [!WARNING]  
> **Pitfall Alert:** All indices in DFT properties are computed **modulo $N$**, written as $\langle \cdot \rangle_N$ or $(\cdot \mod N)$. For example, $x[\langle -1 \rangle_N] = x[N-1]$, NOT $x[-1]$. This is the **circular** nature of the DFT and is the #1 source of mistakes in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DFT is used pervasively in:

- **Audio processing:** MP3 encoding, equalizers, noise cancellation.
- **Image processing:** JPEG compression, edge detection.
- **Communications:** OFDM modulation in 4G/5G/WiFi.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG spectral analysis for diagnosis.
- **Vibration analysis:** Modal analysis in mechanical/civil engineering.
- **Radar/Sonar:** Doppler frequency estimation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IDFT (Starting from the Forward DFT)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why does the IDFT work?** The derivation hinges on the **orthogonality** of complex exponentials sampled at $N$ discrete frequencies.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the definition of $X[k]$:**

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $W_N^{-km}$ and sum over $k = 0$ to $N-1$:**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-km} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} \right] W_N^{-km}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interchange the order of summation on the right-hand side:**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-km} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(n-m)} \right]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the inner sum using orthogonality:**

Recall the fundamental identity:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(n-m)} = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } n - m = \ell N \text{ (some integer } \ell\text{)} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Since both $n$ and $m$ lie in the range $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, the only way $n - m$ is a multiple of $N$ is when $n = m$. Thus:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(n-m)} = N \cdot \delta[n - m]
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back:**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-km} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot N \cdot \delta[n-m] = N \cdot x[m]
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $x[m]$:**

$$
x[m] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-km}
$$

Renaming $m$ back to $n$ gives the **IDFT** as stated earlier. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Proof of the Periodicity Property

> [!NOTE]  
> We prove that $X[k+N] = X[k]$ for the $N$-point DFT.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write $X[k+N]$ using the DFT definition:**

$$
X[k+N] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{(k+N)n}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the identity $W_N^{Nn} = (e^{-j2\pi/N})^{Nn} = e^{-j2\pi n} = 1$ for all integer $n$:**

$$
X[k+N] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} \cdot W_N^{Nn} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} \cdot 1
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclude:**

$$
X[k+N] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn} = X[k] \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Proof of the Circular Time-Shift Property

> [!NOTE]  
> We prove that if $y[n] = x[\langle n - n_0 \rangle_N]$, then $Y[k] = W_N^{k n_0} X[k]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define $y[n] = x[(n - n_0) \mod N]$.** The sum is over $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, and modulo-$N$ arithmetic maps the shifted indices back into this range.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $Y[k]$:**

$$
Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[\langle n - n_0 \rangle_N] \, W_N^{kn}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $m = \langle n - n_0 \rangle_N$, so $n = \langle m + n_0 \rangle_N$.** As $n$ ranges over $0$ to $N-1$, $m$ also ranges over $0$ to $N-1$ (just a permutation).

$$
Y[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, W_N^{k(m + n_0)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Split the exponent:**

$$
Y[k] = W_N^{k n_0} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, W_N^{km} = W_N^{k n_0} X[k] \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: 4-Point DFT of a Rectangular Pulse

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Practice this exact style of problem** ŌĆö it is the most common KTU question type.

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point DFT of $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 ŌĆö Identify $N = 4$ and compute $W_4 = e^{-j2\pi/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$.

Step 2 ŌĆö The powers of $W_4$:

$$
W_4^0 = 1, \quad W_4^1 = -j, \quad W_4^2 = -1, \quad W_4^3 = +j
$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the DFT formula $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_4^{kn}$:

For $k = 0$:

$$
X[0] = x[0](1) + x[1](1) + x[2](1) + x[3](1) = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4
$$

For $k = 1$:

$$
X[1] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-j) + 1 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot (j) = 1 - j - 1 + j = 0
$$

For $k = 2$:

$$
X[2] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot (1) + 1 \cdot (-1) = 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 = 0
$$

For $k = 3$:

$$
X[3] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (j) + 1 \cdot (-1) + 1 \cdot (-j) = 1 + j - 1 - j = 0
$$

**Result:** $X[k] = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$.

**Verification (IDFT):** Since all $X[k]$ are zero except $X[0] = 4$, the IDFT gives $x[n] = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 4 = 1$ for all $n$ ŌĆö consistent with the original signal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Circular Convolution via DFT

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of $x_1[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö DFT of $x_1[n]$:**

Using the DFT matrix, $X_1[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x_1[n] W_4^{kn}$:

$$
X_1[0] = 1+2+3+4 = 10
$$

$$
X_1[1] = 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j
$$

$$
X_1[2] = 1 + 2(-1) + 3(1) + 4(-1) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2
$$

$$
X_1[3] = 1 + 2(j) + 3(-1) + 4(-j) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j
$$

So $X_1[k] = \{10, \, -2+2j, \, -2, \, -2-2j\}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö DFT of $x_2[n]$** (computed similarly):

$$
X_2[0] = 4+3+2+1 = 10
$$

$$
X_2[1] = 4 + 3(-j) + 2(-1) + 1(j) = 4 - 3j - 2 + j = 2 - 2j
$$

$$
X_2[2] = 4 - 3 + 2 - 1 = 2
$$

$$
X_2[3] = 4 + 3(j) - 2 - j = 2 + 2j
$$

So $X_2[k] = \{10, \, 2-2j, \, 2, \, 2+2j\}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply $X_3[k] = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k]$:**

$$
X_3[0] = 10 \cdot 10 = 100
$$

$$
X_3[1] = (-2+2j)(2-2j) = -4 + 4j + 4j - 4j^2 = -4 + 8j + 4 = 8j
$$

$$
X_3[2] = (-2)(2) = -4
$$

$$
X_3[3] = (-2-2j)(2+2j) = -4 - 4j - 4j - 4j^2 = -4 - 8j + 4 = -8j
$$

So $X_3[k] = \{100, \, 8j, \, -4, \, -8j\}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö IDFT to get circular convolution $x_3[n] = x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n]$:**

$$
x_3[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X_3[k] W_4^{-kn}
$$

For $n=0$:

$$
x_3[0] = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 8j - 4 - 8j) = \frac{96}{4} = 24
$$

For $n=1$:

$$
x_3[1] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 + 8j(j) - 4(-1) - 8j(-j)\right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 - 8 + 4 - 8\right] = \frac{88}{4} = 22
$$

For $n=2$:

$$
x_3[2] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 + 8j(-1) - 4(1) - 8j(-1)\right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 - 8j - 4 + 8j\right] = \frac{96}{4} = 24
$$

For $n=3$:

$$
x_3[3] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 + 8j(-j) - 4(-1) - 8j(j)\right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[100 + 8 + 4 + 8\right] = \frac{120}{4} = 30
$$

**Result:** $x_3[n] = x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n] = \{24, 22, 24, 30\}$. $\blacksquare$

**Verification by direct circular convolution formula** (graphical method):

$$
x_3[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{3} x_1[m] \cdot x_2[\langle n-m \rangle_4]
$$

For $n=0$: $x_3[0] = x_1[0]x_2[0] + x_1[1]x_2[3] + x_1[2]x_2[2] + x_1[3]x_2[1] = 1(4)+2(1)+3(2)+4(3) = 4+2+6+12 = 24$. Ō£ō

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö DFT Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sequence x n of length N] --> B[Store in Buffer 0 to N-1]
    B --> C[Compute Twiddle Factors W_N to the kn]
    C --> D[Multiply and Accumulate for each k]
    D --> E[Output X k for k equals 0 to N-1]
    E --> F{Application}
    F --> G[Spectral Analysis]
    F --> H[Filtering via Convolution]
    F --> I[Compression JPEG / MP3]
    F --> J[OFDM in 5G / WiFi]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#f0e1ff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö IDFT Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input X k of length N] --> Q[Store X k Buffer]
    Q --> R[Compute W_N to the minus kn]
    R --> S[Multiply X k by Twiddle and Sum]
    S --> T[Divide Sum by N]
    T --> U[Output x n for n equals 0 to N-1]
    
    style P fill:#ffe1e1
    style U fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.3 Architecture ŌĆö Relationship Between FT Family

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FT[Fourier Transform\nContinuous Time / Continuous Freq] -->|Sample in time| FS[Fourier Series\nContinuous Time / Discrete Freq]
    FT -->|Sample in freq| DTFT[DTFT\nDiscrete Time / Continuous Freq]
    FS -->|Sample in time| DTFS[DTFS or DFS\nDiscrete Periodic Both]
    DTFT -->|Sample in freq| DTFS
    DTFT -->|Window in time| DFS[DFT\nFinite Length Sampled]
    DFS --> DFT[DFT Computable on Digital Hardware]
    
    style FT fill:#ffcccc
    style DTFT fill:#ccffcc
    style DFS fill:#ccccff
    style DFT fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.4 Butterfly-Style Operation (Conceptual 4-Point DFT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x0[x0] --> M00[Multiply by W to the 0 equals 1]
    x1[x1] --> M10[Multiply by W to the k]
    x2[x2] --> M20[Multiply by W to the 2k]
    x3[x3] --> M30[Multiply by W to the 3k]
    M00 --> S[Sum]
    M10 --> S
    M20 --> S
    M30 --> S
    S --> Xk[Output Xk]
    
    style x0 fill:#cce5ff
    style x1 fill:#cce5ff
    style x2 fill:#cce5ff
    style x3 fill:#cce5ff
    style Xk fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note:** The 4-point DFT requires computing 4 such sums, each involving 4 complex multiplications and 3 complex additions ŌĆö totaling $N^2 = 16$ multiplications and $N(N-1) = 12$ additions for $N=4$. The FFT reduces this to $N \log_2 N = 8$ multiplications.
