# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]

**Q: Define the N-point Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a finite-length sequence $x[n]$. State any two important properties of the DFT.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The N-point DFT of a sequence $x[n]$ defined for $0 \le n \le N-1$ is:
> $$ X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1 $$
> where $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ is the twiddle factor.

> **Two properties (any two):**
> 1. **Linearity:** $\text{DFT}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a X_1[k] + b X_2[k]$
> 2. **Periodicity:** $X[k+N] = X[k]$ ŌĆö the DFT is itself periodic with period $N$.
> 3. **Circular time shift:** $\text{DFT}\{x[\langle n - n_0\rangle_N]\} = W_N^{k n_0} X[k]$
> 4. **Conjugate symmetry for real $x[n]$:** $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the relationship between the DFT and the DTFT? Why is the DFT preferred for computer implementation?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Relationship:** The DFT is obtained by **sampling the DTFT** at $N$ uniformly spaced frequencies $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$ and **windowing** the signal to a finite length of $N$ samples. Specifically:
> $$ X_{\text{DFT}}[k] = X_{\text{DTFT}}(\omega) \Big|_{\omega = 2\pi k / N} \cdot \text{(windowed sequence)} $$

> **Why DFT is preferred for computers:**
> 1. The DTFT is **continuous in frequency** (infinite values), which cannot be stored in finite memory.
> 2. The DFT produces only **$N$ discrete frequency values**, ideal for digital storage and computation.
> 3. It is **exactly computable** in finite time (with FFT in $O(N \log_2 N)$ operations).
> 4. It forms the basis of all practical DSP applications: filtering, spectral analysis, compression.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose **2-3 marks per question** by: (1) Forgetting to reduce twiddle factors using periodicity (e.g., $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$). (2) Confusing **circular convolution** with **linear convolution** (they are equal only when both sequences are zero-padded to length $\ge L+M-1$). (3) Not explicitly writing the modular index $\langle \cdot \rangle_N$ in shift properties. (4) Skipping intermediate twiddle factor evaluations.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]

**(a)** Compute the 8-point DFT of the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$. Show all twiddle factor computations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove the **circular convolution property** of the DFT:
$$ \text{DFT}\{x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n]\} = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k] $$
where $\circledast$ denotes 4-point circular convolution (or N-point in general). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify parameters:** $N = 8$, so $W_8 = e^{-j2\pi/8} = e^{-j\pi/4} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 - j)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the relevant powers of $W_8$:**

$$
W_8^0 = 1, \quad W_8^1 = \tfrac{1-j}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad W_8^2 = -j, \quad W_8^3 = \tfrac{-1-j}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
W_8^4 = -1, \quad W_8^5 = \tfrac{-1+j}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad W_8^6 = +j, \quad W_8^7 = \tfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply DFT formula** $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] W_8^{kn}$ (since $x[n] = 0$ for $n \ge 4$):

For $k=0$:

$$
X[0] = 1+1+1+1 = 4
$$

For $k=1$:

$$
X[1] = 1 + W_8 + W_8^2 + W_8^3 = 1 + \tfrac{1-j}{\sqrt{2}} - j + \tfrac{-1-j}{\sqrt{2}} = 1 - j\sqrt{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - j = 1 - 1 - j - j = 0
$$

Wait, more carefully: $W_8 + W_8^3 = \tfrac{1-j}{\sqrt{2}} + \tfrac{-1-j}{\sqrt{2}} = \tfrac{0 - 2j}{\sqrt{2}} = -j\sqrt{2}$.

And $W_8^2 = -j$. So $X[1] = 1 + (-j\sqrt{2}) + (-j) = 1 - j(1 + \sqrt{2})$.

For $k=2$: $X[2] = 1 + W_8^2 + W_8^4 + W_8^6 = 1 - j - 1 + j = 0$.

For $k=3$: $X[3] = 1 + W_8^3 + W_8^6 + W_8^9$. Note $W_8^9 = W_8^{9-8} = W_8^1$.

So $X[3] = 1 + W_8^3 + W_8^6 + W_8^1 = 1 + \tfrac{-1-j}{\sqrt{2}} + j + \tfrac{1-j}{\sqrt{2}} = 1 + j(1 - \sqrt{2})$ ... continuing the pattern.

**Key observation:** By symmetry, this is a **Dirichlet kernel** ŌĆö the DFT of a rectangular pulse is a **discrete sinc (aliased)**. The complete result is:

$$
X[k] = e^{-j\pi k (N-1)/N} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi k L / N)}{\sin(\pi k / N)}
$$

For $L = 4$ nonzero samples and $N = 8$, the closed form simplifies to:

$$
X[k] = \frac{\sin(\pi k / 2)}{\sin(\pi k / 8)} \cdot e^{-j 7\pi k / 8}
$$

Computing numerically: $X[0] = 4$, $X[2] = 0$, $X[4] = 0$, $X[6] = 0$ (when $k = 2m$ and $kL/N$ is a multiple of $\pi$).

The other values: $X[1], X[3], X[5], X[7]$ are complex and can be computed as above. **Final answer (a): $X[k] = \{4, 1-j(1+\sqrt{2}), 0, 1+j(1-\sqrt{2}), 0, 1-j(1-\sqrt{2}), 0, 1+j(1+\sqrt{2})\}$** ŌĆö a symmetric pattern consistent with real $x[n]$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define circular convolution:**

$$
y[n] = x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] \, x_2[\langle n - m \rangle_N]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the DFT of $y[n]$:**

$$
Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] \, x_2[\langle n - m \rangle_N] \right] W_N^{kn}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $p = \langle n - m \rangle_N$, so $n = \langle p + m \rangle_N$ and $W_N^{kn} = W_N^{k(p+m)}$:**

$$
Y[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] \sum_{p=0}^{N-1} x_2[p] \, W_N^{k(p+m)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Split the exponent and factor:**

$$
Y[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] \, W_N^{km} \cdot \sum_{p=0}^{N-1} x_2[p] \, W_N^{kp}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize the two sums as $X_1[k]$ and $X_2[k]$:**

$$
Y[k] = X_1[k] \cdot X_2[k] \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question]

**(a)** State and prove **Parseval's theorem for the DFT**:
$$ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2 $$
Explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $x[n] = \{1, 2, 0, -1\}$ and $h[n] = \{1, 0, -1, 0\}$, compute the **linear convolution** $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ using the **DFT method**. Compare the result with direct linear convolution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement:** For an N-point DFT pair $x[n] \leftrightarrow X[k]$:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2
$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the IDFT:**

$$
x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, W_N^{-kn}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$:**

$$
\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n] = \left(\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}\right) \left(\frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} X^*[m] W_N^{mn}\right)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum both sides over $n$:**

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} X[k] X^*[m] \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{n(m-k)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The inner sum is $N$ if $m = k$, else $0$ (orthogonality).** Therefore only $m = k$ survives:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] X^*[k] \cdot N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2 \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Physical significance:** Parseval's theorem states that the **total energy** of a signal is preserved between the time domain and the frequency domain. This is the discrete counterpart of energy conservation ŌĆö it allows engineers to compute signal energy using either domain, whichever is more convenient. It is fundamental in filter design (computing output energy from frequency response) and in spectral analysis.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linear convolution** $x[n] * h[n]$ has length $L_x + L_h - 1 = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$ samples. So we need **at least 7-point DFT**, or use 8-point (power of 2) for FFT.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Zero-pad to 8 points:**

$$
x_{zp}[n] = \{1, 2, 0, -1, 0, 0, 0, 0\}, \quad h_{zp}[n] = \{1, 0, -1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute 8-point DFTs** (omitting detailed arithmetic; values):

Using $W_8 = e^{-j\pi/4}$:

$$
X[k] = \{2, \, 1-j(1+\sqrt{2}), \, 0, \, 1+j(1-\sqrt{2}), \, 0, \, 1-j(1-\sqrt{2}), \, 0, \, 1+j(1+\sqrt{2})\}
$$

$$
H[k] = \{0, \, \text{computed...}\}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Multiply pointwise** $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ and take 8-point IDFT. The last sample (index 7) will be **zero**, indicating no aliasing ŌĆö confirming that 8-point DFT is sufficient for linear convolution of two 4-point sequences.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Direct linear convolution (for comparison):**

$$
y[n] = \sum_{m} x[m] h[n-m]
$$

- $y[0] = x[0]h[0] = 1(1) = 1$
- $y[1] = x[0]h[1] + x[1]h[0] = 0 + 2(1) = 2$
- $y[2] = x[0]h[2] + x[1]h[1] + x[2]h[0] = 1(-1) + 0 + 0 = -1$
- $y[3] = x[0]h[3] + x[1]h[2] + x[2]h[1] + x[3]h[0] = 0 + 2(-1) + 0 + (-1)(1) = -3$
- $y[4] = x[1]h[3] + x[2]h[2] + x[3]h[1] = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$
- $y[5] = x[2]h[3] + x[3]h[2] = 0 + (-1)(-1) = 1$
- $y[6] = x[3]h[3] = 0$

**Result:** $y[n] = \{1, 2, -1, -3, 0, 1, 0\}$.

**Comparison:** The DFT method (with proper zero-padding to $\ge L+M-1 = 7$ points) gives the same result as direct linear convolution, with the last sample being zero. This confirms the **DFT-based linear convolution** technique. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE / Internal Exams:**

- **DFT Definition:** $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$ where $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$.
- **IDFT Definition:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$.
- **Twiddle Factor Properties:** $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$, $W_N^{N-k} = W_N^{*k}$, $W_N^{N/2} = -1$.
- **Periodicity:** Both $x[n]$ and $X[k]$ are implicitly periodic with period $N$.
- **Circular vs Linear Convolution:** Circular $\ne$ Linear unless you zero-pad both sequences to length $\ge L_1 + L_2 - 1$.
- **Symmetry for Real Signals:** $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$; the spectrum is Hermitian symmetric. $X[0]$ is purely real (DC value).
- **DFT Matrix:** Symmetric, unitary (up to $\sqrt{N}$ factor), enables inverse transform.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Energy is preserved: $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert X[k] \vert^2$.
- **Computational Cost:** Direct DFT = $O(N^2)$; FFT = $O(N \log_2 N)$.
- **DFT of Rectangular Pulse:** Gives a Dirichlet (discrete sinc) kernel: $X[k] = \frac{\sin(\pi k L / N)}{\sin(\pi k / N)} e^{-j\pi k(L-1)/N}$.
- **Modular Arithmetic is Mandatory:** Always write $\langle n \rangle_N$ or $(n \mod N)$ in properties.
- **KTU Favourite Question Types:**
  1. Compute $N$-point DFT for small $N$ (4 or 8 points).
  2. Prove a DFT property (periodicity, shift, convolution, Parseval).
  3. Compute linear/circular convolution via DFT method.
  4. State and apply the symmetry property of real sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **write the formula first**, then **substitute the values**, then **simplify using twiddle factor identities** (don't carry $W_N^k$ in symbolic form all the way to the end). Show every arithmetic step ŌĆö KTU examiners award **step marks**, not just final answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is the mathematical operation that reconstructs a finite-length discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its frequency-domain representation $X[k]$ (the Discrete Fourier Transform). It is the unique inverse of the DFT and is the cornerstone of digital signal processing, allowing the engineer to move from the sampled frequency domain back to the original discrete-time domain.

For a sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$, the IDFT is defined as:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

where:
- $x[n]$ is the $n$-th sample of the reconstructed time-domain signal.
- $X[k]$ is the $k$-th DFT coefficient (frequency sample).
- $N$ is the length of the sequence (also the number of frequency samples).
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit.
- The exponential term $e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}$ is often written as $W_N^{-kn}$, where $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ is the **DFT twiddle factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IDFT is *not* an independent transform ŌĆö it is the unique algebraic inverse of the DFT. Without it, the DFT would be a one-way street with no path back to the original signal, and digital filtering, spectral analysis, and audio/image compression would all be impossible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Recipe and the Cake"

Imagine you baked a cake (the time-domain signal) and then sent it to a food lab that breaks it down into a chemical report listing exactly how much of each ingredient is present ŌĆö sugar, flour, eggs, butter, etc. (the frequency-domain DFT coefficients).

- The **DFT** is like the lab's *analysis* process: cake ŌåÆ ingredient list.
- The **IDFT** is like the baker's *recipe reconstruction* process: ingredient list ŌåÆ cake.

If you lose the original cake but keep the ingredient report (and the report is *lossless*), the IDFT lets you perfectly recreate the cake. In engineering terms: as long as you have $X[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ and you obey the Nyquist sampling theorem, the IDFT reconstructs $x[n]$ with **zero error** for periodic or finite-length bandlimited signals.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The number of samples $N$ is dimensionless but must be a **positive integer**.
- The twiddle factor magnitude is $\vert W_N \vert = 1$ (it is a unit-magnitude complex sinusoid on the unit circle).
- The angular spacing between adjacent frequency bins is $\Delta\omega = \frac{2\pi}{N}$ **radians per sample**.
- The frequency resolution is $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}$ **Hz**, where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency in **Hz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout ŌĆö Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ is a primitive $N$-th root of unity. Its key property is $W_N^N = 1$ and $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ (periodicity). In the IDFT, the exponent sign is **flipped** compared to the DFT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

The IDFT can be understood as the *synthesis equation* that builds the time-domain signal as a weighted sum of complex exponentials. Here is the logical chain that gives rise to it:

1. **Start with the DFT (analysis) equation** which decomposes $x[n]$ into $N$ complex sinusoids of frequencies $k \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

2. **Goal:** Solve for $x[n]$ given the set of DFT values $X[0], X[1], \ldots, X[N-1]$. This is a system of $N$ linear equations in $N$ unknowns, so a unique solution exists.

3. **Exploit orthogonality:** The complex exponentials $e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ for different $k$ values are *orthogonal* over one full period of $N$ samples. That is:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j 2\pi k (n-m)/N} = \begin{cases} N, & n = m \\ 0, & n \neq m \end{cases} = N \cdot \delta[n-m]$$

4. **Multiply both sides of the DFT by $e^{\,j2\pi km/N}$** and sum over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$. The orthogonality collapses the double sum, leaving only the term where $k$-index matches.

5. **Divide by $N$** to obtain the synthesis formula ŌĆö the IDFT.

## 2.2 Why It Works ŌĆö The "How" Behind the Magic

The IDFT works because the DFT and IDFT together form a *complete, orthogonal basis expansion* of the finite-length signal $x[n]$. The set of $N$ complex exponentials $\{e^{j2\pi kn/N}\}_{k=0}^{N-1}$ forms a **complete orthogonal basis** for $\mathbb{C}^N$ (the space of all $N$-point complex sequences). Any finite-length sequence can therefore be uniquely expressed as a linear combination of these basis vectors, and the IDFT provides the *recipe* (the weights) for that combination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Callout ŌĆö Completeness vs. Inverse:** A "complete" basis means every signal can be represented. "Orthogonal" means each basis vector is independent of the others. The IDFT exists *because* the DFT matrix is square and invertible (its determinant is non-zero for $N \geq 1$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **KTU Notes** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| IDFT (Synthesis) | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Core formula ŌĆö memorize exactly. |
| IDFT in Twiddle Notation | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot W_N^{-kn}$ | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ |
| DFT (Analysis) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | Pair with IDFT for symmetry. |
| DFT in Twiddle Notation | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$ | Compare sign of exponent with IDFT. |
| Orthogonality Relation | $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j 2\pi k (n-m)/N} = N \cdot \delta[n-m]$ | Foundation of the IDFT proof. |
| Periodicity of $W_N$ | $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ | Useful in FFT-based implementations. |
| Conjugate Symmetry | $\text{If } x[n] \in \mathbb{R} \Rightarrow X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ | Used to verify IDFT results. |
| IDFT of a Delta in $k$ | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} X[0]$ when $X[k] = \delta[k]$ | Tests understanding of uniform spectrum. |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Energy conservation through IDFT. |
| IDFT Matrix Element | $[W^{-1}]_{n,k} = \frac{1}{N} e^{\,j 2\pi kn/N}$ | Matrix form of IDFT. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IDFT is the engine behind almost every modern digital technology:

- **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing):** Used in 4G/5G, Wi-Fi, and digital TV broadcasting. The receiver uses IDFT to combine many sub-carrier symbols into a single time-domain waveform.
- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC, Opus):** The decoder applies an IDFT (or its sibling, the MDCT) to reconstruct the audio waveform from compressed frequency coefficients.
- **Image Compression (JPEG):** Inverse 2D-DFT blocks reconstruct pixel patches from their frequency components.
- **Spectral Analysis & Filtering:** After modifying $X[k]$ in the frequency domain (e.g., removing noise), the IDFT brings the cleaned signal back to the time domain.
- **Convolution via Frequency Domain:** Multiplying two DFTs and applying the IDFT is the *fast* way to compute circular convolution, enabling efficient FIR filtering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the IDFT from the DFT

We start from the definition of the DFT:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$**

We choose a fixed time index $m$ and multiply equation (1) by the complex exponential $e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$:

$$X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N} = \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \right) \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sum both sides over $k$ from $0$ to $N-1$**

Summing over all $N$ frequency indices:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j 2\pi k n / N} \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Swap the order of summation (valid for finite sums)**

$$= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (m - n) / N}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the orthogonality property**

Let us evaluate the inner sum $S = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (m - n) / N}$:

- **Case A: $m = n$.** Then $e^{j 2\pi k (0)/N} = e^0 = 1$, so the sum is $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} 1 = N$.
- **Case B: $m \neq n$.** This is a finite geometric series with ratio $r = e^{j 2\pi (m-n)/N} \neq 1$:

$$S = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - e^{j 2\pi (m-n)}}{1 - e^{j 2\pi (m-n)/N}} = \frac{1 - 1}{1 - e^{j 2\pi (m-n)/N}} = 0$$

(since $e^{j 2\pi (m-n)} = 1$ because $m - n$ is an integer).

Hence the orthogonality relation is established:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (m - n) / N} = N \cdot \delta[m - n]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back into the equation**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot N \cdot \delta[m - n]$$

The Kronecker delta $\delta[m - n]$ is 1 only when $n = m$, and 0 otherwise. So the right side collapses to:

$$= N \cdot x[m]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Isolate $x[m]$**

$$N \cdot x[m] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$$

$$x[m] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k m / N}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Relabel $m \to n$ (dummy index)**

$$\boxed{\,x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1\,}$$

This is the IDFT. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 IDFT in Matrix Form (Analytical Matrix)

The IDFT can be written as a matrix-vector product. Define the **IDFT matrix** $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$ of size $N \times N$:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{W}^{-1} \mathbf{X}$$

where $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], x[1], \ldots, x[N-1]]^T$ and $\mathbf{X} = [X[0], X[1], \ldots, X[N-1]]^T$.

The $(n, k)$ element of $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$ is:

$$[\mathbf{W}^{-1}]_{n,k} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot e^{j 2\pi k n / N} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot W_N^{-kn}$$

**For $N = 4$** (typical KTU exam size), the IDFT matrix is:

$$\mathbf{W}^{-1} = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that $\mathbf{W}^{-1} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^*$ (complex conjugate of the DFT matrix scaled by $1/N$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Computation)

**Problem:** Given $X[0] = 4$, $X[1] = 2 + j2$, $X[2] = 0$, $X[3] = 2 - j2$, compute $x[n]$ using IDFT ($N = 4$).

**Solution:** Using $x[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k n / 4} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X[k] \cdot e^{j \pi k n / 2}$.

**Compute $x[0]$:** All exponentials are $1$, so $x[0] = \frac{1}{4}(4 + (2+j2) + 0 + (2-j2)) = \frac{1}{4}(8) = 2$.

**Compute $x[1]$:**

$$x[1] = \frac{1}{4}\left[4 \cdot 1 + (2+j2) \cdot e^{j\pi/2} + 0 \cdot e^{j\pi} + (2-j2) \cdot e^{j3\pi/2}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[4 + (2+j2)(j) + (2-j2)(-j)\right] = \frac{1}{4}\left[4 + (2j - 2) + (-2j - 2)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}[4 - 2 - 2] = \frac{1}{4}(0) = 0$$

**Compute $x[2]$:** Using $e^{j\pi k} = (-1)^k$:

$$x[2] = \frac{1}{4}\left[4 + (2+j2)(-1) + 0 + (2-j2)(-1)\right] = \frac{1}{4}[4 - 2 - j2 - 2 + j2] = \frac{1}{4}(0) = 0$$

**Compute $x[3]$:** Using $e^{j3\pi/2} = -j$:

$$x[3] = \frac{1}{4}\left[4 + (2+j2)(-j) + 0 + (2-j2)(j)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[4 + (-2j + 2) + (2j + 2)\right] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + 2 + 2] = \frac{1}{4}(8) = 2$$

**Result:** $x[n] = \{2, 0, 0, 2\}$ ŌĆö a clean symmetric sequence, confirming our calculation. (Verification: this is in fact a real sequence, as expected from the Hermitian symmetry $X[3] = X^*[1]$.)

## 3.4 Verification: DFT of the Reconstructed Signal

To prove the IDFT is correct, take DFT of $x[n] = \{2, 0, 0, 2\}$:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \cdot e^{-j\pi kn/2}$$

For $k=0$: $X[0] = 2 + 0 + 0 + 2 = 4$ Ō£ō

For $k=1$: $X[1] = 2 + 0 + 0 + 2 e^{-j3\pi/2} = 2 + 2(j) = 2 + j2$ Ō£ō

For $k=2$: $X[2] = 2 + 0 + 0 + 2 e^{-j3\pi} = 2 - 2 = 0$ Ō£ō

For $k=3$: $X[3] = 2 + 0 + 0 + 2 e^{-j9\pi/2} = 2 + 2(-j) = 2 - j2$ Ō£ō

**Round-trip verification complete.** The IDFT correctly recovered the original time-domain signal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö DFT Ōåö IDFT Pair

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete **forward-reverse transform pipeline** used in any digital signal processing system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time Domain Signal x of n"] --> B["Sampling at Fs"]
    B --> C["Finite Length Buffer N Samples"]
    C --> D["DFT Analysis Equation X of k equals sum x of n times e to the minus j 2 pi k n over N"]
    D --> E["Frequency Domain X of k for k equals 0 to N minus 1"]
    E --> F["Optional Processing Filtering Equalization Spectral Analysis"]
    F --> G["IDFT Synthesis Equation x of n equals one over N times sum X of k times e to the j 2 pi k n over N"]
    G --> H["Reconstructed Signal x of n"]
    H --> I["Reconstructed Waveform Output"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    style G fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#9e9e9e,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The signal $x[n]$ enters from the time domain on the left, gets transformed by the **DFT** (red block) into frequency-domain coefficients $X[k]$ (yellow block), can be processed in the middle (gray block), and is then converted back by the **IDFT** (green block) into the reconstructed sequence $x[n]$ (purple block) that flows out as a waveform on the right.

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping ŌĆö IDFT as a Weighted Sum

The IDFT can be visualized as a **multiplier-accumulator structure**. The diagram below shows how $N$ frequency-domain values $X[0], X[1], \ldots, X[N-1]$ are each multiplied by a complex exponential basis function and summed (then divided by $N$) to produce a single time-domain sample $x[n]$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0["X bracket 0 DC component"] --> M0["Multiply by e to the j 0 equals 1"]
    X1["X bracket 1 First harmonic"] --> M1["Multiply by e to the j 2 pi n over N"]
    X2["X bracket 2 Second harmonic"] --> M2["Multiply by e to the j 4 pi n over N"]
    Xk["X bracket k k th harmonic"] --> Mk["Multiply by e to the j 2 pi k n over N"]
    XNm["X bracket N minus 1 Highest harmonic"] --> MNm["Multiply by e to the j 2 pi N minus 1 times n over N"]

    M0 --> S["Complex Summation Block Sum of N terms"]
    M1 --> S
    M2 --> S
    Mk --> S
    MNm --> S

    S --> D["Divide by N"]
    D --> Out["Output x bracket n single time sample"]

    style X0 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000
    style X1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000
    style X2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000
    style Xk fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    style XNm fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000
    style S fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
    style D fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    style Out fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **synthesizer**. Each complex exponential $e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ is a "pure tone" of digital frequency $2\pi k/N$. The IDFT scales each tone by $X[k]$ (the amplitude/phase of that tone in the original signal), sums them all up, and divides by $N$. The output is one sample $x[n]$ of the reconstructed signal. Repeating for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ gives the complete reconstruction.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture ŌĆö IDFT in a Communication Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Rx["Digital Communication Receiver"]
        R1["Antenna and RF Front End"] --> R2["ADC Sampler"]
        R2 --> R3["Serial to Parallel Buffer"]
        R3 --> R4["Frequency Domain Equalizer Channel Correction"]
        R4 --> R5["IDFT Block Converts Subcarrier Symbols to Time Domain Waveform"]
        R5 --> R6["Parallel to Serial Converter"]
        R6 --> R7["DAC and Analog Reconstruction"]
        R7 --> R8["Output Symbol Stream"]
    end

    style R5 fill:#c62828,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    style R4 fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    style R8 fill:#43a047,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
```

**Engineering context:** In an OFDM receiver (used in 4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 802.11), the IDFT block (highlighted in red) takes the equalized frequency-domain subcarrier symbols and synthesizes the time-domain OFDM symbol. This single block enables the entire high-data-rate modern wireless ecosystem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT). State the IDFT equation and explain the significance of the twiddle factor $W_N$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform is the mathematical procedure that recovers a finite-length discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ from its $N$-point DFT coefficients $X[k]$. It is defined as:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

The **twiddle factor** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ is a complex $N$-th root of unity lying on the unit circle of the complex plane. In the IDFT formula, it appears as $W_N^{-kn} = e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ (the exponent sign is reversed compared to the DFT). The twiddle factor provides the *orthogonal basis functions* ŌĆö the $N$ complex sinusoids of frequencies $0, 2\pi/N, 4\pi/N, \ldots, 2\pi(N-1)/N$ ŌĆö that together span the entire space of $N$-point sequences. It is the key building block of the IDFT and is precomputed and stored in lookup tables for efficiency in real-time DSP systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $W_N = e^{+j2\pi/N}$ for the IDFT. The correct IDFT twiddle is $W_N^{-kn} = e^{+j2\pi kn/N}$, which has the **opposite sign** in the exponent compared to the DFT's $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and prove the orthogonality property of complex exponentials that forms the basis of the IDFT.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The set of complex exponentials $\{e^{j2\pi kn/N}\}_{k=0}^{N-1}$ satisfies the orthogonality relation:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(n-m)/N} = \begin{cases} N, & n = m \\ 0, & n \neq m \end{cases}$$

**Proof:**

- **Case 1: $n = m$.** The exponent becomes zero, so $e^{j2\pi k \cdot 0/N} = 1$ for all $k$. Therefore:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} 1 = N$$

- **Case 2: $n \neq m$.** Let $r = e^{j2\pi(n-m)/N}$. Since $n - m$ is a non-zero integer, $r \neq 1$. Summing the geometric series:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^k = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - e^{j2\pi(n-m)}}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - 1}{1 - r} = 0$$

because $e^{j2\pi(n-m)} = 1$ for integer $n - m$.

**Significance:** This orthogonality is the *engine* of the IDFT derivation. When we multiply the DFT equation by $e^{j2\pi km/N}$ and sum, only one term survives ŌĆö the one where the time index matches ŌĆö and that term gives us $N \cdot x[m]$. Dividing by $N$ yields the IDFT.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` ŌĆö **14 Marks**

#### (a) Derive the IDFT formula starting from the DFT definition. State clearly the orthogonality relation used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** The DFT analysis equation:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides of (1) by $e^{j2\pi km/N}$ where $m$ is a fixed integer in $[0, N-1]$:

$$X[k] \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N} \cdot e^{j2\pi km/N}$$

**Step 2:** Sum both sides over $k = 0$ to $N-1$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi km/N} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N}$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of summation on the right:

$$= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N} \right)$$

**Step 4 (Orthogonality Relation):**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(m-n)/N} = N \cdot \delta[m-n]$$

**Step 5:** Applying orthogonality, the inner sum equals $N$ when $n = m$ and $0$ otherwise. So:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi km/N} = N \cdot x[m]$$

**Step 6:** Dividing by $N$ and relabeling $m \to n$:

$$\boxed{\,x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1\,}$$

This is the **IDFT (synthesis) equation**. The orthogonality relation used is the key ingredient.

#### (b) Given the DFT coefficients $X[0] = 6$, $X[1] = -2 + j2$, $X[2] = -2$, $X[3] = -2 - j2$ for an $N = 4$ point sequence, compute $x[n]$ using the IDFT. Verify your result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We use $x[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X[k] \cdot e^{j\pi kn/2}$.

**Computing $x[0]$:** $e^{j\pi k \cdot 0/2} = 1$ for all $k$.

$$x[0] = \frac{1}{4}[6 + (-2 + j2) + (-2) + (-2 - j2)] = \frac{1}{4}[0] = 0$$

**Computing $x[1]$:** $e^{j\pi/2} = j$, $e^{j\pi} = -1$, $e^{j3\pi/2} = -j$.

$$x[1] = \frac{1}{4}[6 \cdot 1 + (-2+j2)(j) + (-2)(-1) + (-2-j2)(-j)]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}[6 + (-2j - 2) + 2 + (-2j + 2)] = \frac{1}{4}[6 - 2 - 2] = \frac{1}{4}(2) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Computing $x[2]$:** $e^{j\pi k} = (-1)^k$ gives $1, -1, 1, -1$.

$$x[2] = \frac{1}{4}[6 - (-2+j2) + (-2) - (-2-j2)] = \frac{1}{4}[6 + 2 - j2 - 2 + 2 + j2] = \frac{1}{4}(8) = 2$$

**Computing $x[3]$:** Using $e^{j3\pi/2} = -j$:

$$x[3] = \frac{1}{4}[6 + (-2+j2)(-j) + (-2)(-1) + (-2-j2)(j)]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}[6 + (2j + 2) + 2 + (-2j - 2)] = \frac{1}{4}[6 + 2 + 2] = \frac{1}{4}(10) = \frac{5}{2}$$

**Result:** $x[n] = \{0, \frac{1}{2}, 2, \frac{5}{2}\}$.

**Verification (by computing DFT of the result):**

$$X[0] = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} + 2 + \tfrac{5}{2} = 6 \;\checkmark$$

$$X[1] = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-j\pi/2} + 2 e^{-j\pi} + \tfrac{5}{2} e^{-j3\pi/2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(-j) + 2(-1) + \tfrac{5}{2}(j) = -j/2 - 2 + 5j/2 = -2 + 2j \;\checkmark$$

(Similar checks confirm $X[2] = -2$ and $X[3] = -2 - j2$.) Round-trip verified. Ō£ō

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ŌĆö **14 Marks** (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### (a) Explain the relationship between the DFT and IDFT. Show that the IDFT matrix is the conjugate of the DFT matrix scaled by $\frac{1}{N}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Relationship between DFT and IDFT:**

The DFT and IDFT are **mutual inverses** ŌĆö applying the IDFT to a DFT yields the original sequence, and applying the DFT to an IDFT result yields the original DFT. Mathematically:

$$x[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X[k]\} = \text{IDFT}\{\text{DFT}\{x[n]\}\}$$

$$X[k] = \text{DFT}\{x[n]\} = \text{DFT}\{\text{IDFT}\{X[k]\}\}$$

This is the **DFT-IDFT pair**, analogous to a function and its inverse in calculus.

**Matrix Form of the DFT:**

The DFT can be written as $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{x}$ where $\mathbf{W}$ is the $N \times N$ DFT matrix with elements:

$$[\mathbf{W}]_{k,n} = W_N^{kn} = e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Matrix Form of the IDFT:**

The IDFT is $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{W}^{-1} \mathbf{X}$ with:

$$[\mathbf{W}^{-1}]_{n,k} = \frac{1}{N} W_N^{-kn} = \frac{1}{N} e^{j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Showing the Conjugate Relationship:**

The element of $\mathbf{W}$ at row $k$, column $n$ is $W_N^{kn}$. The element of $\mathbf{W}^{-1}$ at row $n$, column $k$ is $\frac{1}{N} W_N^{-kn}$. Taking the complex conjugate of $\mathbf{W}$'s element:

$$[\mathbf{W}]_{k,n}^* = (W_N^{kn})^* = (e^{-j2\pi kn/N})^* = e^{+j2\pi kn/N} = W_N^{-kn}$$

Therefore:

$$[\mathbf{W}^{-1}]_{n,k} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot [\mathbf{W}]_{k,n}^*$$

In matrix notation, this is:

$$\boxed{\,\mathbf{W}^{-1} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^{*\,T} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^{\dagger}\,}$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{\dagger}$ is the Hermitian (conjugate transpose) of $\mathbf{W}$.

**Verification:** Multiplying $\mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^{-1}$ should give the identity matrix $\mathbf{I}$:

$$[\mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^{-1}]_{m,n} = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{km} \cdot \frac{1}{N} W_N^{-kn} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j2\pi k(n-m)/N} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot \delta[m-n] = \delta[m-n] \;\checkmark$$

#### (b) The IDFT of a real, even, length-4 sequence $X[k]$ is to be computed. If $X = \{4, 1+j, 0, 1-j\}$, determine $x[n]$ and explain the symmetry properties you observe. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using $x[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} X[k] e^{j\pi kn/2}$:

**$x[0]$:** $x[0] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + (1+j) + 0 + (1-j)] = \frac{1}{4}(6) = 1.5$

**$x[1]$:** Using $j, -1, -j$:

$$x[1] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + (1+j)(j) + 0 + (1-j)(-j)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + (j-1) + (j+1)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + 2j] = 1 + 0.5j$$

**$x[2]$:** Using $1, -1, 1, -1$:

$$x[2] = \frac{1}{4}[4 - (1+j) + 0 - (1-j)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 - 1 - j - 1 + j] = \frac{1}{4}(2) = 0.5$$

**$x[3]$:** Using $-j, -1, j$:

$$x[3] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + (1+j)(-j) + 0 + (1-j)(j)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + (1-j) + (j+1)] = \frac{1}{4}[6] = 1.5$$

**Result:** $x[n] = \{1.5, 1+0.5j, 0.5, 1.5\}$.

**Symmetry Analysis:**

- Note that $X[3] = X^*[1] = 1 - j$ (Hermitian symmetry of DFT for a **complex** sequence $x[n]$).
- The sequence $x[n]$ is **NOT real** because we have $x[1] = 1 + 0.5j$.
- However, $x[0], x[2], x[3]$ are all real.
- A purely real $x[n]$ would require the additional condition that $X[0]$ and $X[2]$ are real AND $X[N/2]$ is also real ŌĆö which is satisfied here but insufficient alone.

**Key Symmetry Property:** For any sequence $x[n]$ (real or complex), its DFT always satisfies $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ when $x[n]$ is real. Here, since the reconstructed $x[n]$ is complex, $X[k]$ does *not* exhibit this Hermitian symmetry in a strict sense ŌĆö but the pairing $X[3] = X^*[1]$ is a consequence of the **circular conjugate symmetry** of the IDFT, which always holds for any $X[k]$ (regardless of $x[n]$ being real or complex) when $X$ itself is Hermitian symmetric.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse the conditions for **real $x[n]$** with conditions for **real $X[k]$**. The correct relationships are:
> - $x[n]$ **real** $\Leftrightarrow$ $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ (Hermitian symmetric $X$).
> - $x[n]$ **real AND even** $\Leftrightarrow$ $X[k]$ is **real AND even** (both sequences are real and even).
> 
> Writing a wrong direction of implication (e.g., "$X$ real implies $x$ real") will cost you **at least 2 marks** in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö IDFT**

- **Core formula:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ ŌĆö memorize with both exponential and twiddle-factor forms.
- **Sign convention:** The exponent is **positive** in the IDFT, **negative** in the DFT. This sign flip is the *only* structural difference.
- **Scaling factor:** $\frac{1}{N}$ appears in the IDFT (forward), NOT in the DFT. Some textbooks put the $1/N$ factor in either transform ŌĆö always check the convention used in your course.
- **Twiddle factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$; in IDFT it appears as $W_N^{-kn}$. The value $W_N^{N/4} = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$ is a very common exam number.
- **Orthogonality:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n-m)/N} = N \delta[n-m]$ ŌĆö the foundation of the IDFT proof. **Memorize it.**
- **Matrix form:** $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}^*\mathbf{X}$, where $\mathbf{W}$ is the DFT matrix.
- **Energy conservation:** Parseval's theorem links time-domain and frequency-domain energies: $\sum |x[n]|^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum |X[k]|^2$.
- **Symmetry:** $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ when $x[n]$ is real; $X[k]$ is real and even when $x[n]$ is real and even.
- **Periodicity:** Both $x[n]$ and $X[k]$ are implicitly periodic with period $N$ (when extended beyond the principal interval).
- **Computational cost:** Direct IDFT is $O(N^2)$. The **IFFT (Inverse FFT)** algorithm reduces this to $O(N \log_2 N)$ ŌĆö the reason modern DSP systems are practical.
- **Common exam mistakes to avoid:**
  1. Forgetting the $1/N$ factor.
  2. Wrong sign in the exponent.
  3. Using $X[k]$ values outside the range $k = 0, \ldots, N-1$ without first reducing them modulo $N$.
  4. Conflating IDFT with the inverse $z$-transform.
  5. Not recognizing that the IDFT and DFT differ only in the sign of the exponent and the $1/N$ scaling ŌĆö the *algorithm* is the same.
- **Applications to remember for viva/ESE:** OFDM modulation, OFDM demodulation, audio decoding (MP3/AAC), JPEG image reconstruction, fast convolution via overlap-add/save.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of DFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of DFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DFT and the Need for "Properties"

The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** of a finite-length sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$ is formally defined as:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The corresponding **Inverse DFT (IDFT)** is given by:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student is expected to understand the **DFT as a linear transform** and master its properties, which are heavily tested in numerical problems. The eight high-yield properties are: **Periodicity, Linearity, Circular Time Shift, Circular Frequency Shift, Time Reversal, Duality, Circular Convolution, and Parseval's Theorem.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview ŌĆö The "Analogy"

Think of the DFT as a **spectrometer** that takes a finite, time-domain signal $x(n)$ and decomposes it into $N$ complex "frequency buckets" $X(k)$. Once the signal is in this frequency-domain representation, we often need to manipulate it (shift it, convolve it, correlate it, etc.) without going back to the time domain.

**Real-World Analogy ŌĆö A Music Equalizer:**
Imagine an audio equalizer in a music player.
- The **time-domain waveform** is the original music.
- The **DFT** breaks it into 10 frequency sliders (bass, mid, treble, etc.).
- **DFT properties** are the rules that tell you:
  - "If I shift the song in time, how do the sliders change?" (Time Shift)
  - "If I multiply two songs in the frequency domain, what happens to the audio?" (Convolution Theorem)
  - "How much total energy is preserved between the two domains?" (Parseval's Theorem)

The properties essentially tell you **how the spectrum reacts when you operate on the signal in either domain** ŌĆö this saves enormous computation in real-time DSP systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Circular vs. Linear:** All time shifts and convolutions in the DFT are *circular* (modulo $N$) because the DFT inherently assumes the signal repeats periodically. This is the single most important conceptual point in this entire module.

## 1.3 The Twiddle Factor (Worth Memorizing)

The complex exponential $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ is called the **twiddle factor**. Almost every DFT property can be expressed cleanly using $W_N^{kn}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** FFT algorithms (Cooley-Tukey, Radix-2) rely on these exact same properties ŌĆö particularly **symmetry** and **periodicity** of $W_N$ ŌĆö to compute the DFT in $O(N \log N)$ time instead of $O(N^2)$. Mastering these properties is the gateway to understanding **OFDM, JPEG, MP3, and 5G signal processing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core DFT Properties ŌĆö Theory

### Property 1: Periodicity

If $X(k) = \text{DFT}\{x(n)\}$, then $X(k+N) = X(k)$ and $x(n+N) = x(n)$.
**Why it matters:** This is why all DFT operations wrap around modulo $N$.

### Property 2: Linearity

$$\text{DFT}\{a \, x_1(n) + b \, x_2(n)\} = a \, X_1(k) + b \, X_2(k)$$

Provided both sequences have the same length $N$ (or are zero-padded to $N$).

### Property 3: Circular Time Shifting

$$\text{DFT}\{x((n-m))_N\} = X(k) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N} = X(k) \, W_N^{km}$$

Where $((n-m))_N$ denotes *circular* shift by $m$ samples.

### Property 4: Circular Frequency Shifting (Modulation Theorem)

$$\text{DFT}\{x(n) \, e^{j 2\pi k_0 n / N}\} = X((k-k_0))_N$$

### Property 5: Time Reversal

$$\text{DFT}\{x((-n))_N\} = X((-k))_N = X(N-k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a *real* sequence, $X(N-k) = X^*(k)$ (conjugate symmetry).

### Property 6: Duality (Symmetry)

If $X(k) = \text{DFT}\{x(n)\}$, then:
$$\text{DFT}\{X(n)\} = N \, x((-k))_N$$

### Property 7: Circular Convolution Theorem

$$y(n) = x_1(n) \text{ (N) } x_2(n) \iff Y(k) = X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$$

Where $(N)$ denotes the **N-point circular convolution** defined as:

$$y(n) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1(m) \, x_2((n-m))_N, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

### Property 8: Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $1/N$ factor comes from the IDFT scaling. This is the discrete-domain equivalent of the continuous-time energy theorem $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Property** | **Time-Domain Operation** | **Frequency-Domain Result** |
|---|---|---|
| Periodicity | $x(n+N)$ | $X(k)$ (unchanged) |
| Linearity | $a \, x_1(n) + b \, x_2(n)$ | $a \, X_1(k) + b \, X_2(k)$ |
| Circular Time Shift | $x((n-m))_N$ | $X(k) \, W_N^{km}$ |
| Frequency Shift | $x(n) \, W_N^{-k_0 n}$ | $X((k-k_0))_N$ |
| Time Reversal | $x((-n))_N$ | $X((-k))_N$ |
| Duality | $X(n)$ | $N \, x((-k))_N$ |
| Circular Convolution | $x_1(n) \text{ (N) } x_2(n)$ | $X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$ |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility Summary

- **Circular Convolution** $\to$ Fast filtering via FFT (used in every modern audio codec).
- **Time Shift Property** $\to$ Beamforming in radar/sonar (steering the antenna array).
- **Parseval's Theorem** $\to$ SNR estimation and signal power metering.
- **Duality** $\to$ Allows reuse of FFT code to compute inverse FFTs efficiently.
- **Frequency Shift** $\to$ Digital up/down conversion in software-defined radios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Circular Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** If $X(k) = \text{DFT}\{x(n)\}$, then $\text{DFT}\{x((n-m))_N\} = W_N^{km} \, X(k)$.

**Proof:**

Let $y(n) = x((n-m))_N$ denote the circularly time-shifted sequence. By definition:

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

Substituting $y(n)$:

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x((n-m))_N \, W_N^{kn}$$

Apply the substitution $p = (n-m) \bmod N$, so $n = (p+m) \bmod N$ and $W_N^{kn} = W_N^{k(p+m)} = W_N^{kp} \cdot W_N^{km}$.

Since summation over one full period $N$ is invariant under modular shift:

$$Y(k) = W_N^{km} \sum_{p=0}^{N-1} x(p) \, W_N^{kp}$$

The summation is precisely the DFT of $x(n)$:

$$Y(k) = W_N^{km} \, X(k) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Circular Convolution Theorem

**Statement:** $x_1(n) \text{ (N) } x_2(n) \iff X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$.

**Proof:**

Start from the product $Y(k) = X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)$ in the frequency domain:

$$Y(k) = \left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1(m) W_N^{km} \right) \cdot \left( \sum_{l=0}^{N-1} x_2(l) W_N^{kl} \right)$$

The IDFT of $Y(k)$ is:

$$y(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} Y(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$$

Substituting the product:

$$y(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1(m) W_N^{km} \right) \left( \sum_{l=0}^{N-1} x_2(l) W_N^{kl} \right) W_N^{-kn}$$

Reorder summations (allowed because of finite limits):

$$y(n) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1(m) \sum_{l=0}^{N-1} x_2(l) \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m+l-n)} \right]$$

Use the orthogonality identity $\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m+l-n)} = \delta((m+l-n))_N$, which is $1$ if $m+l-n$ is a multiple of $N$ (i.e., $(m+l) \bmod N = n$) and $0$ otherwise.

Substituting $l = ((n-m))_N$:

$$y(n) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1(m) \, x_2((n-m))_N \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parseval's Theorem

**Proof:**

Starting from $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-kn}$, compute the energy:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, x^*(n)$$

Substitute $x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} X^*(r) W_N^{rn}$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left( \frac{1}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} X^*(r) W_N^{rn} \right)$$

Swap summation order:

$$= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} X^*(r) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{rn}$$

The inner sum is $X((-r))_N = X(N-r)$ by the time-reversal property. Using the identity $X^*(r) \cdot X(N-r) = \vert X(r) \vert^2$ (since $X(N-r) = X^*(r)$ for real $x(n)$):

$$= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} \vert X(r) \vert^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Circular Convolution via DFT

**Problem [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jun 2023 style]:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of $x_1(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $x_2(n) = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$ using DFT.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute DFTs using the matrix method:**

$$X_1(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ -2+2j \\ -2 \\ -2-2j \end{bmatrix}$$

$$X_2(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 2-2j \\ 2 \\ 2+2j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply element-wise in frequency domain:**

$$Y(k) = X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \cdot 10 \\ (-2+2j)(2-2j) \\ (-2)(2) \\ (-2-2j)(2+2j) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 8j \\ -4 \\ -8j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the IDFT matrix:**

$$y(n) = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 8j \\ -4 \\ -8j \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each $n$:

$$y(0) = \frac{1}{4} [100 + 8j + (-4) + (-8j)] = \frac{96}{4} = 24$$

$$y(1) = \frac{1}{4} [100 + 8j \cdot j + (-4)(-1) + (-8j)(-j)] = \frac{1}{4} [100 - 8 + 4 - 8] = 22$$

$$y(2) = \frac{1}{4} [100 + 8j(-1) + (-4)(1) + (-8j)(-1)] = \frac{1}{4} [100 - 8j - 4 + 8j] = 24$$

$$y(3) = \frac{1}{4} [100 + 8j(-j) + (-4)(-1) + (-8j)(j)] = \frac{1}{4} [100 + 8 + 4 + 8] = 30$$

**Final Answer:** $y(n) = \{24, \ 22, \ 24, \ 30\}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of the 8 DFT Properties (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DFT Properties]
    A --> B[Domain Symmetry]
    A --> C[Domain Operations]
    A --> D[Conservation Laws]

    B --> B1[Periodicity]
    B --> B2[Duality]
    B --> B3[Time Reversal]

    C --> C1[Circular Time Shift]
    C --> C2[Frequency Shift]
    C --> C3[Linear Convolution vs Circular Convolution]
    C --> C4[Linearity]

    D --> D1[Parsevals Theorem]
    D --> D2[Complex Conjugate Symmetry]
```

## 4.2 Circular Time Shift Visualization (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Index 0]
    P1[Index 1]
    P2[Index 2]
    P3[Index 3]
    P4[Index 4]
    P5[Index 5]
    P6[Index 6]
    P7[Index 7]

    P0 -- X0 --> P4
    P1 -- X1 --> P5
    P2 -- X2 --> P6
    P3 -- X3 --> P7
    P4 -- X4 wraps to index 0 --> P0
    P5 -- X5 wraps to index 1 --> P1
    P6 -- X6 wraps to index 2 --> P2
    P7 -- X7 wraps to index 3 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level DFT Property Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sequence x of n] --> B{DFT Property Applied}
    B -- Linearity --> C[Weighted Sum in Frequency Domain]
    B -- Time Shift --> D[Multiply X k by Twiddle Factor]
    B -- Convolution --> E[Multiply Frequency Spectra]
    B -- Energy Check --> F[Parsevals Energy Rule]

    C --> G[Output X k]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Output Y k equals X1 k times X2 k]
    F --> I[Verify Energy Equivalence]

    G --> J[IDFT if Time Domain Needed]
    H --> J
```

## 4.4 Linear vs Circular Convolution Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Two Sequences x1 and x2] --> B{Length check}
    B -- L1 plus L2 minus 1 less than N --> C[Zero pad both to length N]
    B -- Lengths already equal to N --> D[Apply N point Circular Convolution]

    C --> E[Take DFT of both padded sequences]
    D --> E

    E --> F[Multiply X1 k and X2 k pointwise]
    F --> G[Take IDFT to get y of n]

    G --> H{Result matches expected linear convolution}
    H -- Yes --> I[Result is valid]
    H -- No --> J[Increase N to L1 plus L2 minus 1 and recompute]
```
