<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IIR Filter Realization (Structure)**: An IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) digital filter is characterized by a rational system function of the form $H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$ containing **both feed-forward (zeros) and feed-backward (poles) terms**. A *realization* or *structure* refers to the specific block-diagram / signal-flow-graph arrangement of adders, multipliers (coefficients), and delay elements that physically implements the difference equation governing the filter.

The two principal **Direct Form** realizations are:

- **Direct Form-I**: Direct implementation of the non-canonical difference equation ŌĆö separates the *all-zero (FIR) part* and the *all-pole (IIR) part* as two cascaded sections.
- **Direct Form-II** (also called **Canonical Form**): An equivalent, reduced realization obtained by swapping the order of the FIR and IIR sections and sharing the delay line ŌĆö uses only **N** delay elements for an Nth-order filter (minimum memory realization).

The **Cascade Form** realization expresses $H(z)$ as a product of *second-order* (biquad) sections, each implemented using Direct Form-II, and then chains them in series. This is the industry-preferred structure for fixed-point hardware implementation.

## 1.2 The General IIR Difference Equation (KTU 2024 Standard Form)

An $N^{th}$ order LCCDE (Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation) with input $x[n]$ and output $y[n]$ is written as:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

The corresponding system function in the Z-domain is:

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

where the convention $a_0 = 1$ is **standard in KTU notation** for the denominator.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Cooking Recipe" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students**: Think of $H(z)$ as a *recipe* for a filter.
> - **Direct Form-I** = a chef who first mixes all the *ingredients* (input $x[n]$ and its past values, multiplied by $b_k$), then *ferments* the mixture (feedback of past $y[n]$ through $a_k$). Two separate counter-stations are needed.
> - **Direct Form-II** = the same chef but a cleverer kitchen layout ŌĆö *one shared counter* is used to hold intermediate stock (the **state variable** $w[n]$). This is the **canonical (memory-optimal)** version, exactly like a chef reusing the same chopping board for two tasks.
> - **Cascade Form** = a *meal prepared in two courses*. The recipe is broken into 2-step mini-recipes (biquads), each prepared sequentially. Easier to control portion sizes, easier to fix if one course goes wrong.

The key engineering trade-off is therefore:

| Aspect | Direct Form-I | Direct Form-II (Canonical) | Cascade |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory (delays) | $N+M$ | $\max(N,M) = N$ | Sum of biquad delays |
| Coefficient sensitivity | High for high order | High for high order | **Low (well-conditioned)** |
| Recommended for | Pedagogy / small order | Software prototyping | **Hardware / Fixed-point** |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly maps this topic to **CO3** (Apply the knowledge of Z-transform in the analysis of discrete time systems) and to Bloom's Level **Apply / Analyze**. Direct and Cascade realizations are repeatedly asked in both **ESE (End Semester Exam)** Module 4 and in **internal module tests**.

**Physical constants / standard metrics** used throughout:
- Sample period: $T_s$ (seconds) ŌĆö typically $1/F_s$ where $F_s$ is the sampling frequency in **Hz**.
- Normalized angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ (dimensionless, in **radians/sample**).
- Coefficient quantization: coefficients $a_k, b_k$ are stored as **Q15 / Q31 fixed-point** integers in real DSP chips (e.g., TMS320C55x).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Layer Structure of the LCCDE

Analyzing the LCCDE in two conceptual layers makes every structure easier to derive:

**Layer A ŌĆö All-Zero (Moving Average / FIR) Block:**

$$v[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

$$V(z) = \left(\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}\right) X(z)$$

This block has only inputs and delays ŌĆö no feedback. It produces a finite-length impulse response (hence the name **all-zero / FIR / MA**).

**Layer B ŌĆö All-Pole (Autoregressive / IIR) Block:**

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] + v[n]$$

$$Y(z) = \frac{V(z)}{1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}}$$

This block has only feedback ŌĆö its impulse response is generally infinite (hence **all-pole / IIR / AR**).

The complete filter is simply $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ ŌĆö but the *order* in which these two blocks are cascaded changes the structure, the number of delay elements, and the numerical properties.

## 2.2 Direct Form-I ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1.** Implement Layer A first: feed $x[n]$ through a tapped delay line with multipliers $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_M$ and sum into an intermediate signal $v[n]$.

**Step 2.** Implement Layer B next: feed $v[n]$ into a feedback loop that maintains the last $N$ values of $y[n]$ via delays and multiplies them by $-a_1, -a_2, \dots, -a_N$ before summing back to $v[n]$.

**Step 3.** Total memory = $M$ delays (for $x$) + $N$ delays (for $y$) = $M + N$ delays.

**Why?** Because the two layers are kept *separate* and each retains its own delay line. There is no sharing.

## 2.3 Direct Form-II ŌĆö The Canonical Reduction

**Step 1.** Realize that the LCCDE is **Linear and Time-Invariant (LTI)**. Because of LTI commutativity, swapping the order of $A(z)$ and $B(z)$ produces the same overall $H(z)$.

**Step 2.** Realize that when Layer A is moved *after* Layer B, the intermediate signal (let us call it $w[n]$) is identical for both ŌĆö meaning **one delay line is sufficient** to hold the state.

**Step 3.** Define the intermediate signal:

$$w[n] = x[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, w[n-k]$$

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, w[n-k]$$

**Step 4.** Total memory = $\max(M, N)$ delays (we choose $N \geq M$ for proper IIR). For an Nth-order IIR, this is exactly $N$ ŌĆö the **theoretical minimum** (canonical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Form** = a realization that uses the minimum number of delay elements (equal to the filter order). Direct Form-II is canonical; Direct Form-I is **non-canonical**.

## 2.4 Transposed Direct Form-II (Frequently Tested)

Derived by applying the **Transposition Theorem** to Direct Form-II:

1. Reverse the direction of all signal flows.
2. Replace adders with nodes (branch points) and nodes with adders.
3. Swap input Ōåö output.

The transposed structure is *numerically superior* in many fixed-point implementations because internal node magnitudes are often smaller.

## 2.5 Cascade Form Realization

Factor $B(z)$ and $A(z)$ into second-order (biquadratic) sections:

$$H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z), \qquad H_k(z) = \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$$

where $K = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ is the number of biquads. Each biquad is implemented in **Direct Form-II** to minimize memory, and the biquads are cascaded (output of one feeds input of next).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pairing & Ordering Rule** (high-yield for KTU problems): Pair the pole closest to the unit circle with the zero closest to it, then arrange biquads in order of **increasing peak gain** to avoid overflow in fixed-point arithmetic.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LCCDE (general) | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Specification of IIR filter |
| 2 | System Function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Frequency response, stability |
| 3 | Direct Form-I delays | $N + M$ | Memory estimate |
| 4 | Direct Form-II delays | $\max(N,M) = N$ (for proper IIR) | Canonical memory |
| 5 | Intermediate DF-II signal | $w[n] = x[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k w[n-k]$ | State equation |
| 6 | DF-II output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k w[n-k]$ | Output equation |
| 7 | Number of biquads | $K = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ | Cascade sizing |
| 8 | Cascade product | $H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z)$ | Modular realization |
| 9 | Impulse response length | $h[n]$ decays but never exactly zero (infinite) | Defines "IIR" |
| 10 | Stability | All poles of $H(z)$ strictly inside unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$ | BIBO stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly lists *Direct Form-I, Direct Form-II, and Cascade Form* as required realizations. Transposed forms are bonus/extra-credit in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Direct Form-I from the LCCDE

**Start** with the standard LCCDE:

$$y[n] + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

**Step 1** ŌĆö Solve for the highest-order output term $y[n]$:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

*Conversion logic:* this is a direct rearrangement of the LCCDE with the sign convention "all $a_k$ on the LHS in standard form."

**Step 2** ŌĆö Define an intermediate signal $v[n]$ as the *input to the all-pole block*:

$$v[n] \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

*Conversion logic:* this is exactly Layer A ŌĆö the all-zero (FIR) part of the filter, generating $v[n]$ as a weighted sum of present and past input samples.

**Step 3** ŌĆö Express $y[n]$ using $v[n]$:

$$y[n] = v[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k]$$

*Conversion logic:* this is Layer B ŌĆö the all-pole (IIR) part. The output $y[n]$ equals the intermediate $v[n]$ plus a feedback correction built from the previous $N$ outputs.

**Step 4** ŌĆö Take Z-transforms of both layers:

$$V(z) = \left(\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}\right) X(z) = B(z)\, X(z)$$

$$Y(z) = \frac{V(z)}{A(z)} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

*Conversion logic:* confirms the implementation exactly realizes $H(z)$. Two delay lines of lengths $M$ and $N$ are required. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of Direct Form-II (Canonical Form)

**Start** with the same $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ and the assumption $N \geq M$ (proper IIR).

**Step 1** ŌĆö Define an intermediate signal $W(z)$ as the output of the all-pole block alone, when driven by $X(z)$:

$$W(z) = \frac{X(z)}{A(z)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad w[n] = x[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, w[n-k]$$

*Conversion logic:* the *all-pole section is moved first*. Its output $w[n]$ is the "filtered version" of $x[n]$ that contains all the system memory.

**Step 2** ŌĆö Pass $W(z)$ through the all-zero block:

$$Y(z) = B(z)\, W(z) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, w[n-k]$$

*Conversion logic:* this is the *output equation* ŌĆö $y[n]$ is built only from $w[n]$ and its past values, multiplied by the $b_k$ coefficients.

**Step 3** ŌĆö Count the delays. The delay line now stores $w[n], w[n-1], \dots, w[n-N+1]$ ŌĆö only **N** elements. The output taps $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_M$ all read from the *same* delay line.

*Conversion logic:* the LTI property (commutativity of $A(z)$ and $B(z)$) guarantees identical input-output behaviour, but with the minimum possible memory. This is what makes it *canonical*. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Format)

**Problem.** Realize the IIR filter described by the difference equation

$$y[n] = 0.5\, y[n-1] - 0.3\, y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.4\, x[n-1] + 0.2\, x[n-2]$$

in (a) **Direct Form-I**, (b) **Direct Form-II**, and (c) **Cascade Form** (grouping into biquads).

### (a) Direct Form-I ŌĆö Coefficient Identification

Comparing with the standard LCCDE, we identify:

$$a_1 = -0.5,\quad a_2 = 0.3,\quad b_0 = 1,\quad b_1 = 0.4,\quad b_2 = 0.2$$

(with the sign convention $y[n] = -\sum a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_k x[n-k]$, so the $y[n-1]$ coefficient on the LHS being $0.5$ means $a_1 = -0.5$).

The structure uses **2** delays for $y$ and **2** delays for $x$, totalling $M+N = 4$ delays. Multipliers required: 5 (one each for $a_1, a_2, b_0, b_1, b_2$).

### (b) Direct Form-II ŌĆö Coefficient Identification

The intermediate signal is:

$$w[n] = x[n] - a_1\, w[n-1] - a_2\, w[n-2] = x[n] + 0.5\, w[n-1] - 0.3\, w[n-2]$$

The output equation is:

$$y[n] = b_0\, w[n] + b_1\, w[n-1] + b_2\, w[n-2] = w[n] + 0.4\, w[n-1] + 0.2\, w[n-2]$$

Delays used: **2** (canonical, $N=2$). Multipliers: 5. This implementation is **memory-optimal**.

### (c) Cascade Form ŌĆö Biquad Factorization

System function in $z^{-1}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2}}$$

Since $N = M = 2$, we have $K = \lceil 2/2 \rceil = 1$ biquad. The single biquad is:

$$H_1(z) = \frac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2}}$$

So the cascade reduces to a single Direct Form-II biquad in this case. For higher-order examples, the polynomial $B(z)$ and $A(z)$ would each be factored into 2nd-order polynomials in $z^{-1}$, with one biquad per pair of conjugate poles (and one per pair of conjugate zeros).

### Verification ŌĆö Impulse Response via the Realizations

Set $x[n] = \delta[n]$, all initial conditions zero.

| $n$ | $w[n]$ | $y[n]$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | $w[0]=\delta[0]=1$, $y[0] = b_0 w[0] = 1$ |
| 1 | 0.5 | 1.4 | $w[1] = 0.5(1) = 0.5$, $y[1] = 0.5 + 0.4(1) = 0.9$ ŌåÆ re-evaluate below |

Re-evaluating with the *correct* cascaded values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w[0] &= x[0] = 1, \\
y[0] &= b_0 w[0] = 1, \\
w[1] &= x[1] + 0.5\, w[0] = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5, \\
y[1] &= b_0 w[1] + b_1 w[0] = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9, \\
w[2] &= x[2] + 0.5\, w[1] - 0.3\, w[0] = 0 + 0.25 - 0.3 = -0.05, \\
y[2] &= b_0 w[2] + b_1 w[1] + b_2 w[0] = -0.05 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.35.
\end{aligned}
$$

The sequence $h[n] = \{1,\ 0.9,\ 0.35,\dots\}$ decays but never reaches exactly zero, confirming the IIR nature.

## 3.4 Derivation of Transposed Direct Form-II

**Step 1** ŌĆö Apply the **Transposition Theorem** to the Direct Form-II signal flow graph:
- Reverse every arrow.
- Replace nodes (branch points) with adders, and adders with nodes.

**Step 2** ŌĆö The new state equations become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1[n+1] &= b_0\, x[n] - a_1\, y[n] + w_2[n], \\
w_2[n+1] &= b_1\, x[n] - a_2\, y[n], \\
y[n] &= w_1[n].
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö The transposed structure is useful because each delay input is a *sum of products* rather than a sum of accumulated feedback ŌĆö this often yields **smaller internal signal magnitudes** in fixed-point arithmetic, reducing overflow probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Real DSP chips (Texas Instruments TMS320 series, ARM Cortex-M CMSIS-DSP libraries) provide optimized transposed biquad routines precisely because of this numerical superiority. The cascade of transposed biquads is the **default implementation** in MATLAB's `dfilt.df2t` object.

## 3.5 Why Cascade Form is Preferred for High-Order IIR Filters

For a 6th-order filter ($N=6$):

- **Direct Form-II (single block)** would have one big set of 7 coefficients, with severe coefficient-quantization sensitivity. A 16-bit quantization of $a_6$ can shift the pole from radius $0.99$ to radius $> 1$ (unstable!).
- **Cascade of three 2nd-order biquads** breaks the polynomial roots into pairs; quantization of each biquad coefficient is local, and the pole drift is bounded.

Empirically, **Cascade Form is the most commonly used** realization in production audio codecs, biomedical signal processors, and software-defined radio.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Direct Form-I

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[/"x[n]"/]
    dly1A(["z^-1"])
    dly2A(["z^-1"])
    dly1B(["z^-1"])
    dly2B(["z^-1"])
    sumA(("+"))
    sumB(("+"))
    outNode[/"y[n]"/]

    inNode -- "b0" --> sumA
    inNode --> dly1A
    dly1A -- "b1" --> sumA
    dly1A --> dly2A
    dly2A -- "b2" --> sumA

    sumA -- "v[n]" --> sumB
    sumA --> dly1B
    dly1B -- "-a1" --> sumB
    dly1B --> dly2B
    dly2B -- "-a2" --> sumB

    sumB -- "y[n]" --> outNode
    outNode -. feedback .-> dly1B
```

> **Reading guide**: The upper half (x-delay line) is the **all-zero / FIR** block producing $v[n]$; the lower half (y-delay line) is the **all-pole / IIR** feedback block producing $y[n]$. Total delays = $M + N = 4$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Direct Form-II (Canonical)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[/"x[n]"/]
    sumA(("+"))
    dly1(["z^-1"])
    dly2(["z^-1"])
    sumB(("+"))
    outNode[/"y[n]"/]

    inNode --> sumA
    sumA -- "w[n]" --> dly1
    dly1 --> dly2
    dly2 -- "-a2 * w[n-2]" --> sumA
    dly1 -- "-a1 * w[n-1]" --> sumA

    sumA -- "w[n]" --> sumB
    dly1 -- "b1" --> sumB
    dly2 -- "b2" --> sumB
    sumA -- "b0" --> sumB
    sumB -- "y[n]" --> outNode
```

> **Reading guide**: The shared delay line stores $w[n], w[n-1], w[n-2]$. Total delays = $N = 2$ ŌĆö the canonical minimum.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Cascade of Two Biquads (K = 2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[/"x[n]"/]
    bq1["Biquad H1 z"]
    bq2["Biquad H2 z"]
    outNode[/"y[n]"/]

    inNode --> bq1
    bq1 -- "intermediate" --> bq2
    bq2 -- "y[n]" --> outNode
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BQ1 ["Biquad 1 ŌĆö Direct Form II"]
        direction LR
        i1[/"u1"/]
        sa1(("+"))
        za1(["z^-1"])
        za2(["z^-1"])
        sb1(("+"))
        o1[/"v1"/]
        i1 --> sa1
        sa1 --> za1
        za1 --> za2
        za2 -- "-a21" --> sa1
        za1 -- "-a11" --> sa1
        sa1 -- "b01" --> sb1
        za1 -- "b11" --> sb1
        za2 -- "b21" --> sb1
        sb1 --> o1
    end

    subgraph BQ2 ["Biquad 2 ŌĆö Direct Form II"]
        direction LR
        i2[/"u2"/]
        sa2(("+"))
        zb1(["z^-1"])
        zb2(["z^-1"])
        sb2(("+"))
        o2[/"v2"/]
        i2 --> sa2
        sa2 --> zb1
        zb1 --> zb2
        zb2 -- "-a22" --> sa2
        zb1 -- "-a12" --> sa2
        sa2 -- "b02" --> sb2
        zb1 -- "b12" --> sb2
        zb2 -- "b22" --> sb2
        sb2 --> o2
    end

    inNode[/"x[n]"/] --> BQ1
    BQ1 -- "v1 = u2" --> BQ2
    BQ2 -- "y[n]" --> outNode[/"y[n]"/]
```

> **Reading guide**: Each biquad is internally a Direct Form-II structure. The output of biquad 1 becomes the input of biquad 2. In a *practical* DSP implementation, a per-section **DC-scaling gain** $G_k$ is inserted between biquads to prevent overflow ŌĆö this is the $b_{0k}$ pre-scaling factor.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Transposed Direct Form-II

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[/"x[n]"/]

    sa2(("+"))
    sa3(("+"))

    sa1 --> outNode

```
