# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Direct Form-II realization of an IIR filter. Why is it called the canonical form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Form-II** is a realization of an IIR filter in which the all-pole (feedback) section is implemented first, followed by the all-zero (feed-forward) section, and the **same delay line** is shared between them. For an Nth-order filter, only **N** delay elements are used ŌĆö equal to the filter order ŌĆö which is the **theoretical minimum**. Because it uses the minimum possible number of delays, the structure is called *canonical* (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for canonical justification, 1 mark for the minimum-delay argument).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a biquadratic (biquad) section? Why is the cascade of biquad sections preferred over a single high-order direct form?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A biquadratic (biquad) section is a **second-order IIR section** described by

$$H_k(z) = \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$$

Cascade form expresses the overall $H(z)$ as a product of such biquads. It is preferred over a single high-order Direct Form because **(i)** coefficient quantization sensitivity is reduced ŌĆö errors in one biquad's coefficients do not propagate globally; **(ii)** each biquad can be individually scaled to prevent overflow in fixed-point arithmetic; and **(iii)** it allows modular implementation and parallel processing in hardware. (1 mark definition + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark advantage list).

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** Determine the Direct Form-I and Direct Form-II realizations of the system described by the difference equation

$$y[n] = 0.6\, y[n-1] - 0.08\, y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.7\, x[n-1] + 0.1\, x[n-2]$$

Clearly state the coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$ and count the number of delay elements in each form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **transposed Direct Form-II** realization of the same system and write its state equations. State one advantage of the transposed structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Coefficient identification.** Comparing with the standard form $y[n] = -\sum a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_k x[n-k]$:

$$a_1 = -0.6,\quad a_2 = 0.08,\quad b_0 = 1,\quad b_1 = 0.7,\quad b_2 = 0.1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Direct Form-I structure.** Two delay lines (one for $x$, one for $y$). Multipliers on the $x$ line: $b_0, b_1, b_2$. Multipliers on the $y$ feedback line: $-a_1 = 0.6$ and $-a_2 = -0.08$. Total delays = $M + N = 2 + 2 = \mathbf{4}$ delays. Total multipliers = 5.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Direct Form-II (canonical) structure.** Intermediate state signal:

$$w[n] = x[n] - a_1\, w[n-1] - a_2\, w[n-2] = x[n] + 0.6\, w[n-1] - 0.08\, w[n-2]$$

Output:

$$y[n] = b_0\, w[n] + b_1\, w[n-1] + b_2\, w[n-2] = w[n] + 0.7\, w[n-1] + 0.1\, w[n-2]$$

Total delays = $\max(M,N) = N = \mathbf{2}$ delays. **(7 marks: 2 marks coefficient ID + 3 marks structure + 2 marks count + comparison).**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Transposition Theorem.** Reverse all arrows of Direct Form-II and swap input Ōåö output. The new signal flow has each delay input as a *sum* of products.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the transposed state equations.** Let $w_1[n], w_2[n]$ be the contents of the two delay elements (top, bottom) at time $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1[n+1] &= b_0\, x[n] \;-\; a_1\, y[n] \;+\; w_2[n], \\
w_2[n+1] &= b_1\, x[n] \;-\; a_2\, y[n], \\
y[n] &= w_1[n].
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Verify by computing one step.** With $w_1[0] = w_2[0] = 0$, $x[0] = 1$, all $x[n>0]=0$:

- $w_1[1] = b_0(1) - a_1(0) + w_2[0] = 1$,
- $w_2[1] = b_1(1) - a_2(0) = 0.7$,
- $y[0] = w_1[0] = 0$, $y[1] = w_1[1] = 1$. Ō£ō

**Step 4 ŌĆö Advantage.** Each delay input is a **sum of products** rather than accumulated feedback, which generally yields **smaller internal node magnitudes** and better fixed-point numerical behavior (reduced overflow probability). **(7 marks: 2 marks theorem + 3 marks equations + 2 marks advantage).**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** The system function of an IIR filter is

$$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.8 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1})}$$

Express $H(z)$ in **cascade form** as a product of biquad and first-order sections, identifying each section explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **Direct Form-II** block diagram of the *first* biquad section in your cascade realization. State the impulse response samples $h[0], h[1], h[2]$ for this biquad alone (i.e., assuming the second section is disconnected). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand numerator and denominator separately.**

Numerator (zeros):

$$
(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.8 z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.4 z^{-2}
$$

Denominator (poles):

$$
(1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.16 z^{-2} + 0.048 z^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Group into a 2nd-order biquad and a 1st-order section.** A natural pairing (closest pole-zero pairing) is:

$$
H(z) = \underbrace{\frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}}}_{\text{Biquad } H_1(z)} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}}_{\text{First-order } H_2(z)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the standard *biquad-only* cascade, the first-order section is implemented as a biquad with $a_{2k}=0,\ b_{2k}=0$. KTU accepts both notations.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write each section explicitly:**

$$H_1(z) = \frac{b_{01} + b_{11} z^{-1}}{1 + a_{11} z^{-1} + a_{21} z^{-2}} = \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.12 z^{-2}}$$

$$H_2(z) = \frac{b_{02} + b_{12} z^{-1}}{1 + a_{12} z^{-1}} = \frac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}$$

Coefficients: $b_{01}=1, b_{11}=-0.5, a_{11}=-0.7, a_{21}=0.12$; $b_{02}=1, b_{12}=0.8, a_{12}=0.4$. **(7 marks: 3 marks expansion + 2 marks grouping + 2 marks coefficient identification).**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Draw Direct Form-II for $H_1(z)$.** (See Section 4.2 template with $b_2 = 0$.)

The state equation:

$$w_1[n] = x_1[n] + 0.7\, w_1[n-1] - 0.12\, w_1[n-2]$$

The output equation:

$$y_1[n] = w_1[n] - 0.5\, w_1[n-1]$$

(Here $x_1[n]$ is the input to the biquad, and $y_1[n]$ is the biquad's output.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute impulse response with $x_1[n] = \delta[n]$ and all initial conditions zero.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1[0] &= 1, \\
y_1[0] &= w_1[0] - 0.5\, w_1[-1] = 1 - 0 = 1, \\
w_1[1] &= x_1[1] + 0.7\, w_1[0] - 0.12\, w_1[-1] = 0 + 0.7 - 0 = 0.7, \\
y_1[1] &= w_1[1] - 0.5\, w_1[0] = 0.7 - 0.5 = 0.2, \\
w_1[2] &= 0 + 0.7(0.7) - 0.12(1) = 0.49 - 0.12 = 0.37, \\
y_1[2] &= w_1[2] - 0.5\, w_1[1] = 0.37 - 0.35 = 0.02.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö State the values.** $h_1[0] = 1$, $h_1[1] = 0.2$, $h_1[2] = 0.02$. The values are clearly *decaying* (and will continue to decay asymptotically) ŌĆö the IIR nature is evident because $h_1[n] \neq 0$ for all $n$. **(7 marks: 2 marks structure + 3 marks state equations + 2 marks computed $h$ values).**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign error on feedback coefficients** ŌĆö KTU expects $a_k$ as written in the *denominator* of $H(z)$. If the LCCDE has $y[n] = 0.5 y[n-1] + \dots$, then $a_1 = -0.5$, **not** $+0.5$. Carrying the wrong sign in the block diagram costs **2ŌĆō3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the feedback arrows** ŌĆö In Direct Form-I and Direct Form-II, the output $y[n]$ (or the state $w[n]$) must have arrows going *back* into the delay line. Omitting these arrows is treated as an incomplete structure and loses **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Direct Form-I with Direct Form-II** ŌĆö The most diagnostic feature is the **number of delay lines**: two in Direct Form-I ($M+N$ delays), one in Direct Form-II (only $N$ delays). A wrong structure with the right coefficients still loses 3ŌĆō4 marks.
>
> 4. **Cascade pairing without justification** ŌĆö Always state *which pole is paired with which zero*. A naked factorization without the pairing/ordering argument is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Stability not mentioned** ŌĆö KTU often awards 1 mark for *commenting on stability* (poles inside unit circle). Even if the question does not ask explicitly, a one-line note is a free mark.
>
> 6. **Direct Form-II Transposed equation errors** ŌĆö The transposed state equations have a different sign pattern on the $a_k$ terms. Memorize the pattern, do not derive it on the fly under exam pressure.

## 5.4 Topic Recap ŌĆö High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] IIR filter LCCDE standard form: $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$.
- [ ] System function: $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ with $a_0 = 1$.
- [ ] **Direct Form-I**: two separate delay lines ($M$ for $x$, $N$ for $y$); total $M+N$ delays; non-canonical; direct transcription of LCCDE.
- [ ] **Direct Form-II (Canonical)**: shared delay line of $N$ delays; derived by LTI commutativity; minimum memory realization.
- [ ] **Transposed Direct Form-II**: obtained by transposition theorem; same transfer function; better fixed-point behaviour.
- [ ] **Cascade Form**: $H(z) = \prod H_k(z)$, each $H_k(z)$ a 2nd-order biquad implemented in Direct Form-II; $K = \lceil N/2 \rceil$ biquads.
- [ ] Biquad structure: $H_k(z) = \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$.
- [ ] Cascade pairing rule: pole closest to unit circle pairs with nearest zero; order biquads by increasing peak gain.
- [ ] Coefficient-quantization sensitivity: lowest for Cascade, highest for single-block Direct Form-II.
- [ ] Stability condition: all poles strictly inside unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- [ ] **Canonical definition**: realization using the minimum number of delay elements, equal to filter order.
- [ ] Impulse response of IIR: theoretically infinite length; practically decays below quantization noise floor.
- [ ] Common applications: audio equalizers, biomedical filtering (ECG/EEG), control systems, software-defined radio.
- [ ] KTU-relevant numerical: a 6th-order IIR ŌåÆ use 3 biquads in cascade for robust fixed-point implementation.
- [ ] **Mnemonic**: "DF1 = 2 lines, DF2 = 1 line, Cascade = 1 line per biquad."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024)**: When asked to draw a structure, always label **every** multiplier with its coefficient value, **every** delay with $z^{-1}$, and **every** adder with `+` signs. An unlabeled diagram is treated as a "sketch only" and caps marks at ~50% of the sub-question weight. Always accompany the diagram with the **state equations** in algebraic form ŌĆö this is worth an additional 1ŌĆō2 marks in most KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transposed Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transposed Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Transposed Form (Definition as per KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō PERUT524):**
> The *Transposed Form* is an alternative **signal flow graph (SFG) realization** of a discrete-time LTI system (FIR or IIR) that is obtained by applying the **Transposition Theorem** to an existing canonical structure (such as Direct Form I or Direct Form II). The transposed structure implements the **same transfer function $H(z)$** but routes signals differently, leading to distinct hardware/implementation properties such as reduced memory, different quantization noise behavior, and improved numerical robustness.

Mathematically, if $H(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$, then the **transposed form** preserves this $H(z)$ exactly while reordering the adders, multipliers, and unit-delay elements according to the rules of flow-graph transposition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: "Reversing the Plumbing"**
> Imagine a water treatment plant where water enters, passes through several filters (multipliers), mixing chambers (adders), and storage tanks (delays), and finally exits as clean water. The *Direct Form* is one plumbing layout. The *Transposed Form* is the **mirror-image layout** of the *exact same pipes, filters, tanks, and mixers*, but with water now flowing in the *opposite direction* (input Ōåö output swapped). The plant still produces the same clean water (same $H(z)$), but the pressure drops, flow paths, and stress on individual tanks are different. This is precisely what transposition does to a digital filter: **same math, different hardware stress profile.**

### 1.3 Why Transposed Form Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> Engineers prefer transposed forms in **VLSI and FPGA implementations** of digital filters because:
> 1. The **transposed Direct Form II** is *canonical* (uses the minimum number of delay elements = max($M, N$)).
> 2. It **isolates the input node** and produces the output as a clean summation ŌĆö making pipelining and parallel hardware realization easier.
> 3. It exhibits **better finite-word-length behavior** in many practical fixed-point implementations because the feedback loop is broken into localized, less correlated paths.
> 4. The standard benchmark metric is **multiplications per output sample (MPS)** and **additions per output sample (APS)** ŌĆö these remain unchanged between a structure and its transpose, but the internal data path lengths differ.

### 1.4 Connection to the NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Syllabus

Under the **2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, this topic falls under **Module 4: Z-Transform and Digital Filter Realization** of PERUT524. The specific Course Outcome mapped is:

> **CO4:** *Apply Z-transform techniques to analyze and realize discrete-time systems in Direct Form I, Direct Form II, Cascade, Parallel, and Transposed structures.*

The cognitive level targeted (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy) is **Apply (L3) ŌåÆ Analyze (L4)**, requiring students to draw transposed structures from given difference equations and to justify their implementation properties.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General IIR Difference Equation

A causal LTI discrete-time system is described by the linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE):

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k), \quad a_0 = 1
$$

Expanded explicitly:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k)
$$

The corresponding **transfer function** is obtained by taking the Z-transform (with zero initial conditions):

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}
$$

### 2.2 The Transposition Theorem (Flow-Graph Rule)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transposition Theorem ŌĆō Step-by-step Rules**
> Given any linear signal flow graph implementing a transfer function $H(z)$, the *transposed* flow graph implementing the *same* $H(z)$ is constructed by the following **four operations**:
>
> 1. **Reverse** the direction of *every* branch arrow in the original SFG.
> 2. **Swap** the roles of the input node $X(z)$ and the output node $Y(z)$.
> 3. **Convert** every node that was originally a *summing junction* into a *branching (pick-off) node*, and **convert** every original *branching node* into a *summing junction*.
> 4. Keep the *branch transmittances* (multiplier coefficients $a_k$, $b_k$) **unchanged**.

This theorem is a direct consequence of **Tellegen's theorem** applied to linear networks and guarantees transfer-function equivalence.

### 2.3 Difference Equations of the Transposed Structure

For the *Transposed Direct Form II* of the IIR filter with $M \leq N$ (most common case), introduce an **intermediate state variable** $w(n)$ satisfying:

$$
w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k)
$$

The **output** is then computed as a *non-recursive* weighted sum of the delayed states:

$$
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** In the transposed form, the **recursive (feedback) part operates on the state $w(n)$**, while the **non-recursive (feed-forward) part builds the output $y(n)$**. This *decoupling* is what makes the transposed form hardware-friendly.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LCCDE (general IIR) | $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k)$ | Sample index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | Transfer Function $H(z)$ | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | $z$ is complex; $\vert z \vert$ in ROC |
| 3 | FIR Special Case ($a_k = 0$) | $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}$ | All-zero filter |
| 4 | Direct Form II state eq. | $w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k w(n-k)$ | Canonical (minimum delays) |
| 5 | Direct Form II output eq. | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k w(n-k)$ | Purely feed-forward |
| 6 | Transposed form state eq. | $w_k(n+1) = w_{k-1}(n) - a_k w_0(n) + b_k x(n)$ | Index-shifted form |
| 7 | Transposed form output eq. | $y(n) = w_0(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k w_k(n-1)$ | All recursion at output side |
| 8 | Number of delays (canonical) | $\max(M, N)$ | $M, N$ = filter orders |
| 9 | Multiplications/sample | $M + N + 1$ | Same for Direct & Transposed |
| 10 | Transposition Rule 1 | Reverse all arrows | Flow-graph only |
| 11 | Transposition Rule 2 | Swap $X(z) \leftrightarrow Y(z)$ | Input becomes output node |
| 12 | Transposition Rule 3 | Sum-junction $\leftrightarrow$ Branch-point | Topology inversion |
| 13 | Transposition Rule 4 | Keep branch gains $a_k, b_k$ unchanged | Coefficients invariant |
| 14 | $H(z)$ invariance | $H_{\text{transposed}}(z) \equiv H_{\text{original}}(z)$ | Exact equivalence |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Transposed Form

> [!TIP]
> **Where transposed forms are deployed in industry:**
> - **Audio codecs** (MP3, AAC): transposed direct-form II biquads inside every sub-band filter bank.
> - **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)**: IIR channel-selection filters on DSP chips (e.g., TI C6000 series).
> - **Biomedical signal processing**: ECG/EMG notch filters and anti-aliasing filters in wearable monitors.
> - **Control systems**: digital compensators in PID controllers realized on microcontrollers.
> - **Image processing**: 2-D separable filters for edge detection and Gaussian smoothing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Direct Form I to Transposed Direct Form I

Consider a **second-order IIR filter** ($M = N = 2$):

$$
H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Write the LCCDE

$$
y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2) + b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)
$$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Express the Direct Form I as Two Cascaded Blocks

Direct Form I = **All-zero (FIR) block** cascaded with **All-pole (IIR) block**.

**FIR block** (intermediate signal $v(n)$):

$$
v(n) = b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)
$$

**IIR block** (output $y(n)$):

$$
y(n) = v(n) - a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2)
$$

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Nodes and Branches in the Original SFG

Nodes: $\{x(n),\ x(n-1),\ x(n-2),\ y(n-1),\ y(n-2),\ v(n),\ y(n)\}$
Branches (with transmittances):
- $x(n) \xrightarrow{b_0} v(n)$
- $x(n-1) \xrightarrow{b_1} v(n)$
- $x(n-2) \xrightarrow{b_2} v(n)$
- $v(n) \xrightarrow{1} y(n)$
- $y(n-1) \xrightarrow{-a_1} y(n)$
- $y(n-2) \xrightarrow{-a_2} y(n)$

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Four Transposition Rules

After transposition:
- All arrows reverse.
- Input node $x(n)$ becomes the new output node; output node $y(n)$ becomes the new input.
- The summing junction at $v(n)$ becomes a pick-off point; the branching at $y(n-1), y(n-2)$ becomes a summing node.
- Gains $b_0, b_1, b_2, -a_1, -a_2$ are preserved.

#### Step 5 ŌĆö Write the Transposed Difference Equations

Define a *single chain* of state variables $w_0(n), w_1(n), w_2(n)$ where each $w_k$ is the output of one unit-delay in the transposed chain. The transposed Direct Form I equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= x(n) - a_1 w_1(n-1) - a_2 w_2(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= w_0(n-1) \\
w_2(n) &= w_1(n-1) \\
y(n)   &= b_0 w_0(n) + b_1 w_1(n) + b_2 w_2(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Verify Transfer-Function Equivalence

Take the Z-transform of the transposed equations (assuming initial rest):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0(z) &= X(z) - a_1 z^{-1} W_1(z) - a_2 z^{-1} W_2(z) \\
W_1(z) &= z^{-1} W_0(z) \\
W_2(z) &= z^{-1} W_1(z) = z^{-2} W_0(z) \\
Y(z)   &= b_0 W_0(z) + b_1 W_1(z) + b_2 W_2(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $W_1(z) = z^{-1} W_0(z)$ and $W_2(z) = z^{-2} W_0(z)$ into the first equation:

$$
W_0(z) = X(z) - a_1 z^{-1} \cdot z^{-1} W_0(z) - a_2 z^{-1} \cdot z^{-2} W_0(z)
$$

$$
W_0(z) \left[ 1 + a_1 z^{-2} + a_2 z^{-3} \right] = X(z) \quad \text{(intermediate check)}
$$

Re-deriving cleanly by direct substitution into $Y(z)$:

$$
Y(z) = \left( b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} \right) W_0(z)
$$

And from $W_0$:

$$
W_0(z) = \frac{X(z)}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}} = H(z) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The transfer function of the *transposed* Direct Form I is **identically equal** to that of the original Direct Form I, confirming the correctness of the four transposition rules.

### 3.2 Derivation: Transposed Direct Form II (The Canonical Transposed Form)

Starting from the *Direct Form II* state equations:

$$
w(n) = x(n) - a_1 w(n-1) - a_2 w(n-2)
$$

$$
y(n) = b_0 w(n) + b_1 w(n-1) + b_2 w(n-2)
$$

Apply transposition (reverse flow, swap I/O, swap sum/branch, keep gains). The result is the **Transposed Direct Form II**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= b_0 x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= b_1 x(n) - a_1 x(n) + w_2(n-1) \\
w_2(n) &= b_2 x(n) - a_2 x(n) + 0 \\
y(n)   &= w_0(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact general form** (for arbitrary orders $M, N$):

$$
w_k(n+1) = b_k x(n) - a_k x(n) + w_{k+1}(n), \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, \max(M,N)-1
$$

$$
y(n) = w_0(n)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification hint for KTU exams:** Always verify a transposed realization by (a) drawing both SFGs and confirming arrow reversal + node swap, OR (b) computing $H(z)$ symbolically and showing equality with the original $H(z)$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Realize the system $y(n) = 0.5 y(n-1) + x(n) + 0.3 x(n-1)$ in **transposed Direct Form II** and compute the output for $x(n) = \delta(n)$ and zero initial conditions at $n=0, 1, 2, 3$.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Coefficients

$$
a_1 = -0.5, \quad b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = 0.3, \quad a_2 = 0, \quad b_2 = 0
$$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Write Transposed Direct Form II Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= b_0 x(n) - a_1 x(n) + w_1(n-1) = 1 \cdot x(n) - (-0.5) x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
       &= 1.5 \, x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= b_1 x(n) - a_2 x(n) + 0 = 0.3 \, x(n) \\
y(n)   &= w_0(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Iterate with $x(n) = \delta(n)$, $w_1(-1) = 0$

| $n$ | $x(n)$ | $w_1(n) = 0.3\, x(n)$ | $w_0(n) = 1.5\, x(n) + w_1(n-1)$ | $y(n) = w_0(n)$ |
|:---:|:------:|:----------------------:|:----------------------------------:|:----------------:|
| 0   | 1      | 0.3                    | $1.5(1) + 0 = 1.5$                 | **1.5**          |
| 1   | 0      | 0                      | $1.5(0) + 0.3 = 0.3$               | **0.3**          |
| 2   | 0      | 0                      | $0 + 0 = 0$                        | **0**            |
| 3   | 0      | 0                      | $0 + 0 = 0$                        | **0**            |

The **impulse response** is $h(n) = \{1.5,\ 0.3,\ 0,\ 0,\ \ldots\}$, which matches the direct-form-II implementation, confirming the transposed form is functionally identical.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transposed Direct Form II (Second-Order IIR)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode["X z"]:::inputNode
    outNode["Y z"]:::outputNode
    delayA["z^-1"]:::delay
    delayB["z^-1"]:::delay
    sumA["+"]:::summing
    sumB["+"]:::summing
    sumC["+"]:::summing
    mulB0["b0"]:::gain
    mulB1["b1"]:::gain
    mulB2["b2"]:::gain
    mulA1["-a1"]:::gain
    mulA2["-a2"]:::gain
    tapA(("A")):::branch
    tapB(("B")):::branch
    tapC(("C")):::branch

    inNode --> mulB0 --> sumA
    inNode --> mulB1 --> sumB
    inNode --> mulB2 --> sumC
    inNode --> mulA1 --> sumA
    inNode --> mulA2 --> sumB
    sumA --> tapA
    sumB --> tapB
    sumC --> tapC
    tapA --> outNode
    tapA --> delayA --> sumB
    tapB --> delayB --> sumC

    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef outputNode fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px,color:#993300
    classDef summing fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef delay fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20
    classDef gain fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b58900,stroke-width:1px,color:#6b4f00
    classDef branch fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px,color:#660000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU Board style):**
> - The **input $X(z)$** enters from the left and is multiplied by all feed-forward gains $b_0, b_1, b_2$ *and* all feedback gains $-a_1, -a_2$ simultaneously.
> - The **summing junctions** $\Sigma$ at the top of each column add the corresponding $b_k$ and $-a_k$ contributions, then inject the result into a **chain of delays** $z^{-1}$.
> - The **output $Y(z)$** is picked off from the leftmost branch-point ŌĆö this is the canonical *transposed* output node.
> - Compare with the conventional Direct Form II, where $X(z)$ enters one summing node and the output is the rightmost tap ŌĆö the topology is mirror-imaged.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transposed Direct Form for an FIR Filter ($M = 3$)

For an FIR filter $H(z) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + b_3 z^{-3}$ (no feedback, $a_k = 0$):

```mermaid
graph LR
    inF["X z"]:::inputNode
    outF["Y z"]:::outputNode
    dF1["z^-1"]:::delay
    dF2["z^-1"]:::delay
    dF3["z^-1"]:::delay
    sF0["+"]:::summing
    sF1["+"]:::summing
    sF2["+"]:::summing
    sF3["+"]:::summing
    mF0["b0"]:::gain
    mF1["b1"]:::gain
    mF2["b2"]:::gain
    mF3["b3"]:::gain
    pF0(("P0")):::branch
    pF1(("P1")):::branch
    pF2(("P2")):::branch

    inF --> mF0 --> sF0
    inF --> mF1 --> sF1
    inF --> mF2 --> sF2
    inF --> mF3 --> sF3
    sF0 --> pF0 --> outF
    sF1 --> pF1 --> dF1 --> sF0
    sF2 --> pF2 --> dF2 --> sF1
    dF3 --> sF3

    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef outputNode fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px,color:#993300
    classDef summing fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef delay fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20
    classDef gain fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b58900,stroke-width:1px,color:#6b4f00
    classDef branch fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px,color:#660000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Architecture insight:** Notice that in the FIR transposed form, every coefficient $b_k$ multiplies the **same input node $X(z)$** and the output is the **leftmost** sum. This uniform structure is *ideal* for bit-serial and systolic VLSI implementations where each processing element sees the same input data broadcast.

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping: Direct Form II vs. Transposed Direct Form II

| Aspect | Direct Form II | Transposed Direct Form II |
|---|---|---|
| Input location | Left, into single sum | Top/right, broadcast to all gains |
| Output location | Rightmost tap | Leftmost tap |
| Delay chain direction | Left ŌåÆ Right | Right ŌåÆ Left (reversed) |
| Number of delays | $N$ | $N$ (canonical) |
| Multiplications/sample | $M + N + 1$ | $M + N + 1$ |
| Hardware pipelining ease | Moderate | High (regular structure) |
| Numerical sensitivity | Moderate | Often lower in fixed-point |
| Preferred in | Teaching, software simulation | VLSI / FPGA / ASIC design |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]** 
**State the Transposition Theorem used in realizing digital filters in the transposed form. List the four rules to be applied to a signal flow graph.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Transposition Theorem states that a linear signal flow graph implementing a transfer function $H(z)$ can be transformed into another flow graph implementing the *same* $H(z)$ by applying these four rules:

1. **Reverse** the direction of every branch arrow in the original SFG.
2. **Swap** the input node and the output node.
3. **Convert** every summing junction into a branching (pick-off) point, and every branching point into a summing junction.
4. **Preserve** all branch transmittances (multiplier gains) unchanged.

*Key point to mention for full marks:* the transfer function $H(z)$ of the transposed structure is **mathematically identical** to that of the original structure, as guaranteed by Tellegen's theorem for linear networks. **(3 marks ŌĆö full credit if all four rules are stated in correct order with the invariance property.)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]** 
**Compare Direct Form II and Transposed Direct Form II realizations of an IIR filter in terms of (i) number of delay elements, (ii) location of input/output nodes, and (iii) hardware pipelining suitability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Direct Form II | Transposed Direct Form II |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Number of delays | $N$ (canonical, $\max(M,N)$) | $N$ (canonical, $\max(M,N)$) ŌĆö **same** |
| (ii) Input / Output nodes | Input enters one summing node; output is the rightmost tap of the delay line | Input is broadcast to all multipliers; output is the leftmost pick-off point (mirror of Direct II) |
| (iii) Hardware pipelining | Moderate regularity; less suited to systolic arrays | Highly regular, single broadcast input ŌĆö **ideal for VLSI, FPGA, and systolic implementation** |

**Conclusion:** Both forms are canonical and produce the *same* $H(z)$; however, the transposed form's uniform broadcast-input structure makes it the preferred choice for fixed-point hardware. **(3 marks.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not change the signs of the feedback coefficients $a_k$** during transposition. The branch transmittances (including the *negative* sign in the $-a_k$ terms) must be **preserved exactly**.
> 2. **Do not forget to swap input and output nodes** ŌĆö this is the most common mistake costing 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Do not skip the verification step** ŌĆö explicitly writing $H_{\text{transposed}}(z) = H_{\text{original}}(z)$ earns the "complete answer" credit in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Direction of unit delays:** A $z^{-1}$ block in the transposed form points *opposite* to its orientation in the original form. Mixing up arrow direction loses 1ŌĆō2 marks in the SFG drawing.
> 5. **Initial-condition handling:** KTU expects students to *mention* zero-initial-condition assumption when deriving $H(z)$ from the LCCDE. Forgetting this is a recurring 1-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, PERUT524]**

**(a)** For the IIR filter described by the difference equation

$$
y(n) = 0.6\, y(n-1) - 0.08\, y(n-2) + x(n) + 0.5\, x(n-1)
$$

realize the system in **Transposed Direct Form II**. Clearly draw the signal flow graph and write the state-variable equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the transposed state equations, derive the transfer function $H(z)$ and verify that it matches the transfer function obtained directly from the given LCCDE. Compute the impulse response $h(n)$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ assuming zero initial conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients from the LCCDE.

$$
a_1 = -0.6, \quad a_2 = 0.08, \quad b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = 0.5, \quad b_2 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Write the transfer function directly:

$$
H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Recall the **Transposed Direct Form II** state equations (general $M=N=2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= b_0 x(n) - a_1 x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= b_1 x(n) - a_2 x(n) + w_2(n-1) \\
w_2(n) &= b_2 x(n) - 0 \cdot x(n) + 0 \\
y(n)   &= w_0(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numerical coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= 1 \cdot x(n) - (-0.6) x(n) + w_1(n-1) = 1.6\, x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= 0.5\, x(n) - 0.08\, x(n) + w_2(n-1) = 0.42\, x(n) + w_2(n-1) \\
w_2(n) &= 0 \\
y(n)   &= w_0(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Draw the SFG (textual block description for the answer sheet):

> [Mark the following in the answer script: input $X(z)$ enters from the top-right, multiplied by $b_0=1$, $b_1=0.5$, and feedback gains $0.6$, $0.08$ (note sign flip from $-a_k$). Three summing junctions form a vertical column, each followed by a unit delay $z^{-1}$ in the reversed direction. The output $Y(z)$ is tapped at the leftmost sum.]

**Marks distribution for part (a):** 2 marks ŌĆö coefficient identification & $H(z)$ form; 3 marks ŌĆö state equations correctly written with transposed signs; 2 marks ŌĆö SFG drawn with correct arrow directions and node labels.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Take Z-transform of the transposed state equations (zero IC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0(z) &= (b_0 - a_1) X(z) + z^{-1} W_1(z) = 1.6 X(z) + z^{-1} W_1(z) \\
W_1(z) &= (b_1 - a_2) X(z) + z^{-1} W_2(z) = 0.42 X(z) + 0 \quad \text{(since } W_2 = 0\text{)} \\
W_2(z) &= 0 \\
Y(z)   &= W_0(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** From $W_1(z) = 0.42\, X(z)$, substitute into $W_0(z)$:

$$
Y(z) = W_0(z) = 1.6\, X(z) + z^{-1} \cdot 0.42\, X(z) = (1.6 + 0.42 z^{-1}) X(z)
$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$
H_{\text{transposed}}(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = 1.6 + 0.42 z^{-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait ŌĆö verification step reveals a coefficient-entry error.** A careful re-check shows the *proper* transposed derivation must NOT pre-cancel the $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$ denominator. The correct route is to use the **complete** transposed equations, not the simplified ones we wrote when $b_2=0$. Redo:

**Correct Step 3 (re-derivation):** Use the general transposed equation:

$$
W_0(z) = (b_0 - a_1) X(z) + z^{-1} \big[ (b_1 - a_2) X(z) + z^{-1} (b_2 - 0) X(z) \big]
$$

Substitute $b_0=1,\ a_1=-0.6,\ b_1=0.5,\ a_2=0.08,\ b_2=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0(z) &= (1 - (-0.6)) X(z) + z^{-1} \big[ (0.5 - 0.08) X(z) + z^{-1}(0 - 0) X(z) \big] \\
       &= 1.6 X(z) + z^{-1} \big[ 0.42 X(z) + 0 \big] \\
       &= 1.6 X(z) + 0.42 z^{-1} X(z) = (1.6 + 0.42 z^{-1}) X(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm ŌĆö this **appears** to give an FIR answer, but the original system is IIR. The error is conceptual: the proper transposed Direct Form II uses a *single delay chain* with the recursion in the chain's internal feedback. The complete derivation is:

**Final correct derivation (using the standard transposed form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= x(n) + 0.6\, w_0(n-1) - 0.08\, w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= w_0(n-1) \\
y(n)   &= 1 \cdot w_0(n) + 0.5 \cdot w_1(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: the sign of $a_k$ flips because in the *transposed* form the feedback appears with the **opposite sign convention** from the direct form.)

Z-transform:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0(z) &= X(z) + 0.6 z^{-1} W_0(z) - 0.08 z^{-1} W_1(z) \\
W_1(z) &= z^{-1} W_0(z) \\
Y(z)   &= W_0(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} W_0(z) = (1 + 0.5 z^{-1}) W_0(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

From the first equation:

$$
W_0(z) \left[ 1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-1} \cdot z^{-1} \right] = X(z)
$$

Wait ŌĆö the *standard* transposed form, when $N=2$, has $W_1(z) = z^{-1} W_0(z)$ injected into $W_0$ with coefficient $-a_2 = -0.08$, not $z^{-1} \cdot W_1$:

$$
W_0(z) \left[ 1 - 0.6 z^{-1} \right] = X(z) - 0.08 z^{-1} \cdot z^{-1} W_0(z)
$$

Correcting:

$$
W_0(z) \left[ 1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2} \right] = X(z)
$$

$$
W_0(z) = \frac{X(z)}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
H_{\text{transposed}}(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}
$$

This **matches exactly** the $H(z)$ from the LCCDE. Ō£ō

**Step 4 ŌĆö Impulse response (set $x(n) = \delta(n)$, all ICs zero):**

Compute manually from the *original* LCCDE: $y(n) = 0.6\, y(n-1) - 0.08\, y(n-2) + \delta(n) + 0.5\, \delta(n-1)$.

- $n=0$: $y(0) = 0 - 0 + 1 + 0 = \mathbf{1}$
- $n=1$: $y(1) = 0.6(1) - 0 + 0 + 0.5 = \mathbf{1.1}$
- $n=2$: $y(2) = 0.6(1.1) - 0.08(1) + 0 + 0 = 0.66 - 0.08 = \mathbf{0.58}$
- $n=3$: $y(3) = 0.6(0.58) - 0.08(1.1) = 0.348 - 0.088 = \mathbf{0.26}$
- $n=4$: $y(4) = 0.6(0.26) - 0.08(0.58) = 0.156 - 0.0464 = \mathbf{0.1096}$

So $h(n) = \{1,\ 1.1,\ 0.58,\ 0.26,\ 0.1096,\ \ldots\}$.

**Marks distribution for part (b):** 3 marks ŌĆö derivation of $H(z)$ with correct algebra; 2 marks ŌĆö verification of equivalence; 2 marks ŌĆö impulse response table.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, PERUT524]**

**(a)** An FIR filter has the transfer function $H(z) = 1 + 2z^{-1} + 3z^{-2} + 2z^{-3} + z^{-4}$. Realize it in **Transposed Direct Form** by applying the Transposition Theorem to the conventional Direct Form realization. Write the state equations and the difference equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same filter, calculate the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ at $\omega = 0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi$ and determine whether the filter is a **low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, or band-stop** type. Justify with a sketch of $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Direct Form of the FIR filter uses a tapped delay line with coefficients $b_0=1, b_1=2, b_2=3, b_3=2, b_4=1$:

$$
y(n) = x(n) + 2x(n-1) + 3x(n-2) + 2x(n-3) + x(n-4)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the four transposition rules:
- Reverse all branch arrows.
- Swap $X(z)$ and $Y(z)$ nodes.
- Convert taps (branching points) into summing junctions, and the original output summer into a branching point.
- Keep all gains $b_k$ unchanged.

**Step 3:** Write the transposed state equations (with $w_k$ being the state at column $k$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_0(n) &= x(n) + w_1(n-1) \\
w_1(n) &= 2 x(n) + w_2(n-1) \\
w_2(n) &= 3 x(n) + w_3(n-1) \\
w_3(n) &= 2 x(n) + w_4(n-1) \\
w_4(n) &= 1 \cdot x(n) \\
y(n)   &= w_0(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Difference equation of the transposed form** (obtained by chaining the delays):

$$
y(n) = x(n) + 2x(n-1) + 3x(n-2) + 2x(n-3) + x(n-4)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice that the LCCDE is identical** to the direct form ŌĆö this confirms the transfer-function invariance property of the Transposition Theorem.

**Marks distribution for part (a):** 2 marks ŌĆö coefficient list & direct form; 3 marks ŌĆö transposed state equations; 2 marks ŌĆö final LCCDE & invariance remark.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 2 e^{-j\omega} + 3 e^{-j2\omega} + 2 e^{-j3\omega} + e^{-j4\omega}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute at specific frequencies.

**At $\omega = 0$:** $e^{-jk\cdot 0} = 1$ for all $k$.

$$
H(e^{j0}) = 1 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 9
$$

**At $\omega = \pi$:** $e^{-jk\pi} = (-1)^k$.

$$
H(e^{j\pi}) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 2 + 1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 1
$$

**At $\omega = \pi/2$:** $e^{-jk\pi/2} = (-j)^k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(e^{j\pi/2}) &= 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 2(j) + 1(1) \\
              &= 1 - 2j - 3 + 2j + 1 \\
              &= -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H(e^{j\pi/2}) \vert = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify filter type.

| $\omega$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | **9** (max) | 1 | 1 |

Since $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is **maximum at $\omega = 0$** (DC) and **small at higher frequencies**, the filter is a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Symmetry insight:** The coefficients $\{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ are *symmetric* ($b_k = b_{N-k}$), which guarantees **linear phase** ŌĆö a key property of FIR filters realized in transposed form with symmetric taps.

**Marks distribution for part (b):** 3 marks ŌĆö frequency-response calculation at the three points; 2 marks ŌĆö magnitude table; 2 marks ŌĆö filter-type identification with justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Transposed Form (PERUT524, Module 4)**

- Ō£ģ **Transposed Form** is an **alternative realization** of the *same* $H(z)$, obtained by applying the **Transposition Theorem** to a direct-form signal flow graph.
- Ō£ģ The four transposition rules are: **(1) reverse arrows, (2) swap I/O, (3) swap sum Ōåö branch, (4) preserve gains**.
- Ō£ģ **Transfer-function invariance:** $H_{\text{transposed}}(z) = H_{\text{original}}(z)$ ŌĆö always.
- Ō£ģ **Canonical (Transposed Direct Form II):** uses only $\max(M, N)$ unit-delay elements ŌĆö the *minimum possible* for a general IIR filter.
- Ō£ģ **Number of multiplications per sample:** $M + N + 1$ (same as Direct Form II).
- Ō£ģ **Sign convention:** In the transposed form, the **feedback gains appear with the opposite algebraic sign** compared to the direct form; this is the most error-prone step in KTU scripts.
- Ō£ģ **Hardware advantage:** Uniform *broadcast-input* structure makes the transposed form **ideal for VLSI, FPGA, and systolic-array implementations**.
- Ō£ģ **FIR special case:** All $a_k = 0$ ŌćÆ no feedback path; the transposed form is *symmetric* to the direct-form tapped delay line and exhibits **linear phase** when coefficients are symmetric/anti-symmetric.
- Ō£ģ **Verification protocol** for KTU exams: always (i) draw the SFG, (ii) write the state equations, (iii) derive $H(z)$ symbolically, (iv) show $H_{\text{transposed}}(z) = H_{\text{original}}(z)$, and optionally (v) compute $h(n)$ for an impulse input.
- Ō£ģ **Common KTU pitfalls to avoid:** (a) forgetting to swap input/output nodes, (b) flipping the sign of $a_k$ instead of preserving $-a_k$ as a transmittance, (c) drawing delays in the wrong direction, (d) omitting the zero-IC assumption in the $H(z)$ derivation.
- Ō£ģ **Module 4 linkage:** Transposed Form sits between **Direct Form realizations** and **Cascade/Parallel realizations** in the KTU syllabus ŌĆö mastery of Direct Form II is the prerequisite.
- Ō£ģ **Industrial relevance:** Audio codecs, SDR channel filters, biomedical wearables, and digital control compensators all use transposed direct-form II biquads as their workhorse building block.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade Form and Parallel Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT524 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cascade Form and Parallel Form # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the realization of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** discrete-time systems governed by a rational **Z-domain transfer function** $H(z)$, the **Cascade Form** and **Parallel Form** are two canonical second-order section (SOS) implementation structures used to break a high-order transfer function into lower-order building blocks. **General Transfer Function (N-th order IIR system):** $$ H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}} $$ ### Cascade Form Definition The transfer function is decomposed as a **product** of second-order (or first-order) sub-systems, also called *biquadratic sections* or *biquads*. $$ H(z) = G \cdot H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) \cdots H_K(z) $$ where each section $H_k(z) = \dfrac{b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2}}$ and $G$ is a gain constant. ### Parallel Form Definition The transfer function is decomposed as a **sum** of lower-order sections obtained through Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE). $$ H(z) = C + H_1(z) + H_2(z) + \cdots + H_K(z) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For an *N-th order* system, both cascade and parallel forms require only *N/2* second-order sections (if *N* is even), or *floor(N/2)* biquads plus one first-order section. This drastically reduces coefficient sensitivity and round-off noise compared to the Direct Form. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Cascade Form = Water Treatment Assembly Line** > Imagine a water purification plant. Raw water passes **sequentially** through Stage 1 (sand filter) ŌåÆ Stage 2 (carbon filter) ŌåÆ Stage 3 (UV sterilizer). The output of one stage is the input to the next. Each stage does one specific job well, and they are **chained in series**. > [!NOTE] > **Parallel Form = Supermarket Checkout Counters** > Imagine a grocery store. The same queue of customers is served **simultaneously** by multiple independent checkout counters (Express Lane, Standard Lane, Billing Counter). Each counter processes the *same input* (the customer), and the final total is the **sum** of the partial outputs. The system in this module is an **input signal** $x[n]$, and the **output** $y[n]$ is the result of either chained processing (cascade) or summed parallel processing (parallel). ### Key Realization Metrics (Important for KTU Board Exam) - **Total Delay Elements Required (Direct Form II):** $N$ (order of the system). - **Total Multipliers Required:** Proportional to the number of non-zero coefficients. - **Numerical Robustness:** Cascade > Direct Form (especially for high-order IIR filters). - **Standard Polling Frequency for Real-Time Audio:** **48 kHz**, **44.1 kHz**, or **96 kHz**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ### 2.1.1 Cascade Form Realization ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 ŌĆö Factor the Denominator:** Identify the poles by factoring the denominator polynomial $D(z)$ into first and second-order terms. **Step 2 ŌĆö Factor the Numerator (Pole-Zero Pairing):** Group the zeros with the poles. *Rule of Thumb:* Pair the pole *closest to the unit circle* with the *nearest zero* to minimize peak noise gain. **Step 3 ŌĆö Allocate a Gain Constant:** If the product of all $b_{0k}$ coefficients does not match the leading numerator coefficient, the difference is absorbed into a gain $G$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Implement Each Biquad Using Direct Form II:** Use the difference equation for a generic biquad to compute the output of each stage. ### 2.1.2 Parallel Form Realization ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 ŌĆö Perform Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE):** Decompose $H(z)$ into a sum of first-order and second-order fractions. **Step 2 ŌĆö Handle Improper Fractions:** If $\deg(N(z)) \geq \deg(D(z))$, perform polynomial long division to extract a constant $C$ (a pure gain path). **Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Independent Paths:** Each PFE term becomes a *parallel branch* with its own filter coefficients. **Step 4 ŌĆö Sum the Branch Outputs:** A final summation node adds all branch outputs to produce $y[n]$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why are these forms preferred over Direct Form I/II for high-order IIR systems?** > 1. **Modularity:** Each biquad can be designed, tested, and tuned independently. > 2. **Coefficient Sensitivity:** Lower-order polynomials are less sensitive to finite word-length quantization. > 3. **Stability Guarantee:** If every biquad is stable, the overall system is stable. > 4. **Overflow Control:** Internal states of each biquad can be scaled independently. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | S.No. | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Use Case / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | General IIR Transfer Function | $H(z) = \dfrac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}}$ | Foundation of all realization forms | | 2 | Cascade Decomposition | $H(z) = G \prod_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2}}$ | Poles and zeros paired by proximity | | 3 | Parallel Decomposition (PFE) | $H(z) = C + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{r_k}{1 - p_k z^{-1}}$ | For distinct real poles | | 4 | Complex-Conjugate Pole Pair | $H_k(z) = \dfrac{r_{0k} + r_{1k}z^{-1}}{1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2}}$ | For complex pole pairs (resonator) | | 5 | First-Order DF-II Difference Eqn. | $w[n] = x[n] - a_1 w[n-1]$
$y[n] = b_0 w[n] + b_1 w[n-1]$ | Minimum delay realization | | 6 | Biquad DF-II Difference Eqn. | $w[n] = x[n] - a_1 w[n-1] - a_2 w[n-2]$
$y[n] = b_0 w[n] + b_1 w[n-1] + b_2 w[n-2]$ | Standard biquadratic section | | 7 | Cascade Output | $y[n] = H_2 \ast (H_1 \ast x[n])$ | Convolution in series | | 8 | Parallel Output | $y[n] = \sum_{k} y_k[n]$ | Linear summation of branches | --- ## 2.3 Engineering Applications - **Digital Audio Equalizers:** Cascade biquads for low-shelf, peak, and high-shelf filters. - **Echo & Reverb Effects:** Parallel form is used to combine multiple delayed copies of a signal. - **Communication Channelizers:** Cascade/parallel banks of bandpass filters in software-defined radio (SDR). - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG denoising using parallel notch filters at 50/60 Hz. - **Control Systems:** Implementation of high-order compensators on microcontrollers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Master Worked Example **Given the LTI System Transfer Function:** $$ H(z) = \frac{2 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.75 z^{-1} + 0.125 z^{-2}} $$ **Objective:** Realize the system in (A) Cascade Form and (B) Parallel Form using Direct Form II sections. --- ### 3.1.1 Step A ŌĆö Factor the Denominator (Pole Identification) We must find the roots of $D(z) = z^2 - 0.75z + 0.125 = 0$. Using the quadratic formula: $$ \begin{aligned} z &= \frac{0.75 \pm \sqrt{(0.75)^2 - 4(1)(0.125)}}{2} \\ &= \frac{0.75 \pm \sqrt{0.5625 - 0.5}}{2} \\ &= \frac{0.75 \pm \sqrt{0.0625}}{2} \\ &= \frac{0.75 \pm 0.25}{2} \end{aligned} $$ This yields the two real poles $p_1 = 0.5$ and $p_2 = 0.25$. Therefore, the denominator factors as: $$ 1 - 0.75z^{-1} + 0.125z^{-2} = (1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.25z^{-1}) $$ **Why is this step important?** Factoring the denominator reveals the natural modes (poles) of the system. Both poles lie *inside the unit circle* ($|p| < 1$), confirming that the system is **BIBO stable**. --- ### 3.1.2 Step B ŌĆö Cascade Form Realization **Sub-Step B.1:** Rewrite $H(z)$ in factored form. $$ H(z) = \frac{2 + z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.25z^{-1})} $$ **Sub-Step B.2:** Distribute the numerator across the two sections. We choose to multiply and divide by an appropriate factor to obtain two valid first-order biquads. We observe that $(2 + z^{-1}) = 2(1 + 0.5z^{-1})$. So we rewrite: $$ H(z) = 2 \cdot \frac{(1 + 0.5z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.25z^{-1})} $$ We then split this as a product of two sections: $$ H(z) = \underbrace{\left( \frac{1 + 0.5z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} \right)}_{H_1(z)} \cdot \underbrace{\left( \frac{2}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}} \right)}_{H_2(z)} $$ **Why split like this?** We group the *zero* at $z = -0.5$ with the *pole* at $p_1 = 0.5$ (which is closer to the unit circle, hence more sensitive to quantization). This follows the **pole-zero pairing rule** that minimizes peak noise gain. **Sub-Step B.3:** Implement each section using Direct Form II. For $H_1(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.5z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}$ (with intermediate signal $s[n]$): $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5 \cdot w_1[n-1] \\ s[n] &= 1 \cdot w_1[n] + 0.5 \cdot w_1[n-1] \end{aligned} $$ For $H_2(z) = \dfrac{2}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}$ (with output $y[n]$): $$ \begin{aligned} w_2[n] &= s[n] + 0.25 \cdot w_2[n-1] \\ y[n] &= 2 \cdot w_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ **Cascade Form ŌĆö Complete Difference Equation Set:** $$ \boxed{\begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5 \, w_1[n-1] \\ s[n] &= w_1[n] + 0.5 \, w_1[n-1] \\ w_2[n] &= s[n] + 0.25 \, w_2[n-1] \\ y[n] &= 2 \, w_2[n] \end{aligned}} $$ --- ### 3.1.3 Step C ŌĆö Parallel Form Realization (via Partial Fraction Expansion) **Sub-Step C.1:** Set up the PFE template. Since we have two distinct real poles, $H(z)$ is expanded as: $$ H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}} $$ **Sub-Step C.2:** Combine the RHS over a common denominator: $$ H(z) = \frac{A(1 - 0.25z^{-1}) + B(1 - 0.5z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.25z^{-1})} $$ Equating numerators with the original $2 + z^{-1}$: $$ A(1 - 0.25z^{-1}) + B(1 - 0.5z^{-1}) = 2 + z^{-1} $$ **Sub-Step C.3:** Expand and match coefficients of $z^0$ and $z^{-1}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Constant term:} \quad A + B &= 2 \\ z^{-1} \text{ term:} \quad -0.25A - 0.5B &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-Step C.4:** Solve the system. From the second equation: $-0.25A - 0.5B = 1 \implies A + 2B = -4$. Subtracting the first equation from this: $$ \begin{aligned} (A + 2B) - (A + B) &= -4 - 2 \\ B &= -6 \end{aligned} $$ Substituting back: $A = 2 - B = 2 - (-6) = 8$. **Verification:** $A = 8, B = -6$. Quick check: $8(1 - 0.25z^{-1}) - 6(1 - 0.5z^{-1}) = 8 - 2z^{-1} - 6 + 3z^{-1} = 2 + z^{-1}$ Ō£ō **Final PFE Result:** $$ \boxed{H(z) = \frac{8}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} - \frac{6}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}} $$ **Sub-Step C.5:** Implement each branch using Direct Form II. **Branch 1:** $H_1(z) = \dfrac{8}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5 \, w_1[n-1] \\ y_1[n] &= 8 \, w_1[n] \end{aligned} $$ **Branch 2:** $H_2(z) = \dfrac{-6}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} w_2[n] &= x[n] + 0.25 \, w_2[n-1] \\ y_2[n] &= -6 \, w_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ **Final Output Summer:** $$ \boxed{y[n] = y_1[n] + y_2[n] = 8 \, w_1[n] - 6 \, w_2[n]} $$ **Parallel Form ŌĆö Complete Difference Equation Set:** $$ \boxed{\begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5 \, w_1[n-1], \quad y_1[n] = 8 \, w_1[n] \\ w_2[n] &= x[n] + 0.25 \, w_2[n-1], \quad y_2[n] = -6 \, w_2[n] \\ y[n] &= y_1[n] + y_2[n] \end{aligned}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Cascade Form | Parallel Form | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mathematical Operator | **Product** of sections | **Sum** of sections | | Decomposition Method | Pole-Zero Factoring | Partial Fraction Expansion | | Computational Graph | Series (chained) | Diverging branches with a summer | | Sensitivity to Quantization | Low (good for high-Q filters) | Moderate | | Ease of Coefficient Update | Easy (per-section tuning) | Difficult (PFE recompute) | | Hardware Pipeline Friendliness | High (sequential) | Low (requires simultaneous paths) | | Memory Requirements | $N$ delay elements | $N$ delay elements | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Direct Form II First-Order Section (Building Block) This is the atomic unit used inside both cascade and parallel realizations. Coefficients are $b_0$, $b_1$, and $a_1$. ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input Xz] --> SUM1 A1COEF["Multiply by a1"] --> SUM1 SUM1 --> DELAY["Delay z^-1"] DELAY --> WNODE[Wn] DELAY --> WNODEDELAY["Wn-1 node"] WNODE --> B0COEF["Multiply by b0"] WNODEDELAY --> B1COEF["Multiply by b1"] B0COEF --> SUM2 B1COEF --> SUM2 WNODE --> SUM2 SUM2 --> OUT[Output Yz] WNODEDELAY --> A1COEF WNODEDELAY --> B1COEF ``` ## 4.2 Cascade Form ŌĆö Two Sections in Series ```mermaid flowchart LR XIN[Input xn] --> SEC1["H1 z = 1 + 0.5z^-1 over 1 - 0.5z^-1"] SEC1 --> SMID[Intermediate sn] SMID --> SEC2["H2 z = 2 over 1 - 0.25z^-1"] SEC2 --> YOUT[Output yn] SEC1 --- NOTE1["Section 1 DF II: w1 n = xn + 0.5 w1 n-1, sn = w1 n + 0.5 w1 n-1"] SEC2 --- NOTE2["Section 2 DF II: w2 n = sn + 0.25 w2 n-1, yn = 2 w2 n"] ``` ## 4.3 Parallel Form ŌĆö Two Branches with Summation ```mermaid flowchart LR XIN[Input xn] --> BR1["H1 z = 8 over 1 - 0.5z^-1"] XIN --> BR2["H2 z = -6 over 1 - 0.25z^-1"] BR1 --> Y1[y1n] BR2 --> Y2[y2n] Y1 --> SUMMER["Adder"] Y2 --> SUMMER SUMMER --> YOUT[Output yn] BR1 --- N1["Branch 1: w1 n = xn + 0.5 w1 n-1, y1 n = 8 w1 n"] BR2 --- N2["Branch 2: w2 n = xn + 0.25 w2 n-1, y2 n = -6 w2 n"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CAS[Cascade Architecture] C_X[Input] --> C_S1[Section 1 Biquad] C_S1 --> C_S2[Section 2 Biquad] C_S2 --> C_Y[Output] end subgraph PAR[Parallel Architecture] P_X[Input] --> P_S1[Section 1 Biquad] P_X --> P_S2[Section 2 Biquad] P_S1 --> P_SUM[Adder] P_S2 --> P_SUM P_SUM --> P_Y[Output] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2] **Differentiate between cascade and parallel realization of an IIR system. Mention one advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Cascade Form | Parallel Form | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Structure | Sections connected in series (output of one is input of next) | Sections connected in parallel with a common summer | | Decomposition | Pole-zero pairing via polynomial factoring | Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) | | Implementation | $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) \cdot \ldots$ | $H(z) = H_1(z) + H_2(z) + \ldots$ | **Advantage of Cascade:** Lower coefficient sensitivity and better numerical stability for high-order filters. **(1 Mark)** **Advantage of Parallel:** Simpler coefficient update and easier hardware parallelism. **(1 Mark)** *(Plus 1 mark for correct table-based comparison.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Supplementary] **Why is pole-zero pairing important in cascade realization? State the standard pairing rule.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Pole-zero pairing determines how the poles and zeros of $H(z)$ are grouped into second-order sections. Poor pairing leads to large internal signal swings, overflow, and high noise gain. **(2 Marks)** **Standard Rule:** Pair the pole *closest to the unit circle* with the *zero nearest to it*, and place the section with the highest $Q$-factor first in the cascade chain. **(1 Mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question 1 (Choice A) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Expected Pattern] **Q1 (a)** Realize the following transfer function in **Cascade Form** using Direct Form II first-order sections: $$ H(z) = \frac{2 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.75z^{-1} + 0.125z^{-2}} $$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Factor the denominator:** The poles are at $p_1 = 0.5$ and $p_2 = 0.25$ (computed via the quadratic formula as shown in Section 3.1.1). **(1 Mark)** **Step 2 ŌĆö Factor and rearrange the numerator:** $(2 + z^{-1}) = 2(1 + 0.5z^{-1})$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 3 ŌĆö Pair poles and zeros by proximity:** Pair the zero at $z = -0.5$ with the pole $p_1 = 0.5$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 4 ŌĆö Write the cascade product:** $H(z) = \left(\dfrac{1 + 0.5z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}\right)$. **(2 Marks)** **Step 5 ŌĆö Write the difference equations for each section (DF-II):** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5\, w_1[n-1], \quad s[n] = w_1[n] + 0.5\, w_1[n-1] \\ w_2[n] &= s[n] + 0.25\, w_2[n-1], \quad y[n] = 2\, w_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ --- **Q1 (b)** Realize the **same** $H(z)$ in **Parallel Form** using Direct Form II sections. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Set up PFE:** $H(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for residues:** Matching coefficients of $z^0$ and $z^{-1}$ in the equation $A + B = 2$ and $-0.25A - 0.5B = 1$ yields $A = 8$, $B = -6$. **(2 Marks)** **Step 3 ŌĆö Write the parallel expansion:** $H(z) = \dfrac{8}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} - \dfrac{6}{1 - 0.25z^{-1}}$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 4 ŌĆö Write DF-II difference equations for both branches:** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.5\, w_1[n-1], \quad y_1[n] = 8\, w_1[n] \\ w_2[n] &= x[n] + 0.25\, w_2[n-1], \quad y_2[n] = -6\, w_2[n] \\ y[n] &= y_1[n] + y_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Draw the parallel block diagram with two parallel branches converging at a summer.** **(1 Mark)** --- ### Question 1 (Choice B) ŌĆö Alternative Internal Choice **Q2 (a)** Explain **Cascade Realization** of an IIR system. For $$ H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.3z^{-2}}{(1 - 0.6z^{-1})(1 - 0.4z^{-1})} $$ obtain the cascade realization in Direct Form II. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** **Step 1:** The denominator is already factored with poles at $p_1 = 0.6$ and $p_2 = 0.4$. The numerator can be split as $(1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.3z^{-2})$ ŌĆö keep it as a second-order polynomial since direct factoring may not yield real roots. **(2 Marks)** **Step 2:** Assign the entire numerator to the *first* section, keeping the second section as a pure pole: $$ H(z) = \underbrace{\left(\frac{1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.3z^{-2}}{1 - 0.6z^{-1}}\right)}_{H_1(z)} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.4z^{-1}}\right)}_{H_2(z)} $$ **(2 Marks)** **Step 3:** Write DF-II difference equations. **(3 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.6\, w_1[n-1] \\ s[n] &= 1 \cdot w_1[n] - 0.5\, w_1[n-1] + 0.3\, w_1[n-2] \\ w_2[n] &= s[n] + 0.4\, w_2[n-1] \\ y[n] &= w_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ --- **Q2 (b)** Realize the transfer function $$ H(z) = \frac{2(1 - 0.4z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6z^{-1})(1 - 0.3z^{-1})} $$ in **Parallel Form** using Direct Form II. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** **Step 1:** PFE: $H(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.6z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.3z^{-1}}$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 2:** Numerator matching: $2(1 - 0.4z^{-1}) = A(1 - 0.3z^{-1}) + B(1 - 0.6z^{-1})$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 3:** Equating coefficients: $A + B = 2$ and $-0.3A - 0.6B = -0.8$. Solving gives $A = 0.8$ and $B = 1.2$. **(3 Marks)** **Step 4:** Write DF-II equations for both branches and combine. **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1[n] &= x[n] + 0.6\, w_1[n-1], \quad y_1[n] = 0.8\, w_1[n] \\ w_2[n] &= x[n] + 0.3\, w_2[n-1], \quad y_2[n] = 1.2\, w_2[n] \\ y[n] &= y_1[n] + y_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Sign Error in Feedback Path (2 marks lost):** The DF-II feedback coefficient is $+a_1$ inside the *sum* (since the original denominator is $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}$). Forgetting this sign causes a wrong system. Always derive from the difference equation. > 2. **PFE Residue Computation (2 marks lost):** Students often forget to expand the numerator *fully* before matching coefficients. The constant term and $z^{-1}$ term must both be matched. > 3. **Skipping the Gain Constant:** When factoring the numerator in cascade form, leftover factors must be absorbed into a gain $G$. Examiners explicitly test for this. > 4. **Drawing the Block Diagram:** A *full-mark* answer in KTU 2024 ESE **must** include a labeled block diagram showing the multipliers ($b_0, b_1, a_1$) and the delay element. Text-only answers are penalized by 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 5. **Pole-Zero Pairing Justification:** When asked for cascade, do *not* present it as just a product ŌĆö briefly mention *why* the chosen pairing is optimal. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 4: Cascade and Parallel Forms** - **Cascade Form** is a **product** of lower-order sections; **Parallel Form** is a **sum** of lower-order sections. - Cascade is obtained by **factoring the numerator and denominator** polynomials; Parallel is obtained by **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**. - **Direct Form II** is the canonical building block for both realizations ŌĆö it uses only $N$ delays for an $N$-th order system. - **Pole-zero pairing rule:** Pair the pole closest to the unit circle with the *nearest* zero; place the highest-$Q$ section first in cascade. - The **gain constant $G$** in cascade form must equal the leading numerator coefficient divided by the product of $b_{0k}$ terms. - For **improper** transfer functions ($\deg N(z) \geq \deg D(z)$), polynomial long division yields a constant $C$ that appears as a *direct feed-forward* path in parallel form. - **BIBO Stability:** Check that all poles satisfy $|p_k| < 1$ (inside the unit circle). - The output of a cascade system is the **sequential convolution** $y[n] = (H_K \ast (\ldots \ast (H_1 \ast x[n])))$. - The output of a parallel system is the **linear sum** $y[n] = \sum_k y_k[n]$. - Cascade form is preferred for **narrow-band, high-$Q$ filters** (e.g., notch and resonator filters). - Parallel form is preferred for **wideband filters** composed of simple first-order stages (e.g., shelving equalizers). - For an $N$-th order IIR system, the number of delay elements is **always $N$** regardless of realization form. - When asked to "realize" in KTU exams, **always provide** (i) factored form, (ii) difference equations, and (iii) block diagram to score full marks.
